options: The sequence moves from immediate/earthly to distant/celestial — it maps the world as it is experienced from the ground up
The Creator comes first in all Haudenosaunee traditions
The sequence is borrowed from European religion
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Address moves outward from the community — people first, then the ground beneath them, then the waters, then plants and animals, then up through the sky to the stars. It is a phenomenological map of the world as it is lived, from the earth up.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: An animated version of the Thanksgiving Address was produced by Kontinónhstats in Kanehsatà:ke. Why does a language revitalization organization invest in animated media?
exercises.correct: Media in the language creates new domains of use — children and families can access the language through familiar formats
options: Media in the language creates new domains of use — children and families can access the language through familiar formats
The words are too difficult to teach directly
Animated media is required by law
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! This is García & Li Wei's (2014) point about expanding language domains. When Kanien'kéha appears in animation, YouTube, podcasts, and apps, it gains new contexts of use — which is essential for revitalization.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: 'The words that come before all else' — the idea that before any meeting, any learning, any gathering, we first acknowledge the world we are part of. What would it mean in your life to begin that way? What would you acknowledge, in your own words, before you began something that mattered?

title: Unit 6 — Animals, Shapes & Clans
desc: Shapes, wild animals, domestic animals, and the Mohawk clan system

lessons.title: Lesson 19 — Shapes
lessons.sub: The geometry of Kanien'kéha
intro: Kanien'kéha has names for shapes — and many of them are descriptive compounds using vocabulary you already know. Triangle is literally Áhsen nikahióhsake — 'three sides'. Diamond is Kaié:ri nikahióhsake — 'four sides'.
This demonstrates one of Kanien'kéha's most productive features: new vocabulary is built from existing roots. You do not need to memorise a new arbitrary word — you combine pieces you already know.
The lesson also reviews the number words Áhsen (three) and Kaié:ri (four) from the Numbers lesson, showing spaced repetition in action.
lessons.ped: Productive morphology: Mary McDonald explicitly highlights that shape vocabulary shows 'Mohawk is a productive language'. Productiveness — the ability to generate new words from known parts — is a key feature of language vitality (Mithun, 1999).

exercises.prompt: Áhsen nikahióhsake means 'triangle'. You know Áhsen from the Numbers lesson. What does Áhsen mean?
exercises.correct: Three
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Áhsen = three. Triangle = three sides. Shape names in Kanien'kéha describe the shape using number roots you already know.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Kaié:ri nikahióhsake means 'diamond'. What does Kaié:ri mean?
exercises.correct: Four
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Kaié:ri = four. Diamond = four sides (in a rhombus). Knowing the numbers unlocks the shape names.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the shape names
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Four shapes matched.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Teionteriwaién:te' (circle) uses the te- prefix you know from dual pronouns and dualic verbs. What does te- signal in circle?
exercises.correct: The te- prefix marks continuous/dual movement — a circle has no beginning or end
options: The te- prefix marks continuous/dual movement — a circle has no beginning or end
te- means the shape is large
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The te- prefix signals dualic or continuous action. A circle is a shape that continuously returns to its starting point — the prefix captures that quality.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'square'
exercises.hint: The word ends at te' — no suffix needed
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Teiotokerón:te' — square.
exercises.wm: The word is complete as written — Teiotokerón:te'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Star'?
exercises.correct: Otsísto
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Otsísto — star. The stars are the last beings acknowledged before the Creator in the Thanksgiving Address.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Otsísto

exercises.prompt: You have now seen Karáhkwa (sun) in three different lessons. Where else did it appear?
exercises.correct: In the Kanehsatà:ke place names lesson and as a shape in this lesson
options: In the Kanehsatà:ke place names lesson and as a shape in this lesson
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Karáhkwa has appeared as a place-name vocabulary word (Lesson 14), as a shape (this lesson), and in the Thanksgiving Address context. This is how vocabulary deepens — one word, many contexts.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Triangle and diamond are both named using number words + nikahióhsake (sides). What does this word-formation strategy tell you about Kanien'kéha?
exercises.correct: The language generates new words from existing parts — knowing roots unlocks vocabulary you have never explicitly learned
options: The language generates new words from existing parts — knowing roots unlocks vocabulary you have never explicitly learned
These are borrowed words from French
Shapes are too complex to name originally
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is what Mary McDonald means by 'productive language'. Knowing Áhsen, Kaié:ri, and nikahióhsake gives you triangle and diamond without memorising arbitrary new words.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Four syllables: o-tsís-to. Stress on the second syllable (tsís). Short and clear.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Teionteriwaién:te' — circle. The circle is a recurring symbol in Haudenosaunee tradition: the circle of the Confederacy, the circular design of the longhouse, the cycle of ceremonies through the year. What circles exist in your life — relationships, routines, returns? What keeps coming back around?

lessons.title: Lesson 20 — Wild Animals
lessons.sub: The animals that share our territory
intro: Wild animals are central to Kanien'kehá:ka life — in traditional diet, in the clan system, in ceremony, and in oral tradition. Bear (Ohkwá:ri), Turtle (A'nó:wara), and Wolf (Okwáho) are the three founding clans of the Kanien'kehá:ka.

Recognising the clan animals in Kanien'kéha is politically and socially important: when you know which clan someone belongs to, you understand their kinship obligations, their ceremonial roles, and their relational position in the community.
This lesson draws from Lesson 22 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum. The bear Ohkwá:ri is also the main character in the Tóta tánon Ohkwá:ri puppet series produced in Kahnawà:ke.
lessons.ped: Animals and clan identity: Presenting wild animals in the context of the clan system follows Freire's (1970) principle of generative themes — the animals are not vocabulary items but social identifiers. Learning Ohkwá:ri (bear) also means learning who the Bear Clan people are.

exercises.prompt: What does Ohkwá:ri mean?
exercises.correct: Bear
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri — bear. The Bear Clan (Rotihskeré:wake) takes its name from this animal. Ohkwá:ri also appears in the Tóta tánon Ohkwá:ri puppet series from Kahnawà:ke.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: A'nó:wara means turtle. In Haudenosaunee creation story, what role does the turtle play?
exercises.correct: North America (Turtle Island) is formed on the back of a great turtle
options: North America (Turtle Island) is formed on the back of a great turtle
The turtle carries the sun across the sky
The turtle is the fastest animal
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Turtle Island — the Haudenosaunee name for North America — rests on the back of a great turtle. A'nó:wara is not just a vocabulary word but a cosmological reference.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the three clan animals to their Kanien'kéha names
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The three founding clans of the Kanien'kehá:ka: Bear, Turtle, Wolf.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the other wild animals
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Beaver, rabbit, deer, moose — animals of the Kanehsatà:ke territory.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Tsaní:to (beaver) was historically important to Kanien'kehá:ka political life. Why?
exercises.correct: The fur trade in beaver pelts dramatically affected Kanien'kehá:ka alliances and territory in the 17th–18th centuries
options: The fur trade in beaver pelts dramatically affected Kanien'kehá:ka alliances and territory in the 17th–18th centuries
Beavers were used in ceremony only
Beaver is only eaten in winter
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The beaver pelt trade was a primary driver of the Beaver Wars of the 17th century, in which Haudenosaunee nations competed for access to trade networks. Language, animals, and history are inseparable here.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Bear'?
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ohkwá:ri. Three syllables: oh-KWÁ-ri.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Ohkwá:ri

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'wolf'
exercises.correct: ho
exercises.hint: h
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Okwáho — wolf. The Wolf Clan (Ronathahí:non) are associated with pathfinding.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ho'.

exercises.prompt: Ahwénsion means porcupine. Before European contact, what were porcupine quills used for?
exercises.correct: Traditional quillwork — an art form predating European glass beads
options: Traditional quillwork — an art form predating European glass beads
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Porcupine quillwork is one of the oldest Haudenosaunee art traditions. When Europeans arrived with glass beads, quillwork gradually shifted to beadwork — though both traditions continue.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Three syllables: oh-KWÁ-ri. The stress falls on the second syllable. The long vowel (:) after kwá is slightly extended.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohkwá:ri — bear. One of the three founding clan animals. In Haudenosaunee tradition, knowing someone's clan means knowing your relationship to them — you cannot marry within your clan, and you have specific ceremonial obligations to your clan members. If you were to belong to a clan named after an animal, what animal would it be, and why? What qualities of that animal do you carry?

lessons.title: Lesson 21 — Domestic Animals
lessons.sub: Animals that arrived with contact
intro: When European settlers arrived in North America, they brought animals the Kanien'kehá:ka had never seen: horses, cows, sheep, pigs. Rather than borrowing European names, Kanien'kéha created new words using its own word-formation system.

Akohsá:tens (horse) is related to 'it carries on its back'. Teiotina'karontón:'a (sheep) is 'it has wool on its back'. These descriptive names demonstrate that the language did not need to borrow — it described.
This is exactly what Mary McDonald means when she says Kanien'kéha is a 'productive and creative language' in her Lesson 23 introduction.
lessons.ped: Language contact and creativity: García & Li Wei (2014) note that all living languages encounter new words and adapt. Kanien'kéha's strategy of descriptive naming (rather than borrowing) shows a language with strong internal generative capacity — a mark of vitality.

exercises.prompt: Akohsá:tens (horse) is related to 'it carries on its back'. What does this tell you about how Kanien'kéha names new animals?
exercises.correct: New animals are named by describing their defining function or appearance — not by borrowing foreign words
options: New animals are named by describing their defining function or appearance — not by borrowing foreign words
The word was borrowed from French
All animal names come from clan animals
Horses had Kanien'kéha names before contact
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Descriptive naming: horse = 'the thing that carries on its back', sheep = 'the thing with wool on its back'. The language generates new vocabulary from within its own system.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Takós mean?
exercises.correct: Cat
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Takós — cat.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the domestic animals
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Four domestic animals — all named using Kanien'kéha's own word-formation rules.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Teiotina'karontón:'a (sheep) means 'it has wool on its back'. What other animal name uses the same descriptive strategy?
exercises.sub: Think about what Akohsá:tens means
exercises.correct: Akohsá:tens (horse = it carries on its back)
options: Akohsá:tens (horse = it carries on its back)
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Both sheep and horse are named by describing a physical characteristic or function. Descriptive compound naming is the consistent strategy for post-contact animals.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Cat'?
exercises.correct: Takós
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Takós — one of the shorter domestic animal names.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Takós

exercises.prompt: Tiohnhónhskwaron (cow) contains the root 'hónhskwar'. The bull is Tiohnhónhskwaron rá:tsi'n. What does rá:tsi'n likely indicate?
exercises.correct: It marks the male form — rá:tsi'n is a masculine suffix/modifier
options: It marks the male form — rá:tsi'n is a masculine suffix/modifier
It is a separate unrelated word
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! rá:tsi'n marks the male form of the animal. This parallels the Ra-/I- prefix system you know from person nouns — Kanien'kéha consistently marks gender.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Kanien'kéha responded to European contact by creating new words from within its own grammatical system rather than borrowing. How does this compare to what English did with new concepts?
exercises.correct: English typically borrows words from other languages (e.g. 'potato' from Taíno, 'chocolate' from Nahuatl); Kanien'kéha describes new things using existing roots
options: English typically borrows words from other languages (e.g. 'potato' from Taíno, 'chocolate' from Nahuatl); Kanien'kéha describes new things using existing roots
Both languages do exactly the same thing
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Different languages have different strategies for new vocabulary. Kanien'kéha's descriptive compounding strategy reflects a different philosophy of naming — you describe what something is, rather than adopting a foreign sound.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: a-koh-SÁ-tens. Stress on the third syllable. The colon (:) after sá extends the vowel slightly.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Akohsá:tens — horse — named for what it does: it carries. When Europeans arrived with horses, the Kanien'kehá:ka did not call them by a European name. They described what the animal does. Think of something new in your own life that you have had to find a name for. How did you name it — by borrowing someone else's word, or by describing what it is?

lessons.title: Lesson 22 — The Clan System
lessons.sub: Who we are to each other
intro: The Kanien'kehá:ka have three original clans: Bear (Rotihskeré:wake), Turtle (Rotiniáhton), and Wolf (Ronathahí:non). Clan membership is matrilineal — you belong to your mother's clan forever.

At Kanehsatà:ke specifically, two additional clans are present: Snipe (Roti'nehsí:io) and Eel (Rotineniotthró:non), both originally Onondaga clans. Their presence reflects the 1806 resettlement of Oswegatchie Onondaga people at Akwesasne, followed by intermarriage and migration.
The clan system governs who you can marry (never within your own clan), who attends your ceremonies, and who speaks at your funeral. The language and the social structure are inseparable.
lessons.ped: Clans as social grammar: McCarty & Lee (2014) argue that language learning must honour cultural institutions. The clan system is not background knowledge — it is the social structure that the language serves. Teaching clan names without this context produces vocabulary without meaning.

exercises.prompt: What does Rotihskeré:wake mean?
exercises.correct: Bear Clan
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Rotihskeré:wake — Bear Clan. The Bear Clan is associated with healing in oral tradition.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match each clan name to its animal
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The three founding Mohawk clans. You can verify your own clan by asking your mother's family.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Roti'nehsí:io (Snipe Clan) is present at Kanehsatà:ke. Why?
exercises.correct: Oswegatchie Onondaga people resettled at Akwesasne in 1806 after being driven out by US troops; intermarriage brought these clans to Kanehsatà:ke
options: Oswegatchie Onondaga people resettled at Akwesasne in 1806 after being driven out by US troops; intermarriage brought these clans to Kanehsatà:ke
Snipe Clan was always a Mohawk clan
The clan system is the same at all communities
Snipe Clan members moved to avoid the Bear Clan
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The Kontinónhstats curriculum explicitly explains this history. The presence of Snipe and Eel Clans at Kanehsatà:ke is not an irregularity — it is evidence of the community's specific history of displacement, resettlement, and kinship.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Clan membership is matrilineal. You belong to your mother's clan. What does this mean for the Kanien'kehá:ka kinship system you studied earlier?
exercises.correct: Your clan connects directly to the matrilineal kinship nouns — ista (mother), tota (grandmother) — because clan passes through the female line
options: Your clan connects directly to the matrilineal kinship nouns — ista (mother), tota (grandmother) — because clan passes through the female line
Clan membership is separate from kinship vocabulary
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The entire vocabulary you learned in Unit 2 (kinship nouns, matrilineal family structure) connects directly to the clan system. The language is a map of the social structure.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Turtle Clan'?
exercises.correct: Rotiniáhton
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Rotiniáhton — Turtle Clan. The Turtle Clan carries leadership responsibilities in Haudenosaunee governance.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Rotiniáhton

exercises.prompt: Complete the Wolf Clan name
exercises.correct: non
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ronathahí:non — Wolf Clan. The Wolf Clan are the pathfinders, those who know the way.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'non'.

exercises.prompt: 'You cannot marry within your own clan' is a fundamental rule of Haudenosaunee society. How is this reflected in the kinship vocabulary you learned?
exercises.correct: Kinship terms mark every relationship — knowing your clan tells you which people are prohibited as marriage partners and which ceremonial roles you hold
options: Kinship terms mark every relationship — knowing your clan tells you which people are prohibited as marriage partners and which ceremonial roles you hold
Marriage rules are unrelated to language
The kinship vocabulary only applies to blood relatives
Only elders must follow clan rules
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The kinship vocabulary from Unit 2 and the clan system from this lesson are the same social structure viewed from two angles. Language is social architecture.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this clan name aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: ro-tih-ske-RÉ-wake. Stress on the fourth syllable. A long, ceremonial-sounding word.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: The Kanien'kehá:ka have three founding clans — Bear, Turtle, Wolf. Belonging to a clan means belonging to a network of people across all Haudenosaunee communities, with specific rights and responsibilities. Many people learning this language are also discovering (or recovering) their own clan identity. Do you know your clan, or do you know where you might find out? What would it mean to know?

title: Unit 7 — Food, Land & Ceremony
desc: Fruits, traditional foods, cultural items, household vocabulary, nature & seasons, and cultural heroes

lessons.title: Lesson 23 — Fruits & Traditional Foods
lessons.sub: What the land gives us
intro: Kanien'kéha has rich vocabulary for the fruits of the land. Many of these words appear in the Thanksgiving Address — the berries, plants, and fruits of the earth are acknowledged as gifts.
The Strawberry (Ken' niiohontésha') holds particular ceremonial importance as the first fruit of spring — the Strawberry Ceremony marks the return of sweetness to the world after winter.

The Three Sisters — corn (Onón:ta), beans (Ohsahéta'), and squash (Otá:ra) — are the agricultural foundation of Haudenosaunee life. They are companion plants: grown together, they sustain each other and the people.
lessons.ped: Food as cultural knowledge: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) emphasise that language revitalization must connect to land-based knowledge. Food vocabulary is not neutral — it maps the relationship between the people and their territory.

exercises.prompt: What does Ken' niiohontésha' mean?
exercises.correct: Strawberries
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ken' niiohontésha' — strawberries. The first fruit of spring, celebrated in the Strawberry Ceremony.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Onón:ta means corn. What are the Three Sisters?
exercises.correct: Corn (Onón:ta), beans (Ohsahéta'), and squash (Otá:ra) — the three companion crops of Haudenosaunee agriculture
options: Corn (Onón:ta), beans (Ohsahéta'), and squash (Otá:ra) — the three companion crops of Haudenosaunee agriculture
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The Three Sisters are companion crops: corn provides the trellis for beans, beans fix nitrogen in the soil, and squash shades the ground to retain moisture. They are grown together and named together.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the fruits
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Four fruits of the Kanehsatà:ke territory.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the Three Sisters
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Onón:ta, Ohsahéta', Otá:ra — the Three Sisters. Grow them together and name them together.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ohsi'tá:na means apples. The apple orchards at Kanehsatà:ke were historically significant. Why?
exercises.correct: The 1990 Oka Crisis partly involved the threatened destruction of orchards and a sacred pine grove for a golf course expansion
options: The 1990 Oka Crisis partly involved the threatened destruction of orchards and a sacred pine grove for a golf course expansion
Apples were the most traded food
Apple orchards were introduced in the 1990s
Apples are not significant at Kanehsatà:ke
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The land that community defended in 1990 included orchards, a sacred pine grove, and a traditional burial ground. The word Ohsi'tá:na connects to that history.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Strawberries'?
exercises.correct: Ken' niiohontésha'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ken' niiohontésha'. A beautiful and phonetically rich word for the first fruit of spring.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Ken' niiohontésha'

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'corn'
exercises.correct: ta
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onón:ta — corn. The most sacred of the Three Sisters.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ta'.

exercises.prompt: Ohsahéta' (beans), Onón:ta (corn), and Otá:ra (squash) are companion plants. How does growing them together mirror the Haudenosaunee social philosophy you have learned?
exercises.correct: Interdependence — different members of a community support each other, just as the Three Sisters support each other in the garden
options: Interdependence — different members of a community support each other, just as the Three Sisters support each other in the garden
They are grown together only for convenience
The connection to social philosophy is metaphorical only
Only one of the three is important
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Three Sisters are a living metaphor for confederacy — different nations, different strengths, working together. The clan system, the Great Law, and the Three Sisters are all expressions of the same underlying value.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Three syllables: o-NÓN-ta. Stress on the second syllable. The long vowel (:) after nón is held slightly.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Ken' niiohontésha' — strawberries, the first sweetness after winter. The Strawberry Ceremony marks their return. Think of something in your life that returns each year and signals renewal — a season, a food, a person, an activity. What does its return mean to you? What would you give thanks for when it arrives?

lessons.title: Lesson 24 — Foods of the Home
lessons.sub: What we eat, and what it carries
intro: Beyond the Three Sisters and fruits, Kanien'kéha vocabulary covers the broader range of foods in modern Kanien'kehá:ka households. This lesson covers everyday food nouns from Lesson 30 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum.
Several words here demonstrate noun compounding in action: Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron (bear meat) combines Ohkwá:ri (bear, from the wild animals lesson) with o'wá:ron (meat) — you already know both halves.
Food vocabulary in Kanien'kéha also reflects the history of the community — some words are ancient, others adapted to post-contact ingredients.
lessons.ped: Compounding as review: Presenting Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron (bear meat) as a compound draws on vocabulary from two previous lessons (animals and food). This is spaced retrieval practice — encountering known words in new combinations strengthens long-term memory (Nation, 2001).

exercises.prompt: What does Ohnenná:ta mean?
exercises.correct: Potatoes
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohnenná:ta — potatoes. An important staple in the modern Kanien'kehá:ka household.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron means 'bear meat'. You already know both words separately. What is o'wá:ron?
exercises.correct: Meat (general term)
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! o'wá:ron = meat. Ohkwá:ri = bear. Together: bear meat. Kanien'kéha compounds nouns by placing them side by side — the same strategy you saw in shape names.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the food words
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Everyday food vocabulary for the home.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'meat'
exercises.correct: ron
exercises.hint: r
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! O'wá:ron — meat. The glottal stop (') at the beginning is part of the word.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ron'.

exercises.prompt: Kanà:taros means bread. Corn bread is Onón:ta kanà:taros. Based on this, what does the compound Onón:ta kanà:taros literally describe?
exercises.correct: Corn + bread = corn bread (a direct compound of the two noun roots)
options: Corn + bread = corn bread (a direct compound of the two noun roots)
A new word with no connection to the parts
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is the same compounding strategy as bear meat (Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron). Kanien'kéha builds compound nouns by placing nouns side by side. Knowing the parts gives you the compound.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: You learned corn in the previous lesson. How do you say it?
exercises.sub: Spaced review
exercises.correct: Onón:ta
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Onón:ta — corn. The most sacred of the Three Sisters.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Onón:ta

exercises.prompt: O'wá:ron (meat) in traditional diet primarily referred to game — deer, moose, bear. How does knowing the wild animal vocabulary from the previous unit connect to food vocabulary?
exercises.correct: Wild animal names + o'wá:ron (meat) generate compound food words: Oskenhón:ton o'wá:ron (deer meat), Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron (bear meat)
options: Wild animal names + o'wá:ron (meat) generate compound food words: Oskenhón:ton o'wá:ron (deer meat), Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron (bear meat)
Animal names and food names are unrelated
Only bear meat has a Kanien'kéha name
Food vocabulary is a separate system
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is the power of systematic vocabulary learning — once you know the pattern (animal + o'wá:ron = that animal's meat), you can generate new compound words yourself.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Four syllables: oh-nen-NÁ-ta. Stress on the third syllable. The long vowel (:) before 'ta' is important.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Onón:ta — corn. Sacred, central, sustaining. The Haudenosaunee Green Corn Ceremony gives thanks for the corn harvest. Think about a food that is central to your family or community — not just nutritionally but in terms of memory, identity, gathering. What would you give thanks for? What is your Onón:ta?

lessons.title: Lesson 25 — Cultural Items & Political Vocabulary
lessons.sub: The objects that hold the culture
intro: This lesson introduces vocabulary from Lesson 31 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum — cultural items and political vocabulary central to Kanien'kehá:ka identity.
The Longhouse (Kanonsesné:ha) is not only a building — it is the governing unit of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy. 'People of the Longhouse' is how the Haudenosaunee describe themselves.

The Great Law of Peace (Kaià:ner) is the constitution of the Confederacy. Peace (Skennón:'a), strength/righteousness, and good mind are its three principles. Native tobacco (Onkwatà:wen) carries prayers upward — it is ceremonial, not recreational.
The Two Dog Wampum Belt was repatriated to Kanehsatà:ke from the McCord Stewart Museum in 2024, a landmark act described on the Kontinónhstats website.
lessons.ped: Political vocabulary as critical literacy: Freire (1970) argued that reading the word means reading the world. Learning Kaià:ner (Great Law), Skennón:'a (peace), Iakoiánehr (clan mother), and Rotiiánehr (longhouse chief) is reading the Haudenosaunee political world — its institutions, its principles, its structure.

exercises.prompt: What does Kanonsesné:ha mean?
exercises.correct: Longhouse
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanonsesné:ha — longhouse. The Haudenosaunee Confederacy is 'People of the Longhouse'. Clan mothers governed the longhouse.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Kaià:ner mean?
exercises.correct: The Great Law of Peace / The Good Path
options: The Great Law of Peace / The Good Path
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Kaià:ner — the Good Path, the Great Law of Peace. The three principles are peace (Skennón:'a), strength/righteousness, and good mind.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the cultural and political vocabulary
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Longhouse, Great Law, Wampum, Peace — the political architecture of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy named.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Onkwatà:wen (native tobacco) is used ceremonially, not recreationally. What is its ceremonial function?
exercises.correct: Tobacco carries prayers upward — it is placed in fire, water, or earth as an offering and expression of thanks
options: Tobacco carries prayers upward — it is placed in fire, water, or earth as an offering and expression of thanks
Tobacco is used for trade only
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The ceremonial use of native tobacco (Onkwatà:wen) is distinct from commercial tobacco. It is an offering — a way of sending thanks and prayer into the world.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: The Two Dog Wampum Belt (Teioháte) was returned to Kanehsatà:ke from the McCord Stewart Museum in 2024. What does wampum repatriation represent?
exercises.correct: The return of cultural records and governance documents to the community they belong to
options: The return of cultural records and governance documents to the community they belong to
A museum policy change unrelated to community
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Wampum belts are records — of treaties, alliances, and history. Their repatriation is the return of a community's own documentary archive. The Kontinónhstats website covers this directly.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Peace'?
exercises.correct: Skennón:'a
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Skennón:'a — peace. One of the three principles of the Great Law of Peace.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Skennón:'a

exercises.prompt: Ionkwaiénhton (corn husk doll) has no face in Haudenosaunee tradition. What does the oral tradition say about why?
exercises.correct: She gave her face to the people and was taught humility — she is not to look at her own reflection
options: She gave her face to the people and was taught humility — she is not to look at her own reflection
The face was never added for practical reasons
Corn husk dolls are not ceremonial
The face was added later in history
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The corn husk doll story is a teaching about humility and service. Learning these cultural items means learning the stories that give them meaning.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Three syllables: sken-NÓN-'a. The glottal stop (') at the end gives it a soft, complete quality. A word that ends with intention.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Skennón:'a — peace. One of three principles that united five warring nations under the Great Law. Peace here is not the absence of conflict — it is an active choice, a political project, a word that required a whole confederacy to embody. What does peace mean in your own life, in your own community? Is it something you have, something you build, or something you carry?

lessons.title: Lesson 26 — Household Vocabulary
lessons.sub: The language of everyday home life
intro: Lesson 32 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum covers household items — the objects of daily domestic life. Like domestic animals, many household items arrived with European contact, and Kanien'kéha named them from within its own grammatical system.
Several of these words connect to the body part vocabulary you learned earlier: soap connects to washing, towel to the 'washing face' verb, bed to resting.
Knowing household vocabulary is essential for using Kanien'kéha in daily life — one of the goals of the Kontinónhstats curriculum is to make the language present in everyday domestic conversation.
lessons.ped: Domain expansion: García & Li Wei (2014) identify domestic and household domains as critical for language vitality. When Kanien'kéha can be spoken in the kitchen and bathroom as well as in ceremony, it is truly a language of daily life.

exercises.prompt: What does Atsihnhétste' mean?
exercises.correct: Soap
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Atsihnhétste' — soap. Essential for the 'washing hands' and 'washing face' verbs you learned from the A-stem lesson.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Ohkwáriyentha' mean?
exercises.correct: Bed
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ohkwáriyentha' — bed. The -yentha' locative suffix marks 'the place where one does something'. Compare ani'tskwá:ra (chair) from the A-class nouns lesson.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the household items
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Three household items — each connected to a daily routine.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Atsihnhétste' (soap) connects to two A-stem verbs you already learned. Which ones?
exercises.sub: Think about what soap is used for
exercises.correct: Washing hands (sato'nnenhstáhkwa's) and washing face — both body-care verbs from the A-stem lesson
options: Washing hands (sato'nnenhstáhkwa's) and washing face — both body-care verbs from the A-stem lesson
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Household objects connect to the verbs that use them. Soap → washing hands/face. This is how vocabulary networks build — each word connects to others you already know.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Soap'?
exercises.correct: Atsihnhétste'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Atsihnhétste' — soap. An A-class noun beginning with 'at'.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Atsihnhétste'

exercises.prompt: Atié:wen (towel/face cloth) connects to which body part vocabulary from the Body Parts lesson?
exercises.correct: Ononkwa' (head/face) and the face-washing verb — towels are used after washing
options: Ononkwa' (head/face) and the face-washing verb — towels are used after washing
None — household and body vocabulary are separate
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Vocabulary networks: towel connects to face, which connects to the face-washing verb, which connects to soap. Learning in networks is more efficient than learning isolated words.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: oh-kwá-ri-yen-tha'. The glottal stop (') at the end is the locative suffix '-yentha'' meaning 'the place of'. Stress on the second syllable.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohkwáriyentha' — the place where one rests. The -yentha' suffix turns any activity into a place: the place of resting, the place of washing, the place of sitting. Think about the spaces in your home — each one is defined by what happens there. Which space in your home would you most want to be able to name in Kanien'kéha, and why?

lessons.title: Lesson 27 — The Natural World: Seasons & Land
lessons.sub: The world we live in and move through
intro: This lesson covers the vocabulary of the natural world from Lesson 33 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum: the moon, the earth, trees, rivers, mountains, and the four seasons.

Many of these words appear in the Thanksgiving Address. The moon (Tsí:tewen, 'Grandmother Moon') governs tides and the female cycle. The maple (Wahta:) marks the beginning of spring with its sap. The river (Kaniatarà:ke) is the St. Lawrence — the waterway of Kanehsatà:ke's history.

The four seasons in Kanien'kéha map onto a ceremonial calendar: spring (Sé:ske) brings the Strawberry Ceremony; summer (Ohiarí:ha) is the growing season; autumn (Ken'niká:ien) brings the Green Corn and Harvest Ceremonies; winter (Kenién:ke) is a time of storytelling.
lessons.ped: Land-based vocabulary and seasonal ceremony: In Haudenosaunee pedagogy, the land is not background — it is the primary text. Learning the seasons means learning when to hold ceremonies, when to plant, when to harvest. This is what Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) mean by decolonizing language pedagogy: the curriculum should serve the community's relationship to the land.

exercises.prompt: What does Tsí:tewen mean?
exercises.correct: Moon
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsí:tewen — moon, our Grandmother Moon. You already know Karáhkwa (sun) from the shapes lesson. Sun and moon — two celestial bodies now named.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the four seasons
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The four seasons — each connected to ceremony, agriculture, and the natural world.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Sé:ske (spring) is associated with which ceremony?
exercises.correct: The Strawberry Ceremony — celebrating the first fruit of spring
options: The Strawberry Ceremony — celebrating the first fruit of spring
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Sé:ske → Strawberry Ceremony (Ken' niiohontésha'). You already know the word for strawberries from the food lesson. The vocabulary connects.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Wahta: means maple tree. Why is the maple tree particularly significant at Kanehsatà:ke?
exercises.correct: The sugar bush tradition — gathering maple sap each spring — is a land-based practice connecting community to territory
options: The sugar bush tradition — gathering maple sap each spring — is a land-based practice connecting community to territory
Maple was used to make longhouses
The maple tree has no special significance
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The sugar bush (tapping maple trees for sap) is a spring ceremony and a community activity. The maple leaf appears on the Canadian flag — but for the Kanien'kehá:ka, Wahta: is much more than a national symbol.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the nature vocabulary
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Moon, earth, maple, river — the natural world named.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Spring'?
exercises.correct: Sé:ske
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Sé:ske — spring. A short, clean word for renewal.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Sé:ske

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for autumn
exercises.correct: ien
exercises.hint: i
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ken'niká:ien — autumn. The season of the Green Corn and Harvest Ceremonies.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ien'.

exercises.prompt: Tsí:tewen (moon) is called 'Grandmother Moon' in Haudenosaunee tradition. You already know the word for grandmother. What is it?
exercises.sub: Spaced review from Unit 2
exercises.correct: tota (base form) / Aksótha (my grandmother)
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! tota / Aksótha — grandmother. The moon is Tsí:tewen, Grandmother Moon. The kinship vocabulary you learned in Unit 2 extends into the ceremonial and natural world.
exercises.wm: Please try again — think back to the female kinship nouns.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: ken'-ni-KÁ-ien. The glottal stop after Ken' is important. Stress on the third syllable.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Kenién:ke — winter. In Haudenosaunee tradition, winter is the time for telling the old stories — the stories that can only be told when snow is on the ground. The language itself has rules about when certain things are said, sung, or told. What is the winter of your own life — a time of quiet, of inwardness, of stories? What do you hold during that season that you put down in others?

lessons.title: Lesson 28 — Cultural Heroes, Leaders & Legends
lessons.sub: The people and stories that made the nation
intro: The final lesson of this unit draws from Lesson 34 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum — the names of cultural figures, political roles, and legendary beings of the Haudenosaunee tradition.

Tekanawí:ta (the Peacemaker) and Aiionwátha (Hiawatha) founded the Haudenosaunee Confederacy. Their story is one of the most important political narratives in North American history — and yet most people outside the Haudenosaunee communities do not know it.
Iakoiánehr (clan mother) and Rotiiánehr (longhouse chief) are the two central political roles of the Confederacy. Shonkwaiá:tison (the Creator) closes the Thanksgiving Address.
Learning these names is learning a political and spiritual genealogy — the people and powers that made and sustain the community.
lessons.ped: Cultural heroes as curriculum: Tuck & Yang (2012) argue that decolonization involves not just learning about the present but restoring a connection to the historical and spiritual lineage. These names — the Peacemaker, Hiawatha, clan mothers — are that lineage, made speakable in the language.

exercises.prompt: Tekanawí:ta is the Peacemaker. What did the Peacemaker accomplish?
exercises.correct: United five warring nations under the Great Law of Peace, founding the Haudenosaunee Confederacy
options: United five warring nations under the Great Law of Peace, founding the Haudenosaunee Confederacy
Led a military campaign against European settlers
Established the first longhouse at Kanehsatà:ke
Wrote down the first Kanien'kéha dictionary
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tekanawí:ta — the Peacemaker. Together with Aiionwátha (Hiawatha), he brought the Great Law of Peace to the five founding nations.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Iakoiánehr mean?
exercises.correct: Clan Mother
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Iakoiánehr — clan mother. The most important political figure in the Haudenosaunee system, who selects and can remove chiefs.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the cultural and political figures
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The founding figures and the central political roles of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Aiionwátha is often anglicised as 'Hiawatha'. What is important to note about the historical Aiionwátha?
exercises.correct: He is a real Mohawk political figure who worked alongside the Peacemaker — not the fictional character in Longfellow's poem
options: He is a real Mohawk political figure who worked alongside the Peacemaker — not the fictional character in Longfellow's poem
He and the fictional Hiawatha are the same person
Longfellow's poem accurately depicts his life
Aiionwátha is a legendary figure with no historical basis
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Longfellow's 1855 poem 'The Song of Hiawatha' is a work of fiction that appropriated the name from Haudenosaunee tradition and applied it to an Ojibwe setting. The historical Aiionwátha is a Mohawk political leader of immense importance.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Rotiiánehr (longhouse chief) serves 'at the pleasure of the clan mother'. What does this mean?
exercises.correct: Clan mothers select chiefs and can remove them — chiefs serve the people, not themselves
options: Clan mothers select chiefs and can remove them — chiefs serve the people, not themselves
Chiefs are elected by popular vote
Only men can become clan mothers or chiefs
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is the structure of the Great Law: clan mothers (Iakoiánehr) hold the ultimate authority. They select chiefs (Rotiiánehr) and can 'dehorn' them — remove their chief's antlers — if they fail the people. The system has checks and balances.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'The Creator'?
exercises.correct: Shonkwaiá:tison
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shonkwaiá:tison — 'the one who created us'. Named last in the Thanksgiving Address, after all of creation has been acknowledged.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Shonkwaiá:tison

exercises.prompt: Complete the Peacemaker's name
exercises.correct: ta
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Tekanawí:ta. A name spoken with respect.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ta'.

exercises.prompt: Say this name aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: shon-kwai-Á-ti-son. Stress on the third syllable. The colon (:) after á extends the vowel slightly. A weighty, complete word.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Tekanawí:ta — the Peacemaker. He brought a vision of peace to five nations that had been at war. The story of how he did this — the persuasion, the ceremony, the planting of the Great Tree of Peace — is one of the great political stories in human history. Think of a moment in your own life when peace had to be actively built, not just hoped for. What did it require?

title: Unit 8 — Tools, Verb Stems & More Grammar
desc: Tool nouns, consonant-stem verbs, I-stem verbs, True E-stem verbs, and dual/plural commands

lessons.title: Lesson 29 — Tool Nouns (Famine Indefinite Class)
lessons.sub: The things that do things
intro: Lesson 10 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum introduces a special noun class called 'famine indefinite nouns' — nouns beginning with the syllables ie-, ion-, iako-, teion-, tewa-. These are verb-derived nouns: the word describes what the object does.

Iekwahrahnhá:ra' (hammer) = the thing that pounds. Ionkwarihón:ni (scissors) = the thing that cuts things. Ietkarihón:ni (knife) = the cutting thing. This naming strategy — describing function — is consistent with the pattern you've seen throughout the course.
The famine indefinite class is one of the most productive noun classes for naming new objects, because any tool can be named by describing what it does.
lessons.ped: Productive naming continued: The famine indefinite class demonstrates that Kanien'kéha's productivity (Mary McDonald's term) extends to tools and technology. A hammer is not just a hammer — it is 'the thing that pounds'. Learning the pattern means you can generate novel names.

exercises.prompt: Ionkwarihón:ni means scissors. The root -hon:ni appears in several tool words. What does it likely indicate?
exercises.correct: The object that does/makes something — a functional noun suffix
options: The object that does/makes something — a functional noun suffix
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! -hon:ni is a nominalising suffix meaning roughly 'the thing that does X'. You saw it in Ietkarihón:ni (knife = cutting-thing) and Ionkwarihón:ni (scissors = cuts-things). The suffix unlocks a whole category of tool naming.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Iekwahrahnhá:ra' mean?
exercises.correct: Hammer
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iekwahrahnhá:ra' — hammer. A famine indefinite noun describing the pounding action.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the tool nouns
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Four tools — each named by what it does.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Iakoa'sháhen (awl / hole puncher) is described as a traditional tool. What was it traditionally used for?
exercises.correct: Punching holes in hides for sewing — before metal needles, bone or antler awls were used
options: Punching holes in hides for sewing — before metal needles, bone or antler awls were used
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The awl is one of the oldest tools in the archaeological record at Haudenosaunee sites. Before European metal needles, bone and antler awls were used to pierce hides for garment construction.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ietshennón:ni (screwdriver) is a modern tool given a Kanien'kéha name. How does this connect to what you learned about domestic animals and Ka-class nouns?
exercises.correct: All three cases show Kanien'kéha naming new objects from within its own grammatical system rather than borrowing foreign words
options: All three cases show Kanien'kéha naming new objects from within its own grammatical system rather than borrowing foreign words
Screwdrivers are an ancient Haudenosaunee tool
Ka-class and famine indefinite nouns are the same
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The consistent thread: Kanien'kéha generates new vocabulary from existing patterns. Horse = 'it carries on its back'. Screwdriver = 'the thing that turns'. Car = Kà:sere' (Ka-class). Each new object gets a name grounded in what it does.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Hammer'?
exercises.correct: Iekwahrahnhá:ra'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Iekwahrahnhá:ra' — the pounding thing.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Iekwahrahnhá:ra'

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for scissors
exercises.correct: ni
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ionkwarihón:ni — the thing that cuts things.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ni'.

exercises.prompt: Iekwáhhere' (shovel) begins with Ie- and describes digging. How does the Ie- prefix signal the noun class?
exercises.correct: Ie- (and Ion-, Iak-, Teion-, Tewa-) are the opening syllables that mark the famine indefinite class — verb-derived functional nouns
options: Ie- (and Ion-, Iak-, Teion-, Tewa-) are the opening syllables that mark the famine indefinite class — verb-derived functional nouns
Ie- is a prefix borrowed from French
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The famine indefinite class is identified by its initial syllables: ie-, ion-, iako-, teion-, tewa-. These syllables are the surface trace of underlying verb roots — the noun is built on a verb.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Six syllables: ion-kwa-ri-HÓN-ni. Stress on the fourth syllable. The long vowel (:) before ni holds briefly.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Ietkarihón:ni — knife — the cutting thing. In Haudenosaunee tradition, the knife is one of the oldest tools — used for hunting, preparing food, carving, and medicine. A language that names its tools by what they do encodes a different relationship to objects than one that gives them arbitrary names. Think about the tools in your own life: what would you call a hammer if you had to describe it rather than name it?

lessons.title: Lesson 30 — Consonant-Stem Verbs
lessons.sub: The second verb stem class
intro: You learned A-stem verbs in Unit 3. Now meet the consonant-stem class — verbs whose root begins with a consonant (h, s, t, k). These are among the most common action verbs in Kanien'kéha.

The key difference from A-stem verbs: when the pronoun 'I' (k-) attaches to a consonant-stem root, it doubles the initial consonant. So where A-stem gives you k-á:tien (I sit), consonant-stem gives you kk-we'tarón:ko (I cut it up). The doubled consonant is the diagnostic feature.
Dualic consonant-stem verbs again use the te- prefix you know from dual pronouns and A-stem dualic verbs — the same prefix carries the same meaning across all verb classes.
lessons.ped: Cross-stem pattern recognition: Each new verb stem class reinforces the underlying grammar of Kanien'kéha. The te- dualic prefix, the -s habitual suffix, and the command-without-suffix rule all appear consistently. Learning the second stem class deepens the pattern rather than adding new rules.

exercises.prompt: kkwe'tarón:kwas means 'I cut it up (habitual)'. What is distinctive about the k- pronoun in consonant-stem verbs?
exercises.correct: The k- pronoun doubles the initial consonant of the root — giving kk- at the start
options: The k- pronoun doubles the initial consonant of the root — giving kk- at the start
Nothing — it is the same as A-stem
The k- pronoun only appears in singular forms
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! In consonant-stem verbs, k- (I) + a consonant root initial = doubled consonant. kk-we'tarón:kwas. This is the diagnostic feature that identifies the consonant-stem class.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does skwe'tarón:ko mean?
exercises.correct: You cut it up (command)
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! skwe'tarón:ko — you cut it up. Command form: s- (you) + root. No habitual suffix.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the consonant-stem verb forms
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Consonant-stem verbs in four forms. Notice the doubled kk- in the first-person form.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: tetshon'ká:ria'k (you saw the board) is dualic. Why would sawing be a dualic verb?
exercises.correct: Sawing requires two motions — pushing and pulling — so it takes the te- dualic prefix
options: Sawing requires two motions — pushing and pulling — so it takes the te- dualic prefix
Dualic means two people are sawing
The te- prefix marks consonant-stem verbs
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! You've seen this logic before: tesaráhtat (run) is dualic because running requires two phases (standing → moving). Sawing: push + pull = two actions = te-. The same grammatical logic applies across verb classes.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sweeping verb
exercises.hint: The command form ends at skáhere' — no suffix added
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! skáhere' — you sweep. Commands never take a suffix. The word is complete.
exercises.wm: The word skáhere' is already complete — it's a command form.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'You give' (command)?
exercises.correct: skahó:wi
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! skahó:wi — you give. A consonant-stem command used in daily exchange and ceremony.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: skahó:wi

exercises.prompt: The consonant-stem class has s- for 'you' in commands (like A-stem). But what makes the first-person form visibly different?
exercises.correct: The kk- doubled consonant at the start: kkwe'tarón:kwas vs ká:tiens
options: The kk- doubled consonant at the start: kkwe'tarón:kwas vs ká:tiens
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! A-stem first person: k-á:tiens (single k). Consonant-stem first person: kk-we'tarón:kwas (doubled kk). This doubling is the surface signal of the consonant-stem class.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: You now know two verb stem classes (A-stem and consonant-stem) out of six total. What are the six stem classes in Mary McDonald's curriculum?
exercises.correct: A stem, consonant stem, fake E stem, O/On stem, I stem, True E stem
options: A stem, consonant stem, fake E stem, O/On stem, I stem, True E stem
Past, present, future, command, habitual, dualic
Singular, dual, plural, male, female, neutral
Subjective, objective, reflexive, causative, passive, middle
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The six verb stem classes are the core organising principle of Kanien'kéha verb morphology. You've now seen A-stem (Units 3 and 10) and consonant-stem (this lesson). I-stem and True E-stem are in the next two lessons.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this verb aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: skwe'-ta-RÓN-ko. Stress on the fourth syllable. The initial skw- cluster is a quick consonant blend.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: skáhere' — you sweep. Sweeping is a daily household action — humble, repetitive, essential. In the Freire (1970) tradition of literacy, the most generative words are those rooted in daily life. Sweeping, cutting, giving. These are the actions that fill the hours of any life. Which everyday action in your life would you most want to be able to name in Kanien'kéha?

lessons.title: Lesson 31 — I-Stem & True E-Stem Verbs
lessons.sub: The remaining verb stem classes
intro: Two more verb stem classes complete your verb vocabulary toolkit. The I-stem class has verb roots beginning with 'i' — the distinctive feature is that the pronoun 'you' appears as ts- rather than s-. You also hear a distinctive (sh) sound when s falls between h and i.
The True E-stem class has roots beginning with 'é'. These are common in everyday speech — expressing putting, remembering, wanting, and mowing. The pronoun 'you' is s- as in A-stem.
Together with A-stem and consonant-stem, these bring you to four of the six verb classes. With all four, you can produce commands and habitual forms for the most common verbs in Kanien'kéha.
lessons.ped: Completing the verb system: Mary McDonald's curriculum teaches all six stem classes in Lessons 12–17. By lesson 31, you have seen A-stem (L12), consonant-stem (L13), I-stem (L16), and True E-stem (L17). The pattern is consistent: s- or ts- for 'you', k- or ki- for 'I', -s for habitual, te- for dualic.

exercises.prompt: tsí'stenht (you take scales off fish) uses ts- for 'you'. Why ts- instead of s-?
exercises.correct: I-stem verbs use ts- as the 'you' pronoun — this is the diagnostic feature of the I-stem class
options: I-stem verbs use ts- as the 'you' pronoun — this is the diagnostic feature of the I-stem class
ts- is only used with fish verbs
ts- is a dialect variant of s-
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The I-stem class is identified by ts- (you) instead of s-. When the s falls between h and i, you also get the English 'sh' sound. This is a phonological feature of the class, not a random variation.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does sé:ta mean?
exercises.correct: You put it in (command)
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! sé:ta — you put it in. True E-stem command: s- (you) + é:ta (E-stem root).
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the verb forms
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! I-stem and E-stem verbs — four common actions.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: ské:ta' means 'do you remember?' The same root appears in ké:ta'ne's (I remember, habitual). What does this tell you about how questions and statements relate in Kanien'kéha?
exercises.correct: Questions and statements use the same verb root — the difference is in the pronoun and context, not in a special question word like English 'do'
options: Questions and statements use the same verb root — the difference is in the pronoun and context, not in a special question word like English 'do'
Questions always use a different verb
ské:ta' only means 'remember' not 'do you remember'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! English uses auxiliary verbs ('do you remember?') to form questions. Kanien'kéha does not — the same verb form can be a command ('remember!') or a question ('do you remember?') depending on intonation and context.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: sahshá:wi means 'you want'. What is the habitual form 'I want'?
exercises.sub: Based on the pattern: s- → k-; command → habitual adds -s
exercises.correct: kahshá:wis
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! s- (you) → k- (I), command → habitual adds -s: kahshá:wis (I want, habitually). The same pattern works across all E-stem verbs.
exercises.wm: Please try again — apply the k- pronoun and -s suffix.

exercises.prompt: Complete the mowing verb
exercises.hint: The command ends at sanerénhton
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! sanerénhton — you mow the lawn. Command form — no suffix.
exercises.wm: The verb is complete as sanerénhton.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'You make a fire' (command)?
exercises.correct: sahsennón:ni
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! sahsennón:ni — you make a fire. An I-stem command.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: sahsennón:ni

exercises.prompt: tenhsi'tstenhrhó:tha' means 'you are a bricklayer'. What does the -rhó:tha' suffix indicate?
exercises.correct: It is the habitual suffix marking an ongoing occupation or repeated action
options: It is the habitual suffix marking an ongoing occupation or repeated action
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The habitual suffix (-s in simple forms, -rhó:tha' in longer dualic forms) marks actions done repeatedly or professionally. 'You are a bricklayer' = you habitually lay bricks. Occupations in Kanien'kéha are expressed as habitual verbs.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this verb aloud
exercises.hint: Two syllables: SÉ-ta. Very short. Stress on the first syllable. The colon (:) extends the é slightly. A common, useful verb.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: ské:ta' — do you remember? Memory is the vessel that carries language. For many learners of Kanien'kéha, 'do you remember?' is also a question about cultural memory — of ceremonies half-forgotten, of grandparents' phrases, of a childhood word heard once. What do you remember? What are you trying not to forget?

title: Unit 9 — Fish, Birds, Numbers & Conversation
desc: Fish and bird vocabulary, numbers to 100, dual/plural commands, and everyday conversation

lessons.title: Lesson 32 — Fish of the Waters
lessons.sub: Aquatic life of the Kanehsatà:ke territory
intro: Kanien'kéha has rich vocabulary for the fish of the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence watershed. Lesson 24 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum introduces 12 species, reflecting the depth of Haudenosaunee ecological knowledge.

Several fish in this lesson carry environmental and political significance. Eel (Aterón:taks) gives its name to the Eel Clan. American eel populations in the St. Lawrence have collapsed due to hydroelectric dams — their restoration is an environmental justice issue tied directly to Haudenosaunee territory.
Sturgeon (Kahshakaié:ra') was a major food source in the St. Lawrence — the fish can live over 100 years and grow to enormous size. Lake sturgeon are now threatened.
Learning fish vocabulary is learning a map of the waters — and the threats those waters now face.
lessons.ped: Language and environmental knowledge: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) emphasise that Indigenous languages carry ecological knowledge. Fish vocabulary encodes what species are present, which are significant, and which carry social identity (the Eel Clan). This is not just biology — it is a relational inventory of the watershed.

exercises.prompt: Aterón:taks means eel. Which clan takes its name from this fish?
exercises.correct: The Eel Clan (Rotineniotthró:non) — present at Kanehsatà:ke through the historical Onondaga resettlement
options: The Eel Clan (Rotineniotthró:non) — present at Kanehsatà:ke through the historical Onondaga resettlement
No clan uses the eel name
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Aterón:taks (eel) → Rotineniotthró:non (Eel Clan). You learned the clan names in Unit 6. The fish vocabulary and the clan vocabulary are the same words in different grammatical contexts.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Kahshakaié:ra' means sturgeon. Why is the sturgeon significant to Haudenosaunee communities today?
exercises.correct: Lake sturgeon are now threatened due to dams, pollution, and overfishing — their restoration is an environmental and territorial justice issue
options: Lake sturgeon are now threatened due to dams, pollution, and overfishing — their restoration is an environmental and territorial justice issue
Sturgeon are only used in ceremony
Sturgeon were never a food source
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The sturgeon can live over a century — some alive today were born before the Indian Act. Their near-disappearance from the St. Lawrence is part of the same colonial disruption as the near-disappearance of Kanien'kéha.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the fish to their names
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Trout, sturgeon, salmon, eel — four species of the Kanehsatà:ke watershed.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match more fish
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Pike, bass, sunfish — three more species of the local waters.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Trout'?
exercises.correct: Namé:s
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Namé:s — trout. Two syllables: na-MÉ-s.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Namé:s

exercises.prompt: Kotihnawá:ra's (salmon) once ran in great numbers through the St. Lawrence. What happened to Atlantic salmon runs?
exercises.correct: Dams, pollution, and overfishing dramatically reduced salmon populations in the St. Lawrence watershed
options: Dams, pollution, and overfishing dramatically reduced salmon populations in the St. Lawrence watershed
Salmon populations are at record highs
Salmon were never in the St. Lawrence
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The same colonial infrastructure (dams, industrial pollutants, commercial overfishing) that disrupted Haudenosaunee territorial rights also disrupted the fish runs that sustained their diet. Language, land, water, and food are the same system.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for eel
exercises.correct: ks
exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Aterón:taks — eel. The Eel Clan fish.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ks'.

exercises.prompt: Learning fish vocabulary, clan names, and environmental history together reflects which pedagogical principle?
exercises.correct: Language cannot be separated from cultural knowledge and relational accountability to the land (Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum, 2023)
options: Language cannot be separated from cultural knowledge and relational accountability to the land (Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum, 2023)
Vocabulary should be taught in isolated categories
Environmental history is not relevant to language learning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! This is the heart of decolonizing language pedagogy — the fish, the clan, the watershed, and the colonial history are all present in the word Aterón:taks (eel). Learning the word honestly requires learning all of this.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: ko-tih-na-WÁ-ra's. Stress on the fourth syllable. The glottal stop at the end (ra's) is part of the word.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Aterón:taks — eel. A fish that gives a clan its name. American eels migrate thousands of kilometres from the Sargasso Sea to the St. Lawrence and its tributaries to grow, before returning to spawn and die. Hydroelectric dams now block that journey for most of them. The eel's story is about obstruction, persistence, and the long way home. What obstruction in your own life are you finding a way around?

lessons.title: Lesson 33 — Birds of the Sky
lessons.sub: The winged ones of Kanehsatà:ke
intro: The Thanksgiving Address acknowledges the birds collectively — 'the birds who use their songs to please us daily'. Lesson 25 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum introduces 12 birds of the Kanehsatà:ke territory.

The eagle (Á:kwe'ks) holds the highest ceremonial significance — eagle feathers mark achievement and carry prayers. In the founding story of the Confederacy, an eagle perches at the top of the Great Tree of Peace, watching for danger.
The goose (Káhonk) is the most visible seasonal marker in the region — their V-formations in spring and autumn are the clearest sign of seasonal change.
Bird knowledge is ecological knowledge: knowing which bird arrives when, which sings in which season, which nests where — this is the language of the land.
lessons.ped: Birds in the Thanksgiving Address: The birds are acknowledged as a collective in the Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — their songs 'please us daily'. Learning individual bird names enriches understanding of the Address: when you hear 'the birds', you now know specific birds that word includes.

exercises.prompt: Á:kwe'ks means eagle. In the founding story of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, where does the eagle perch?
exercises.correct: At the top of the Great Tree of Peace, watching for danger
options: At the top of the Great Tree of Peace, watching for danger
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The eagle (Á:kwe'ks) perches at the top of the Great Tree of Peace (Kaienrekówa / White Pine), watching for threats to the Confederacy. Eagle feathers mark achievement and carry prayers upward.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the birds
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Eagle, robin, hawk, goose — four birds of the Kanehsatà:ke territory.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the owl names
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Two different owl words — specific (horned owl) and general. Kanien'kéha maintains this distinction.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Káhonk (goose) is named for what?
exercises.sub: Think about what is distinctive about geese
exercises.correct: The sound they make — 'honk' is an onomatopoeic representation of the goose call
options: The sound they make — 'honk' is an onomatopoeic representation of the goose call
Their connection to the Turtle Clan
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Káhonk is onomatopoeic — the word imitates the sound. This is the same naming strategy as the English word 'honk'. Many bird names across languages are onomatopoeic — the sound becomes the name.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Kwáwenhs (robin) returns each spring. How does this connect to the ceremonial vocabulary you learned?
exercises.correct: The robin's return signals Sé:ske (spring) — which triggers the Strawberry Ceremony. Bird, season, and ceremony are connected
options: The robin's return signals Sé:ske (spring) — which triggers the Strawberry Ceremony. Bird, season, and ceremony are connected
The robin is associated with autumn only
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kwáwenhs (robin) → Sé:ske (spring) → Ken' niiohontésha' (strawberries) → Strawberry Ceremony. The natural world, the language, and the ceremonial calendar are the same system.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Eagle'?
exercises.correct: Á:kwe'ks
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Á:kwe'ks — eagle. Two syllables: Á-kwe'ks. The glottal stop (') is part of the word.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Á:kwe'ks

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for goose
exercises.correct: nk
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Káhonk — goose. Onomatopoeic — it sounds like what it names.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'nk'.

exercises.prompt: Tsistí:keri (horned owl) is associated with death or medicine in some Haudenosaunee traditions. What does learning this tell you about how Kanien'kéha vocabulary works?
exercises.correct: Words carry cultural associations — learning the word without the story gives you only half the knowledge
options: Words carry cultural associations — learning the word without the story gives you only half the knowledge
Cultural associations are not relevant to language learning
The word and the story are separate things
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is what García & Li Wei (2014) mean when they say that translanguaging involves the full linguistic and cultural repertoire. The word Tsistí:keri carries the owl story with it. Vocabulary without context is an empty container.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Two syllables: Á-kwe'ks. The long vowel on Á: is important. The final kwe'ks is a cluster — kw-glottal-ks.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Á:kwe'ks — eagle. Perched at the top of the Great Tree of Peace, watching for danger. In ceremony, eagle feathers mark those who have served the community. Think of someone in your life who watches — who keeps the long view, who sees what others miss. Who is your eagle?

lessons.title: Lesson 34 — Dual & Plural Commands
lessons.sub: Directing groups — the full command paradigm
intro: You have learned the singular command form throughout the course: sá:tien (you sit), tesaráhtat (you run). Lesson 35 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum extends this to dual and plural commands.
The pattern is consistent across all verb stems: replace the singular pronoun s- with seni- (you two) or sewa- (you all). You already know seni- and sewa- from the dual and plural pronoun lessons.
This is the moment the whole pronoun system pays off: the same seni- you learned as a standalone pronoun (teseniiáhshe = you two) now attaches to verbs to tell two people to act.
lessons.ped: Paradigm completion as mastery: Nation (2001) identifies 'productive control' — being able to generate the full paradigm from knowledge of its parts — as a key marker of vocabulary mastery. Dual and plural commands complete the command paradigm you have been building since Unit 1.

exercises.prompt: seniá:tien means 'you two sit'. You know sá:tien (you [one] sit). What changed?
exercises.correct: The singular pronoun s- was replaced by the dual pronoun prefix seni-
options: The singular pronoun s- was replaced by the dual pronoun prefix seni-
The dualic te- prefix was added
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! s- (you, singular) → seni- (you two, dual). The verb root á:tien stays the same. You already know seni- from teseniiáhshe (you two, pronoun). The same prefix appears in commands.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: sewá:tien means 'you all sit'. Which pronoun root does sewa- correspond to?
exercises.sub: Think back to the plural pronoun lesson
exercises.correct: sewakwé:kon — the 'you all' plural pronoun
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! sewakwé:kon (you all, pronoun) → sewa- (you all, verb prefix). The pronoun prefix is the first part of the full pronoun. The pattern is systematic across every pronoun.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the command forms of 'sit'
exercises.sub: Singular, dual, plural
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The complete command paradigm for sitting: singular s-, dual seni-, plural sewa-. Same root á:tien throughout.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the command forms of 'listen'
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Singular, dual, plural — the same pattern for listening as for sitting.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: teseniaráhtat means 'you two run'. You know tesaráhtat (you [one] run). What two changes happened?
exercises.correct: s- became seni- (singular → dual) AND the te- dualic prefix remains — running is still dualic regardless of number
options: s- became seni- (singular → dual) AND the te- dualic prefix remains — running is still dualic regardless of number
The te- prefix was added for duality of people
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! tesaráhtat → teseniaráhtat. The te- prefix stays because running is inherently dualic (two phases of motion). The pronoun s- → seni- because you are now directing two people. Both changes are independent.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you tell a group of people to sit?
exercises.correct: sewá:tien
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! sewá:tien — you all sit. The plural command.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: sewá:tien

exercises.prompt: tesewará:htat (you all run) uses sewa-. You now know the full command paradigm: singular s-, dual seni-, plural sewa-. Where have you seen these three roots before?
exercises.correct: As standalone pronouns: í:se' (you, singular), teseniiáhshe (you two), sewakwé:kon (you all)
options: As standalone pronouns: í:se' (you, singular), teseniiáhshe (you two), sewakwé:kon (you all)
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The pronoun roots you learned in Unit 1 (Lessons 1–3) are the same roots that now appear as verb command prefixes. Everything connects. Kanien'kéha grammar is a system, not a list.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the dual command 'you two sit'
exercises.correct: á:
exercises.hint: á
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! seniá:tien — you two sit. seni- (you two) + á:tien (root).
exercises.wm: Please try again — the root begins with á:.

exercises.prompt: Say this command aloud
exercises.sub: Imagine addressing a group
exercises.hint: Four syllables: se-WÁ-ti-en. Stress on the second syllable. A clear, direct command.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.meaning: You two sit / You all sit
exercises.storyPrompt: seniá:tien — you two sit. sewá:tien — you all sit. Commands directed at groups. Think of a moment when you needed to direct a group — when you were a teacher, a parent, an elder, an organiser. What is the feeling of being the one who says 'sit, listen, stand, run'? What responsibility does that grammatical position carry?

lessons.title: Lesson 35 — Numbers to 100
lessons.sub: Counting further in Kanien'kéha
intro: You learned numbers 1–10 in Unit 4. Now the system extends: Kanien'kéha uses a base-10 additive system — all numbers above 10 are compounds of the numbers you already know.

The pattern is transparent: eleven = ten + one (Óia'k énska), twelve = ten + two (Óia'k tékeni). For tens: twenty = two tens (Tékeni óia'k), fifty = five tens (Wísk óia'k). One hundred is a single word: Tewá:sen.
This system is completely regular — no irregular numbers (compare English 'eleven', 'twelve', 'thirteen'). Once you know 1–10, you can construct every number to 100.
lessons.ped: Transparent number systems: Kanien'kéha's base-10 additive system is more transparent than English (which has irregular forms from 11–19).
