exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! [Noun] ne ní:'i: that is the [noun]. You can build this sentence with any noun you know.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the naming sentence: 'That's the bear'
exercises.correct: ní:'i
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i — that's the bear. [Noun] ne ní:'i is the naming sentence. Use it to point at anything and name it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what word points to the thing being named?

exercises.prompt: Say 'that's the turtle' in Kanien'kéha
exercises.correct: A'nó:wara ne ní:'i
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! A'nó:wara ne ní:'i — that's the turtle. And of course — A'nó:wara is also the Turtle Clan. When you name the turtle, you name the clan.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Pattern: [noun] ne ní:'i

exercises.prompt: Say this naming sentence aloud
exercises.hint: oh-KWÁ:-ri (3 syllables) — ne (1) — NÍ:-'i (2). Point at something when you say ní:'i. The gesture and the word work together.

exercises.prompt: Naming the world
exercises.storyPrompt: Onón:ta ne ní:'i — that's the corn. One of the Three Sisters. When you point at an ear of corn and say its name in Kanien'kéha, you are doing something that has been done in this territory for thousands of years. The corn has a name. The beans have a name. The squash has a name. Every plant in the garden, every animal on the land — they all have names in this language. You are learning to say those names.

lessons.title: Lesson 73 — Describing Things (The Bear Is Big)
lessons.sub: How to say what something is like
intro: Kanien'kéha doesn't have adjectives the way English does. Instead, describing words are verbs — they describe a state something is in.
So instead of 'the bear is big' with an adjective, you say something closer to 'the bear — it is being big'. The describing word is a verb that expresses a state.

You have already seen many of these: owá:nen (it is big/large), ohsì:io (it is good/beautiful), kenní:io (it is good/beautiful), skén:nen (it is calm/peaceful). This lesson puts them to work in full sentences.
lessons.ped: Description through state verbs: This lesson names the pattern plainly — 'describing words are verbs here' — without requiring learners to understand technical terms. Nation (2001) identifies this kind of structural awareness as important for moving beyond vocabulary lists into sentence production. LaFever's Mental domain: understanding how description works. Physical domain: producing description sentences.

exercises.prompt: Owá:nen means 'it is big / large'. To say 'the bear is big', you say: Ohkwá:ri owá:nen. What is owá:nen doing in this sentence?
exercises.correct: It is the describing verb — it says what state the bear is in
options: It is the describing verb — it says what state the bear is in
It is a separate describing word added after the noun
It means 'the big bear' as one word
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen: the bear — it is big. Owá:nen is a describing verb telling you the bear's state. No separate adjective needed — the verb does the describing.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the describing verb to what it means
exercises.sub: You have seen all of these before
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four describing verbs from earlier units. Each one describes a state. Pair any of them with a noun and you have a complete describing sentence.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Build this sentence: 'The bear is big'
exercises.correct: owá:nen
exercises.hint: o
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen — the bear is big. Noun + describing verb. A complete Kanien'kéha sentence.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what describing verb means 'it is big'?

exercises.prompt: Kenní:io means 'it is good / it is beautiful'. How do you say 'the corn is good'?
exercises.correct: Onón:ta kenní:io
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Onón:ta kenní:io — the corn is good. Noun first, then the describing verb. The same pattern you just practised.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun + describing verb.

exercises.prompt: Say 'the wolf is calm' in Kanien'kéha
exercises.correct: Okwáho skén:nen
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Okwáho skén:nen — the wolf is calm. And skén:nen also means peace — so you could hear this as 'the wolf is at peace'. The Wolf Clan carries the responsibility of keeping peace between nations.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Pattern: [noun] + [describing verb]

exercises.prompt: Say this describing sentence aloud
exercises.hint: o-NÓN-ta (3 syllables) — oh-SÌ:-io (3 syllables). A simple, complete sentence. You can say this at a meal, in a garden, at a ceremony.

exercises.prompt: Skén:nen
exercises.storyPrompt: Skén:nen — peaceful, calm. This word appears in greetings, in the names of leaders, in the Great Law of Peace. When you describe something as skén:nen, you are using a word that has been at the centre of Haudenosaunee life for centuries. Peace is not just the absence of conflict — it is a state of balance and right relationship. What in your life right now is skén:nen?

lessons.title: Lesson 74 — There Is a Bear (Saying Something Exists)
lessons.sub: How to say that something is present or exists
intro: Sometimes you want to say that something is simply there — 'there is a bear', 'there are geese', 'there is water'. In Kanien'kéha, the phrase E'tho ká:yen — 'it is there' — is a key building block.
Combined with a noun, it says: that thing is present, right there. Ohkwá:ri — e'tho ká:yen — the bear is there / there is a bear.
This is the kind of sentence you say when you spot something — an animal, a place, food ready on the table.
lessons.ped: Existence sentences as functional vocabulary: The ability to assert that something exists underlies animal sightings, food descriptions, weather reports, and more. Nation (2001) identifies high-frequency functional patterns as priority acquisition targets. The lesson uses culturally relevant content — animals, land, water. LaFever's Spiritual domain is touched in the story exercise connecting existence sentences to the land.

exercises.prompt: E'tho ká:yen means 'it is there'. What does this build when you add a noun in front?
exercises.correct: An existence sentence — 'there is a [thing]' or '[thing] is there'
options: An existence sentence — 'there is a [thing]' or '[thing] is there'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! E'tho ká:yen — 'it is there'. Add a noun: Ohkwá:ri — e'tho ká:yen: the bear is there. Simple and complete.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the existence sentence to what it means

pairs.english: The bear is there / There is a bear
The beaver is there / There is a beaver
The water is there / There is water
The river is there / There is a river

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! [Noun] — e'tho ká:yen. A bear, a beaver, water, a river — you can announce the presence of any of them with this pattern.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Build an existence sentence: 'There is water'
exercises.correct: Ónhka
exercises.hint: Ó
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ónhka — e'tho ká:yen: there is water. Ónhka is from Unit 3 and also appears in the Thanksgiving Address — the waters that sustain us.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the Kanien'kéha word for water?

exercises.prompt: Kaniatarà:ke means 'river / on the river'. It already ends in -ke. What does that ending do?
exercises.sub: You learned this in Unit 12
exercises.correct: It is a locative ending — it means 'at' or 'on' that place
options: It is a locative ending — it means 'at' or 'on' that place
It is only used in place names
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The -ke ending is a locative — it builds 'at' or 'on' into the name itself. Kaniatarà:ke means 'on the river'. The place name tells you where you are standing.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does the -ke ending do?

exercises.prompt: Say this existence sentence aloud
exercises.meaning: The beaver is there / There is a beaver
exercises.hint: Tsa-NÍ:-to (3 syllables) — pause — e'-tho KÁ:-yen (4 syllables). Say it like you've just spotted something — because that's exactly when you'd use it.

exercises.prompt: Noticing what is there
exercises.storyPrompt: Tsaní:to — e'tho ká:yen. The beaver is there. Before there were roads and fences, the people knew the land by what lived in it — where the beaver dams were, where the deer came to drink, where the geese landed in autumn. To say 'the beaver is there' in Kanien'kéha is to read the land the way generations read it before you. What is there, in the land around you right now, that you could name in Kanien'kéha?

lessons.title: Lesson 75 — Where Is It? (Location Sentences)
lessons.sub: The bear is at the river — putting things in place
intro: A location sentence puts something in a specific place: the bear is at the river, the canoe is on the water, the drum is in the longhouse.

In Kanien'kéha, the word tsi — meaning 'where / at' — connects a noun to its location. You have seen it in phrases like tsi niakonónhsoten (where I live). Now you use it to place things and people on the land.

Many location words already carry their 'at' meaning built in — words like Kaniatarà:ke (at the river), Ohnekánoron (on the lake), kanonhséhsne (at the longhouse). These are not just names — they are places.
lessons.ped: Locative vocabulary as land-based learning: This lesson teaches location sentences through concrete places in Kanien'kehá:ka territory — the river, the lake, the longhouse, the field. Every location word is also a description of the land. LaFever's Spiritual domain: the land has language. Physical domain: learners produce location sentences about real places.

exercises.prompt: Tsi means 'where / at'. In the sentence 'Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke' (The bear is at the river), what is tsi doing?
exercises.correct: It connects the bear to its location — 'the bear, at the river'
options: It connects the bear to its location — 'the bear, at the river'
It is an article like ne
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsi links the subject to its place. Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke: the bear, at the river.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the location word to where it puts you
exercises.sub: These are real places on Kanien'kehá:ka territory

pairs.english: At the river / on the river
On the water / on the lake
In the field / on the meadow

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four places on Kanien'kehá:ka territory. These are real — not invented examples.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Build this location sentence: 'The bear is at the river'
exercises.correct: Kaniatarà:ke
exercises.hint: K
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke — the bear is at the river.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the word for 'at the river'?

exercises.prompt: Say 'the canoe is on the water' in Kanien'kéha
exercises.correct: Kaná:tsihs tsi Ohnekánoron
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kaná:tsihs tsi Ohnekánoron — the canoe on the water. Kaná:tsihs from Unit 3 now has a home in a full sentence.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Pattern: [noun] tsi [location]

exercises.prompt: Ohnekánoron means 'on the water / on the lake'. You have seen a water root before in this course. Where?
exercises.correct: Ónhka (water, Unit 3) — the same water root appears in different water words
options: Ónhka (water, Unit 3) — the same water root appears in different water words
Only in the word for rain
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The water root runs through many Kanien'kéha words. When you know a root, you start seeing it in new words — and new words become readable.
exercises.wm: Please try again — where have you seen a water word before?

exercises.prompt: Say this location sentence aloud
exercises.meaning: The bear is at the river
exercises.hint: oh-KWÁ:-ri (3) — tsi (1) — Ka-nia-ta-RÀ:-ke (5). The grave accent on rà: means a falling pitch. A full sentence in six syllables.

exercises.prompt: Putting things in their place
exercises.storyPrompt: Kaniatarà:ke — at the river. Rivers are at the centre of Kanien'kehá:ka life — for travel, for food, for ceremony. The St. Lawrence River, the Ottawa River, the Grand River — these are not just geographic features. They are relatives, acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address. When you say 'the bear is at the river', you are placing a living being alongside another living being. The river is not just a backdrop.

lessons.title: Lesson 76 — Whose Is It? (Possession Sentences)
lessons.sub: That is my shoe — saying who something belongs to
intro: Possession in Kanien'kéha is built into the noun through prefixes. You have already been using this — iakóha (my mother), Ónia'karé:'a (my clan). The possessive is part of the word.
To say 'that is my shoe', you take the possessed form of the noun and use the naming sentence from Lesson 72. Ak-áhta' — my shoe. Ak-áhta' ne ní:'i — that is my shoe.
Ak- marks 'my' on most nouns. Sa- marks 'your'. These two prefixes cover most of the possession sentences you will need in daily life.
lessons.ped: Possession as practical grammar: Possessive constructions are among the highest-frequency structures in daily conversation. Nation (2001) identifies high-frequency, high-utility structures as priority targets. The lesson draws on familiar nouns so learners focus on the new possessive prefix rather than new vocabulary. Emotional domain: possession is also the language of home and belonging.

exercises.prompt: Áhta' means 'shoe'. To say 'my shoe', you add ak- to get Ak-áhta'. How does 'that is my shoe' look as a full sentence?
exercises.correct: Ak-áhta' ne ní:'i
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ak-áhta' ne ní:'i — that is my shoe. The possessed noun + ne ní:'i: the same naming pattern from Lesson 72, now with a possessive prefix on the noun.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the naming sentence pattern?

exercises.prompt: Match the possessed item to its meaning
exercises.sub: Ak- marks 'my' on these nouns
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ak- + [noun] = my [noun]. The prefix attaches to the front of the noun.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the possession sentence: 'That is my soap'
exercises.correct: ní:'i
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ak-atsihnhétste' ne ní:'i — that is my soap. The same naming sentence ending, now with a possessed noun.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what word ends a naming sentence?

exercises.prompt: To say 'that is your book', you use sa- (your) instead of ak- (my). How would you say it?
exercises.sub: Apply the same pattern with sa-
exercises.correct: Sa-kahiatónhsera ne ní:'i
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Sa-kahiatónhsera ne ní:'i — that is your book. Ak- = my, Sa- = your. Two prefixes, two people, the same sentence pattern.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which prefix marks 'your'?

exercises.prompt: Say this possession sentence aloud
exercises.hint: ak-ÁH-ta' (3 syllables) — ne (1) — NÍ:-'i (2). The ak- prefix is quick and unstressed. The stress falls on AH in the noun.

exercises.prompt: What belongs to you
exercises.storyPrompt: Ak-áhta' ne ní:'i — that is my shoe. Such a small sentence. But the word 'mine' in any language carries a lot. In residential schools, children's clothes were taken and replaced with uniforms. Personal belongings were not allowed. To say 'my shoe' in Kanien'kéha is to claim something. Small, ordinary, yours.

lessons.title: Lesson 77 — Saying No (Negative Sentences)
lessons.sub: Iáh — the word for 'not' — and how to use it
intro: You learned Iáh — no / not — in Unit 3. This lesson takes it further: how do you use Iáh to make any sentence negative?

Iáh comes before the verb or describing word. Sometimes it also triggers a small change in the verb — often you will see a te- added. You have already seen this in phrases you know: Iáh tó:kens (not so well), Iáh waké:ka' (I don't like it), Yah tewake'nikonhrayén:ta's (I don't understand).
Yah is the same as Iáh — just a common spoken version. Both are correct.
lessons.ped: Negation as high-frequency grammar: The ability to say 'no', 'not', and 'I don't' is essential from the earliest stages. Nation (2001) identifies negation as a priority structure. This lesson builds on Iáh, which learners have encountered repeatedly since Unit 3, making the pattern explicit without technical grammar terms.

exercises.prompt: You know Iáh means 'no / not'. In Iáh tó:kens (not so well), what is Iáh doing?
exercises.correct: It flips the meaning — turning 'well' into 'not well'
options: It flips the meaning — turning 'well' into 'not well'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iáh flips the meaning. Tó:kens — well. Iáh tó:kens — not well. Iáh is the switch.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the negative phrase to what it means
exercises.sub: You have seen all of these before
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four negative phrases from earlier units — now you can see the pattern they share. Iáh / Yah comes first, and often a te- appears in the verb after it.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'I don't understand'
exercises.correct: Yah
exercises.hint: Y
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Yah tewake'nikonhrayén:ta's — I don't understand. Yah and Iáh are both correct — Yah is common in everyday speech.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what word starts a negative sentence?

exercises.prompt: Iah ka'niká:ien means 'never'. What two parts do you see in it?
exercises.correct: Iah (not) + ka'niká:ien (ever) — not + ever = never
options: It is a single word with no parts
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Iah ka'niká:ien — not ever = never. Kanien'kéha builds complex meanings from smaller pieces. When you can see the pieces, longer expressions become readable.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does Iah always do?

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I don't understand'?
exercises.correct: Yah tewake'nikonhrayén:ta's
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Yah tewake'nikonhrayén:ta's — I don't understand. This is one of the most useful phrases you can know. No one expects a learner to understand everything. Say it, and a speaker will help you.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this negative phrase aloud
exercises.hint: IÁH (1 syllable) — wa-KÉ:-ka' (3 syllables). Short, clear, direct. Iáh lands firmly — it is the word of refusal and negation.

exercises.prompt: The power of no
exercises.storyPrompt: Iáh — no. Such a small word. The ability to say no in your own language — no, I don't agree; no, that's not right; no, I don't understand; no, that is not mine — is not small. In residential schools, Indigenous children were not allowed to say no in their own language. Reclaiming Iáh is reclaiming the right to refuse, to disagree, to draw a line, in the language that belongs to you.

lessons.title: Lesson 78 — Yes or No? (Asking Simple Questions)
lessons.sub: How to turn a statement into a yes/no question
intro: The simplest way to turn a statement into a yes/no question in Kanien'kéha is to add the word ken — often translated as 'is it?' or 'right?'.
You have already seen ken in action: Satonhkaria'ks ken? (Are you hungry?), Iawekon ken? (Is it delicious?). Ken sits after the verb and raises the question.
The answers are simple: Hen' — yes. Iáh — no. These two words, with ken questions, cover a huge range of everyday exchanges.
lessons.ped: Yes/no questions as immediate communicative function: The ability to ask and answer yes/no questions enables participation in conversation at the earliest stages. Nation (2001) identifies question formation as a priority structure. This lesson is deliberately short and focused: one pattern (ken), two answers (Hen' / Iáh), practised through familiar vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: What is Hen'?
exercises.correct: Yes — the affirmative answer
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Hen' — yes. Hen' and Iáh are the two answers to every ken question.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the ken question to what it is asking
exercises.sub: You have seen all of these before

pairs.english: Is it wonderful? / Is it pleasing?

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Remove ken and you have a statement. Add ken and you have a question. That's the whole rule.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Turn this into a yes/no question: 'Are you hungry?'
exercises.correct: ken
exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Satonhkaria'ks ken? — Are you hungry? Ken turns any statement into a yes/no question.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what word makes it a question?

exercises.prompt: Someone asks you Iawekon ken? (Is it delicious?). You think it is. What do you say?
exercises.correct: Hen' — yes
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Hen' — yes. Simple and satisfying.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this question and answer aloud
exercises.sub: Both sides of the exchange
exercises.hint: Sa-ton-hka-RIA'-ks ken? — rising at the end. Hen' — one clear syllable. Practise both sides — you will ask and answer.

exercises.prompt: Yes
exercises.storyPrompt: Hen' — yes. The simplest response in the language. And yet — to say yes in Kanien'kéha, to a question asked in Kanien'kéha, is to participate. It is to be in the conversation, not outside it. Every time you answer Hen' to a question in this language, you are one step further inside. What question would you most like to be able to say yes to, in Kanien'kéha?

lessons.title: Lesson 79 — Who? What? Where? When? (Information Questions)
lessons.sub: The six question words you need to ask about anything
intro: Yes/no questions get you a yes or a no. Information questions get you an answer. This lesson covers the main question words in Kanien'kéha.
You have seen most of these scattered across the course. This lesson brings them together so you can see them as a set and use them with confidence.
Ónhka — who. Nahò:ten — what. Ka' — where. Kátke — when. Oh nontyé:ren — why. To nihá:ti — how many. Six question words that open almost any conversation.
lessons.ped: Question words as conversational keys: Nation (2001) identifies question words as high-frequency vocabulary that must be taught as a set — they form a paradigm together. This lesson presents them together for exactly that reason, using already-known content vocabulary so the focus stays on the question words themselves.

exercises.prompt: Match the question word to what it asks
exercises.sub: Learn these six together — they go everywhere
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four of the six question words. Ónhka (who), Nahò:ten (what), Ka' (where), Kátke (when). These four will carry most of your information questions.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: The last two question words
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Oh nontyé:ren (why) and To nihá:ti (how many) complete the set. Now you have all six.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Nahò:ten means 'what'. You have seen it in Nahò:ten katkáhthos? (What do you see?). Which food question also uses Nahò:ten?
exercises.correct: Naho:ten ni:se' enseke? — What will you eat?
options: Naho:ten ni:se' enseke? — What will you eat?
Kanié:ri iken'? — What is your name?
Ka' wáhse'? — Where are you going?
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Nahò:ten turns up in many question frames. It is one of the busiest question words in the language.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: To nihá:ti means 'how many'. Which question in Unit 12 also asked about a number?
exercises.correct: Kahiatónhkwa tsi ní:se'? — How many winters are you?
options: Kahiatónhkwa tsi ní:se'? — How many winters are you?
Kanié:ri iken'? — What is your name?
Ka' nontáhse'? — Where did you come from?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kahiatónhkwa carries the 'how many' meaning in that age question. To nihá:ti is the standalone 'how many' you can use before any noun.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: What is the Kanien'kéha question word for 'where'?
exercises.correct: Ka'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ka' — where. Short and common. You have seen it in Ka' wáhse'? and Ka' nontáhse'? since Unit 2.
exercises.wm: Please try again — a short word, just two characters.

exercises.prompt: Say the six question words aloud — one at a time
exercises.word: Ónhka — Nahò:ten — Ka' — Kátke — Oh nontyé:ren — To nihá:ti
exercises.meaning: Who — What — Where — When — Why — How many
exercises.hint: Say each one clearly with a pause between. ÓN-hka. Na-HÒ:-ten. Ka'. KÁT-ke. Oh non-TYÉ-ren. To ni-HÁ:-ti. These six words open every conversation.

exercises.prompt: The questions that matter
exercises.storyPrompt: Ónhka — who? This is always the first question. Who are you? Who are these people? Who came before? In Kanien'kehá:ka life, knowing who someone is — their name, their clan, their community, their family — is the foundation of every relationship. Ónhka is not a small question. It is an invitation. Who are you?

lessons.title: Lesson 80 — Putting Weight on a Word
lessons.sub: How to stress what matters most in a sentence
intro: Sometimes you want to put extra weight on part of a sentence — not just 'the bear is there' but 'the bear — yes, right there — is there'. Or not just 'I am going' but 'I am the one going'.

Kanien'kéha does this with small particles you have already met: Wáhi (truly / so it is), ki' (just / only / already), and ón:ne' (it's done / finished). These little words shift what the listener pays attention to.
You have said Onen' ki' wáhi at the end of every unit. Now you know what each piece means and what it is doing.
lessons.ped: Emphasis and focus as communicative precision: This lesson keeps the content familiar while teaching a new function — adding weight and focus. Nation (2001) recommends this kind of functional extension of known vocabulary. LaFever's Physical domain: learners practise producing focused sentences. Emotional domain: emphasis is the language of what matters most.

exercises.prompt: Ki' means 'just / only / already'. In 'Ohkwá:ri ki' ne ní:'i' (It's just the bear — only the bear), what is ki' adding?
exercises.correct: It narrows the focus — 'only this, nothing else'
options: It narrows the focus — 'only this, nothing else'
It makes the sentence a question
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ki' narrows: just the bear, only the bear, nothing more. You have seen ki' in Onen' ki' wáhi — 'that's all, nothing more'. The same narrowing at work.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Wáhi means 'truly / so it is'. What does adding Wáhi to a sentence do?
exercises.correct: It adds weight and certainty — 'it is truly so'
options: It adds weight and certainty — 'it is truly so'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Wáhi confirms and closes. Onen' ki' wáhi — 'now that's truly all'. Wáhi gives a sentence finality.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the particle to what it does

pairs.english: Just / only — narrows to one thing
Okay then — closes or accepts something

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Four small particles that make speech sound natural. They are the difference between a word list and a real sentence.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ioiá:ner means 'it is good / right'. To say 'it is truly good', which particle do you add?
exercises.correct: Wáhi — Ioiá:ner wáhi — it is truly good
options: Wáhi — Ioiá:ner wáhi — it is truly good
Iáh — Iáh ioiá:ner — it is not good
ki' — Ioiá:ner ki' — it is only good
ken — Ioiá:ner ken — is it good?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ioiá:ner wáhi — it is truly good. Wáhi strengthens and confirms.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which particle adds certainty?

exercises.prompt: Say this phrase aloud — and mean it
exercises.meaning: Now that's truly all / I am finished
exercises.hint: O-nen' (2) — ki' (1) — WÁ-hi (2). Three particles working together: now (onen') + just/only (ki') + truly (wáhi). You have said this many times. Now you understand every piece of it.

exercises.prompt: Finishing
exercises.storyPrompt: ón:ne' — it's done. There is something satisfying about a word for completion. In a language that was silenced for generations, every finished lesson is its own small ón:ne' — done, completed, real. You said it. It happened. The language moved through you. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Lesson 81 — Sentences About the World Around You
lessons.sub: Using simple sentences about what you can see right now
intro: Every lesson in this unit has given you a sentence type. This lesson asks you to use all of them — naming, describing, locating, possessing, questioning, negating — about the world right in front of you.
Look around where you are. What do you see? What is big? What is there? What is yours? Can you ask a question about it in Kanien'kéha?
This is not a test. It is an invitation to use what you know with real things. That is how a language becomes yours.
lessons.ped: Transfer to real-world use: Nation (2001) identifies the move from practised production to real use as the final step in acquisition. This lesson moves away from controlled exercises toward open-ended production — using sentence types with the learner's immediate environment. LaFever's four domains converge: knowing the patterns (Mental), using them with real objects (Physical), connecting language to the learner's own life (Emotional), understanding this as a step in the relationship with the language (Spiritual).

exercises.prompt: You want to say 'that is a bear'. Which sentence pattern do you use?
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i — the naming pattern
options: Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i — the naming pattern
Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke — the location pattern
Ohkwá:ri — e'tho ká:yen — the existence pattern
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i — naming. All four are correct Kanien'kéha sentences — but each does a different job.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which pattern names a thing?

exercises.prompt: You want to say 'the corn is there'. Which pattern?
exercises.correct: Onón:ta — e'tho ká:yen — the existence pattern
options: Onón:ta — e'tho ká:yen — the existence pattern
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Onón:ta — e'tho ká:yen. E'tho ká:yen announces that something is present — right there.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which pattern announces that something is there?

exercises.prompt: Match each sentence to its type
exercises.sub: Five sentence types from this unit
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Possession, negation, question, description — four different tools. A sentence is just a tool. You choose which one you need.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Look out a window or step outside. Say what you see — in Kanien'kéha.
exercises.sub: Use any sentence pattern you know
exercises.hint: Name what you see. Describe it. Say where it is. Say if it is big or small, good or old. Ask yourself a question about it. Any sentence you make — in any pattern — is real Kanien'kéha.

exercises.prompt: Saying yes to the language
exercises.storyPrompt: Hen' — yes. You can now build simple sentences in Kanien'kéha. You can name things, describe them, say where they are, say whose they are, ask about them, say no to them. These are not just exercises. They are the tools you need to speak. To be in conversation. To be present in the language. You started without knowing a single Kanien'kéha sentence. Look at where you are now. Hen' — yes. You are here.

lessons.title: Lesson 82 — Reading a Short Kanien'kéha Text
lessons.sub: A few sentences together — reading and understanding
intro: You have been building sentences one at a time. This lesson puts several together into a short text — a few sentences you can read, understand, and respond to.
The text uses only words and sentence patterns from this course. You will not find anything you haven't seen before. What you will find is all of it working together.
Reading Kanien'kéha — seeing it on the page and understanding it — is its own skill. This lesson gives you your first real reading practice.
lessons.ped: Reading as integration: Nation (2001) identifies reading as the activity that most consolidates vocabulary — encountering known words in new combinations, in connected text rather than isolated exercises. This is the unit's integration point. LaFever's Mental domain: reading and comprehension. Physical domain: responding to comprehension questions.

exercises.prompt:
Read this and answer the question:

'Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke. Ohkwá:ri owá:nen. Ohkwá:ri — e'tho ká:yen.'

Where is the bear?
exercises.correct: At the river — Kaniatarà:ke
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke — the bear is at the river. You read a Kanien'kéha sentence and understood it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look for the location word.

exercises.prompt: From the same text: 'Ohkwá:ri owá:nen.' What does this tell you about the bear?
exercises.correct: The bear is big / large
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen — the bear is big. A describing sentence. You read it and understood it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does owá:nen mean?

exercises.prompt:
Read this and answer:

'Onón:ta — e'tho ká:yen. Ohsahéta' — e'tho ká:yen. Otá:ra — e'tho ká:yen.'

What are these three sentences about?
exercises.correct: The Three Sisters — corn, beans, and squash are all present
options: The Three Sisters — corn, beans, and squash are all present
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Three existence sentences, three sisters: Onón:ta (corn), Ohsahéta' (beans), Otá:ra (squash). They are there. You read three complete Kanien'kéha sentences.
exercises.wm: Please try again — you know these three nouns from Unit 7.

exercises.prompt: One more sentence: 'Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — ohsì:io.' What does this mean?
exercises.sub: You know both of these
exercises.correct: The Thanksgiving Address is good / beautiful
options: The Thanksgiving Address is good / beautiful
The Thanksgiving Address — where is it?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — ohsì:io. You can now read and understand a sentence about the Thanksgiving Address — in Kanien'kéha.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does ohsì:io mean?

exercises.prompt: Read this short text aloud
exercises.sub: Take your time — read for meaning, not speed
exercises.word: Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke. Ohkwá:ri owá:nen. Onón:ta — e'tho ká:yen. Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — ohsì:io.
exercises.meaning: The bear is at the river. The bear is big. The corn is there. The Thanksgiving Address is good.
exercises.hint: Four sentences. Four patterns — location, description, existence, description. Say each one clearly. You are reading Kanien'kéha.

exercises.prompt: Reading the language
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. You just read a Kanien'kéha text. Maybe you stumbled on a word, maybe you had to check something. That is exactly what reading a new language feels like. You did it anyway. The bear was at the river. The corn was there. The Thanksgiving Address was good. These things were true in Kanien'kéha, and you understood them. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 13 — The Body in Motion: Daily Routine
desc: Waking, washing, dressing, eating, going, returning, resting — a full day in Kanien'kéha, using A-stem verbs, noun incorporation, and time expressions

lessons.title: Lesson 58 — Waking Up: The Morning Begins
lessons.sub: Time-of-day vocabulary and the verbs of morning
intro: Every day begins the same way. This lesson gives you the language for that beginning: the word for morning, the verb for waking, and the time expressions that frame the first part of the day.
Ohron'ké:ne — in the morning. Onia'karéhson — daytime. These are not just clock-words — in Haudenosaunee life, the morning has a specific ceremonial weight. The day begins with acknowledgement.
You will also meet the particle Onen' (now / so then) in its role as a temporal opener — the word that starts a sequence of events. It appears at the beginning of stories and instructions alike.
lessons.ped: Time vocabulary as daily-life grounding: Freire (1970) argued that learning begins with the generative content of the learner's daily world. Morning is the most universal daily threshold — it is where every day's language use begins. Nation (2001) notes that time-of-day words are high-frequency and highly reusable; introducing them early in a daily-routine unit means they will recur across every subsequent lesson. LaFever's Physical domain is activated through production of time expressions; the Spiritual domain is present in the framing of morning as a threshold of acknowledgement — the Thanksgiving Address is traditionally spoken at the start of each day.

exercises.prompt: What does ohron'ké:ne mean?
exercises.correct: In the morning / Morning (time of day)
options: In the morning / Morning (time of day)
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ohron'ké:ne — in the morning. The -ké:ne ending is a locative-temporal suffix marking 'at that time'. You will see it again in other time expressions.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the time-of-day words
exercises.sub: You will use all of these across this unit
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four time-of-day words that will frame every lesson in this unit. Morning → daytime → noon → evening → night — the daily arc in Kanien'kéha.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Enhnísera means 'a day'. Which particle from Unit 9 combines with this to mean 'today'?
exercises.sub: Think about what makes 'a day' into 'this day'
exercises.correct: onkwa'nikón:ra — today (lit. 'this day that holds us')
options: onkwa'nikón:ra — today (lit. 'this day that holds us')
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! onkwa'nikón:ra — today. The root -nikón:ra relates to the mind or spirit; combined with the temporal frame it gives the sense of 'this present day'. You will use it throughout this unit to anchor daily actions in the present.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what makes a day 'today'?

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address is traditionally spoken at the start of each day. What does this tell you about the Haudenosaunee relationship to morning?
exercises.sub: Connect language to worldview
exercises.correct: Morning is a threshold of acknowledgement — the day begins with gratitude and relationship, not with individual routine
options: Morning is a threshold of acknowledgement — the day begins with gratitude and relationship, not with individual routine
The Address is only spoken on ceremonial days
The Address is spoken at night
exercises.cm: Tho niioht. McCarty & Lee (2014) argue that language learning must connect to the living cultural practices of a community. The Thanksgiving Address — Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — orients the speaker to the world before the day's work begins. Learning 'morning' in Kanien'kéha means learning a morning that has this weight.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say 'in the morning' aloud
exercises.hint: oh-ron'-KÉ:-ne — four syllables. The glottal stop after ron' is a brief throat closure. The long vowel on KÉ: is held. Stress falls on kéː.

exercises.prompt: Your morning
exercises.storyPrompt: ohron'ké:ne — in the morning. Before the first coffee, before the phone, before the list of things to do — there is a morning. The Thanksgiving Address begins by acknowledging the people, the earth, the waters, the plants, the animals, the winds, the sun, the moon, the stars, the spiritual powers. This is a morning practice that predates every morning routine you have ever had. What would it mean to begin your morning with acknowledgement rather than urgency?

lessons.title: Lesson 59 — Washing & Grooming
lessons.sub: Noun-incorporated verbs: brush teeth, wash hands, wash face
intro: Some of the most important verbs in Kanien'kéha are noun-incorporated — the body part or object is built directly into the verb. 'Brush teeth' is not two words in Kanien'kéha; it is one verb with the tooth root incorporated.
You learned body parts in Unit 4 and the concept of noun incorporation in Unit 3. This lesson puts them together in action: grooming verbs that are already in the glossary as full forms.

Satkahnekárhons — you brush your teeth (habitual). Sato'nnenhstáhkwa's — you wash your hands (habitual). Each of these is a single verb containing a body part root. This is how Kanien'kéha handles actions on the body.
lessons.ped: Noun incorporation as productive grammar: Mithun (1984) identifies noun incorporation as a core feature of polysynthetic language — it is not an exception but a primary word-formation strategy. Introducing grooming verbs as the practise ground for incorporation follows Krashen's +1 principle: learners already know the body part vocabulary (Unit 4) and the concept (Unit 3); this lesson adds the new pattern of seeing them fused into verbs. Nation (2001) notes that morphologically complex words are best learned in semantic clusters — all body-action verbs together, so the pattern is visible. LaFever's Physical domain is primary (these are production verbs); the Emotional domain is present in the routine as self-care.

exercises.prompt: Satkahnekárhons — you brush your teeth (habitual). Which body part from Unit 4 is incorporated into this verb?
exercises.sub: Look at the verb root
exercises.correct: Onékahrha — teeth (the root -kahnekárh- comes from the teeth root)
options: Onékahrha — teeth (the root -kahnekárh- comes from the teeth root)
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The verb satkahnekárhons contains the teeth root -kahnekárh-. The body part has been incorporated directly into the verb. You do not need a separate word for 'teeth' — it is inside the verb already.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which body part is about brushing?

exercises.prompt: Sato'nnenhstáhkwa's — you wash your hands (habitual). Which body part is incorporated?
exercises.correct: Onenhstà:'a — hand / hands (the root -nnenhstáhkwa- contains the hand root)
options: Onenhstà:'a — hand / hands (the root -nnenhstáhkwa- contains the hand root)
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The hand root -nnenhst- is visible inside the verb. Kanien'kéha speakers hear the body part in the verb automatically — as you learn more incorporated verbs, you will too.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what do you wash?

exercises.prompt: Match the grooming verb to what it describes
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Verb and tool paired together — the brushing verb and the toothbrush, the washing verb and the hand. Vocabulary lives in clusters, not in isolation.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Both satkahnekárhons and sato'nnenhstáhkwa's are in the habitual form. What does the habitual tell you about these actions?
exercises.sub: Think about why grooming verbs are typically habitual
exercises.correct: They are regular, repeated actions — things you do every day, as a matter of routine
options: They are regular, repeated actions — things you do every day, as a matter of routine
They only happened once in the past
They are commands to someone else
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The habitual (-s suffix on A-stem verbs) expresses regular, repeated action. Daily routines — brushing, washing, dressing — are almost always habitual in Kanien'kéha. You do them every day; the habitual form encodes that rhythm.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does habitual aspect tell you about frequency?

exercises.prompt: Say this habitual verb aloud
exercises.hint: sat-kah-ne-KÁR-hons — five syllables. The stress falls on KÁR. The -s at the end is the habitual suffix — it is quiet but essential.

exercises.prompt: The body in language
exercises.storyPrompt: sato'nnenhstáhkwa's — you wash your hands. The hand is inside the verb. In Kanien'kéha, the body is not separate from the action — the body and what it does are one grammatical unit. This is not just a linguistic observation. Haudenosaunee relationships to the body, to health, to daily care are relational and communal, not merely individual. Whose hands are you washing? Who washed your hands when you were small? Who will you wash hands alongside?

lessons.title: Lesson 60 — Getting Dressed
lessons.sub: Clothing vocabulary in possessive form; the verb of putting on
intro: Getting dressed draws on two things you already know: the A-class clothing nouns from Unit 3 (áhta' — shoes, atháhsteren — pants, atià:tawi — shirt), and the verb énha — to put on clothing.

In Kanien'kéha, clothing vocabulary is rich with possessive prefixes — my shoes, your shirt, her dress. The possessive system you learned for kinship terms in Unit 2 extends to clothing and personal objects.
This lesson also introduces atéwennáhkhwa's — you button up / fasten (habitual) — as an example of a fine motor clothing verb. The pattern is the same: A-stem, habitual, self-directed action.
lessons.ped: Vocabulary recycling across semantic domains: Nation (2001) identifies productive reuse of known vocabulary in new contexts as a key consolidation strategy. Clothing nouns from Unit 3 reappear here in possessed form and in combination with new verbs. Krashen's +1 principle: the vocabulary is known; the new element is the possessive prefix on nouns and the specific clothing verbs. LaFever's Physical domain is primary — this is a production and daily-practice lesson. The Emotional domain is touched in the story exercise, which connects clothing to identity and community belonging.

exercises.prompt: Énha means 'to put on clothing'. It is an A-stem verb. What command form do you use to tell someone (you) to put on their clothes?
exercises.sub: Apply the A-stem command rule from Unit 3
exercises.correct: Sénha — put it on (A-stem command: s- prefix, no habitual -s)
options: Sénha — put it on (A-stem command: s- prefix, no habitual -s)
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! A-stem command: replace the initial vowel with s-. Sénha — put it on. This follows the exact same pattern as sé:ta (you put it in) from Unit 8. The stem class is consistent.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the A-stem command prefix?

exercises.prompt: Match the clothing item to its Kanien'kéha name
exercises.sub: These are from Unit 3 — you know them already
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! All clothing is A-class in Kanien'kéha — the class that includes personal items, things that are worn or carried. The class prefix is part of the word; the possessive prefix attaches before it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — these are from Unit 3.

exercises.prompt: Complete the habitual verb 'you button up / fasten'
exercises.correct: s
exercises.hint: s
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! satewennáhkhwa's — the final -s is the habitual suffix. It is quiet and easy to miss, but it marks the difference between 'you are fastening (right now)' and 'you fasten (as a habit)'. Daily routine verbs are almost always habitual.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what suffix marks habitual aspect on A-stem verbs?

exercises.prompt: atià:tawi is described as 'Fake A-Class'. What makes it 'fake' in the A-class system?
exercises.sub: Recall the true/fake distinction from Unit 3
exercises.correct: Fake A-class nouns behave like A-class words but have a slightly different internal structure — the 'fake' label is a grammatical category marker, not a value judgement
options: Fake A-class nouns behave like A-class words but have a slightly different internal structure — the 'fake' label is a grammatical category marker, not a value judgement
It is not a real Kanien'kéha word
It belongs to a different noun class entirely
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The true/fake distinction in Kanien'kéha noun classes is a grammatical category — 'fake' does not mean incorrect or less valid. Mithun (1982) documents this as a systematic morphological distinction. Both true and fake A-class nouns behave similarly in most contexts.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this verb aloud
exercises.hint: SÉN-ha — two syllables. Short and direct. The command form is crisp — no habitual -s at the end.

exercises.prompt: What you wear
exercises.storyPrompt: atsherón:nia' — clothing. What we wear is not neutral. Traditional Kanien'kehá:ka clothing — beaded moccasins, decorated garments, specific ceremonial dress — carries clan identity, community belonging, and ceremonial role. Residential schools stripped children of their clothing and put them in uniforms. Reclaiming traditional dress — like reclaiming language — is an assertion of presence. What do you put on in the morning that tells the world who you are?

lessons.title: Lesson 61 — Eating: Morning and Daily Food
lessons.sub: The verb of eating; food and hunger vocabulary
intro: Átskhwa' — to eat. This is one of the most important verbs in the language, and it connects directly to the food vocabulary you built across Units 7 and related lessons.

This lesson pairs the eating verb with hunger vocabulary (Katonhkaria'ks — I am hungry) and the food question (Nahò:ten ni:se' enseke? — What will you eat?). These are the three pieces of a meal conversation.
You will also encounter O:nen ni:'i wakekhwenta:on — I have already eaten — which introduces the completion particle onen' in a new context. Eating done is different from eating happening.
lessons.ped: Food as cultural knowledge: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) argue that food vocabulary in Indigenous languages carries land-based and relational knowledge — not just nutrition labels. The Three Sisters (corn, beans, squash) that anchor Haudenosaunee food culture were introduced in Unit 7; this lesson reactivates them in the context of daily meals. Nation (2001) identifies high-frequency vocabulary used in multiple contexts as the fastest-acquired — 'to eat' is a maximum-frequency verb. LaFever's Physical domain is primary; the Spiritual domain is present in the framing of food as relationship with the land.

exercises.prompt: Átskhwa' means 'to eat'. What is the habitual first-person form — 'I eat (regularly)'?
exercises.sub: Apply the A-stem habitual rule
exercises.correct: Wakátskhwa's — I eat (habitual) — the w- marks first person; -s marks habitual
options: Wakátskhwa's — I eat (habitual) — the w- marks first person; -s marks habitual
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wakátskhwa's — I eat (habitual). The w- prefix marks first person on O-stem and some A-stem verbs; -s marks the habitual. You eat every day — that regularity is what the habitual encodes.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which prefix marks first person here?

exercises.prompt: Katonhkaria'ks — I am hungry. This is a stative verb. What does 'stative' mean in this context?
exercises.sub: Recall the active/stative distinction from Unit 10
exercises.correct: It describes a state I am in, not an action I am performing — hunger is a condition, not an activity
options: It describes a state I am in, not an action I am performing — hunger is a condition, not an activity
It is in the past tense
It means I was hungry yesterday
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Katonhkaria'ks is stative — it describes a bodily state, not an activity. You are in the condition of hunger. This is exactly the active/stative distinction from Unit 10: you cannot choose to be hungry; it is a state that has come over you.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is a stative verb?

exercises.prompt: Match the eating phrase to its meaning
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! A complete meal conversation: Are you hungry? → I am hungry → What will you eat? → I have already eaten. These four phrases carry a full social exchange around food.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete 'I'm not hungry yet'
exercises.correct: ks
exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! I:iah sotsi arok tekatonhkaria'ks — not hungry yet. The te- before the verb is the dualic/negative prefix that combines with Iáh to create negation. The -ks ending is the stative marker.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in 'ks'.

exercises.prompt: Say this stative verb aloud
exercises.hint: Ka-ton-hka-RIA'-ks — five syllables. The glottal stop before -ks is part of the root. The stress falls on RIA'. This is a statement of bodily state — say it from the body, not just the mouth.

exercises.prompt: Food and relationship
exercises.storyPrompt: kákhwa — food. In Haudenosaunee culture, food is not just fuel. The Three Sisters — corn, beans, squash — were given by the Creator as gifts to the people, and the Green Corn Ceremony gives thanks for them every year. Sharing food is a relational act; feeding someone is an expression of care. When you eat in the morning, who grew what you are eating? Who prepared it? The language of eating is also the language of relationship and gratitude.

lessons.title: Lesson 62 — Going Out: Direction Verbs in Daily Context
lessons.sub: Where are you going? Going to school, work, and community places
intro: You learned direction verbs in Unit 2 and place vocabulary throughout the course. This lesson brings them together in the context of the daily routine: leaving the house, going to school or work, going to the community.

Wà:ke — I am going. Ka' wáhse'? — Where are you going? These are already in your vocabulary. Now you add the destinations: tsi yontaweya'táhkwa (school), tsi thatitsyénhayens (band office), Ohnikaieron'tò:ten (the village centre).

The direction verb pairs with a place name using the article thi — the article for things in motion. Thi appears when you go toward something; ne appears for stationary reference. This distinction matters.
lessons.ped: Direction verbs in community context: Freire (1970) argued that the places in a learner's daily world are the primary generative content of language instruction. This lesson deliberately uses Kanien'kehá:ka community places — the band office, the longhouse, the school — not generic 'store' or 'library'. Nation (2001) identifies collocations (direction verb + place) as particularly efficient learning units because they are retrieved together in real communication. LaFever's Physical domain is primary (going is movement); the Emotional domain is present in the choice of destinations, which are sites of community life.

exercises.prompt: Wà:ke means 'I am going (somewhere)'. It is a direction verb. What question does it naturally answer?
exercises.correct: Ka' wáhse'? — Where are you going?
options: Ka' wáhse'? — Where are you going?
Ka' nontáhse'? — Where did you come from?
Kanié:ri iken'? — What is your name?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ka' wáhse'? and Wà:ke' are the canonical question-answer pair for going. You learned them in Unit 2. This lesson puts them in a daily context: going out into the community.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which question asks where you are going?

exercises.prompt: When you say 'I am going to school', which article do you use with the school — ne or thi?
exercises.sub: Remember: thi marks things in motion / direction; ne marks things stationary
exercises.correct: thi — because you are moving toward the school, it is the destination of motion
options: thi — because you are moving toward the school, it is the destination of motion
ne — because the school is a fixed place
No article is used with places
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! thi is the article of direction and motion. When something is the destination of movement, thi marks it. Ne marks a specific thing in a stationary reference. You saw this distinction in Unit 3 — it reappears every time you name a destination.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which article marks direction?

exercises.prompt: Match the community place to its Kanien'kéha name
exercises.sub: These are real places in Kanien'kehá:ka communities
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! These are places in Kanien'kehá:ka communities — the school, the band office, the longhouse, the village centre. Learning their names is learning the geography of community life in Kanien'kéha.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the question 'Where are you going?'
exercises.correct: se'
exercises.hint: s
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ka' wáhse'? — Where are you going? The glottal stop (') at the end of wáhse' is part of the word — do not drop it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends with 'se'' including the glottal stop.

exercises.prompt: Say this direction verb aloud
exercises.hint: WÀ:-ke — two syllables. The grave accent on Wà marks a falling pitch. The long vowel on wà: is held. This is a short, quiet verb — say it the way you would say 'I'm heading out.'

exercises.prompt: Going to the longhouse
exercises.storyPrompt: kanonhséhsne — at the longhouse. The longhouse is not just a building. It is the political, ceremonial, and social centre of Kanien'kehá:ka life. Clan mothers meet there. Ceremonies are held there. The Great Law of Peace is kept there. When you say 'I am going to the longhouse', you are not just naming a destination — you are locating yourself in a living political tradition. Where do you go in your community that carries that kind of weight?

lessons.title: Lesson 63 — Coming Home
lessons.sub: Return direction verbs; where did you come from?
intro: Every going has a coming back. The direction verb system in Kanien'kéha encodes both: leaving and returning are different verbs, not the same verb with a different prefix.

Ka' nontáhse'? — Where did you come from? This question from Unit 2 reappears here in the daily routine context: someone comes home; someone asks where they have been. The answer draws on the same community places you learned in the last lesson.
This lesson also introduces the direction sentence pair from the glossary — Ka' nontáhse'? / Non nontà:ke — where did you come from? / I came from (there). The pair is a complete daily-life exchange.
lessons.ped: Direction verb pairs as daily grammar: Nation (2001) identifies paired vocabulary (question + answer, go + come) as efficient learning units because they are always encountered and used together. The direction verb system in Kanien'kéha is not large — but its going/coming distinction is grammatically significant and culturally relevant: you are always located relative to a home. Krashen's +1 principle: the vocabulary (direction verbs, place names) is known; the new element is the return direction in daily conversation context. LaFever's Emotional domain: home is where you return — the language of return is the language of belonging.

exercises.prompt: Ka' nontáhse'? asks 'where did you come from?' It uses a past-direction form. What does the -skwe' suffix on related verbs signal?
exercises.sub: Think about what marks completed past action
exercises.correct: Completed past action — something that happened and is finished
options: Completed past action — something that happened and is finished
The habitual — something you do regularly
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The -skwe' / -'skwe' complex marks completed past action in Kanien'kéha — a single event that happened and is finished. Ka' iéhse'skwe'? — Where did you go (and it's done)? This is the punctual past form you will study in depth in Unit 19.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does -skwe' mark?

exercises.prompt: Match the direction pair
exercises.sub: Question and answer belong together
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Direction pair by pair — going and coming, question and answer. These are the units of daily conversation about movement. You cannot ask where someone went without knowing how to answer where you came from.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Eniorhén'ne means 'tomorrow'. What particle from Unit 9 means 'now / at this point' and often opens a sequence?
exercises.sub: Think about the temporal particle you know
exercises.correct: Onen' — now / so then / at this point (opens narrative sequences and daily routine descriptions)
options: Onen' — now / so then / at this point (opens narrative sequences and daily routine descriptions)
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Onen' opens sequences in Kanien'kéha — it is the word that moves a narrative forward. 'Now I am home. Now I eat. Now I rest.' It is the hinge between events in a daily routine narration.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which particle moves a sequence forward?

exercises.prompt: Complete the question 'Where did you come from?'
exercises.correct: táhse'
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ka' nontáhse'? — Where did you come from? The root -táhse'- is the coming-from direction root. Compare Ka' wáhse'? (going) and Ka' nontáhse'? (coming from) — the roots are distinct.
exercises.wm: Please try again — starts with 't'.

exercises.prompt: Say this question aloud
exercises.hint: Ka' — non-TÁH-se' — four syllables. The stress falls on TÁH. Rising intonation for the question. This is the question someone at home asks when you walk in the door.

exercises.prompt: Coming home
exercises.storyPrompt: Ka' nontáhse'? — Where did you come from? Someone at home asks this when you return. It is a small question and a large one. In Kanien'kehá:ka communities, knowing where people have been is part of community care — who has been at the longhouse, who has been at the school, who has been across the border at Akwesasne. The question of where you come from (in the daily sense) is always inside the larger question of where you come from (in the ancestral sense). They are the same grammar.

lessons.title: Lesson 64 — Evening Activities
lessons.sub: Verbs of sitting, listening, and resting in the evening
intro: The evening draws on verbs you have known since Unit 3: á:tien — to sit, atkáhthon — to listen. In the evening, these are the verbs of winding down — sitting together, listening to stories, resting.

This lesson introduces the evening time expression ohsì:ka — tonight / this evening — and combines it with the activity verbs of the second half of the day. The stative forms of these verbs — being-seated, being-at-rest — are particularly important here.
Evening is also the time of oral tradition in Haudenosaunee culture. Stories are traditionally told in winter, in the evenings. The listening verb atkáhthon carries that weight.
lessons.ped: Known vocabulary in new cultural context: Nation (2001) identifies contextual reuse of known vocabulary as a consolidation activity that deepens retention without adding cognitive load. Sitting (á:tien) and listening (atkáhthon) from Unit 3 reappear here in the evening context — the same verbs, now loaded with the cultural significance of evening gathering and oral tradition. Krashen's +1: the vocabulary is fully known; the new element is the stative form (being-seated as a state vs the act of sitting) and the cultural framing. LaFever's Spiritual domain is activated by the oral tradition connection — evening listening is not just relaxation; it is cultural transmission.

exercises.prompt: Ohsì:ka means 'tonight / this evening'. What time-of-day word is its opposite — the beginning of the day?
exercises.sub: You learned this in Lesson 58
exercises.correct: ohron'ké:ne — in the morning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ohron'ké:ne (morning) and ohsì:ka (tonight/evening) are the two ends of the daily arc. The day moves from ohron'ké:ne through onia'karéhson (daytime) and ón:tie' (noon) to ohsì:ka (evening) and iohsiénhaon (nighttime).
exercises.wm: Please try again — what time word means morning?

exercises.prompt: Á:tien is the active form — 'to sit, sitting (actively)'. Ká:tate' is the stative — 'to be seated (as a condition)'. In an evening context, which form would you use to describe someone sitting by the fire?
exercises.sub: Are they in the act of sitting down, or already settled?
exercises.correct: Ká:tate' — the stative, because they are in the settled condition of being seated, not in the active motion of sitting down
options: Ká:tate' — the stative, because they are in the settled condition of being seated, not in the active motion of sitting down
Á:tien — the active, because they are always actively sitting
Either — they mean the same thing
Neither — a different verb is needed for evening
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Ká:tate' — the stative — is the correct form for someone settled in place. Sitting by the fire in the evening is a state, not an act. The active/stative distinction from Unit 10 applies here in a natural daily context.
exercises.wm: Please try again — active describes the act; stative describes the condition.

exercises.prompt: Atkáhthon — to listen. In Haudenosaunee oral tradition, stories are told in winter evenings. What does the listening verb carry in this context beyond its literal meaning?
exercises.sub: Think about LaFever's Spiritual domain
exercises.correct: Listening is the act of cultural transmission — to listen to a story in the evening is to receive knowledge, identity, and worldview
options: Listening is the act of cultural transmission — to listen to a story in the evening is to receive knowledge, identity, and worldview
The verb has no cultural significance
Listening is only relevant in formal ceremonies
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) argue that Indigenous language pedagogy should connect vocabulary to its cultural weight. Atkáhthon — listening — in the context of oral tradition is the act of receiving a community's knowledge. The verb itself is simple; its significance is not.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the time-of-day expression to its meaning
exercises.sub: Completing the daily arc
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The full daily arc in time expressions: morning → daytime → noon → evening → nighttime → midnight. You can now name any part of the day in Kanien'kéha.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this verb aloud
exercises.hint: at-KÁH-thon — three syllables. The stress falls on KÁH. The -thon ending is the infinitive marker. Say it quietly — listening is a quiet act.

exercises.prompt: Listening in the evening
exercises.storyPrompt: atkáhthon — to listen. In winter evenings, Haudenosaunee storytellers tell the legends — the creation story, the Peacemaker's journey, the tale of the corn husk doll. The rule is that stories are told only in winter, when the snakes and insects are sleeping and cannot carry the stories to places they should not go. Listening to a story in Kanien'kéha is not just entertainment. It is receiving. What have you received by listening — in any language — that you still carry?

lessons.title: Lesson 65 — Sleeping & Rest
lessons.sub: Sleep verbs; the stative of rest; night vocabulary
intro: Anón:we — to sleep. The day ends here. This lesson covers the verb of sleeping, the stative form of rest, and the night vocabulary that closes the daily arc.

Sleep in Kanien'kéha is handled differently from English. The active form of 'sleep' describes the act of falling asleep or sleeping as an ongoing process; the stative describes the state of being asleep. The distinction is the same active/stative logic you have been applying all unit.
This lesson also introduces Ohkwáriyentha' — bed — from the household vocabulary in Unit 7. A household object named in the language of the household: this is what domain expansion looks like.
lessons.ped: Completing the daily arc: Nation (2001) argues that vocabulary taught in coherent semantic sets — here, the full daily routine — is acquired faster than random lists because each word has a structural position in a familiar schema. Sleep closes the set. LaFever's four domains are all present in this closing lesson: the Mental domain (knowing the vocabulary), the Physical (producing the verb), the Emotional (rest as well-being and self-care), and the Spiritual (the night as a liminal time in Haudenosaunee cosmology — the time of dreams, of spiritual encounter, of the ancestors). Maracle (1993) notes that adult language reclamation involves relearning the vocabulary of daily intimacy — sleeping, resting, being at home — that was denied by residential school removal.

exercises.prompt: Anón:we — to sleep. Which form describes the state of being asleep (not the act of falling asleep)?
exercises.sub: Apply the active/stative distinction
exercises.correct: The stative form — describing the condition of being in sleep, not the motion of falling into it
options: The stative form — describing the condition of being in sleep, not the motion of falling into it
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The stative of sleep describes someone who is asleep — in the settled condition of sleep. The active describes the process of sleeping, or the act of going to sleep. The same logic applies here as to sitting (á:tien active / ká:tate' stative).
exercises.wm: Please try again — which form describes a settled state?

exercises.prompt: Ohkwáriyentha' — bed. What noun class does this belong to, based on its initial O-?
exercises.sub: Apply the noun class rules from Units 3 and 8
exercises.correct: O-class — nouns that begin with O- belong to the O-class, which includes many natural and household objects
options: O-class — nouns that begin with O- belong to the O-class, which includes many natural and household objects
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! O-class nouns begin with O- and include a wide range of natural and household objects. Ohkwáriyentha' — bed — joins the household vocabulary you first encountered in Unit 7. The noun class tells you how it will behave with possessive prefixes and in compounds.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what noun class begins with O-?

exercises.prompt: Match the night and rest vocabulary
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Sleep, bed, nighttime, midnight — the vocabulary of rest and the night. The daily arc is complete: from ohron'ké:ne (morning) to ahsón'then (midnight).
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this verb aloud
exercises.hint: a-NÓN:-we — three syllables. The long vowel on NÓN: is held. Say it softly — this is a word of rest.

exercises.prompt: Rest
exercises.storyPrompt: anón:we — to sleep. The day is done. In Haudenosaunee cosmology, the night is not empty — it is the time of dreams, of visits from ancestors, of the spiritual world's activity. The night is not just the absence of day; it is its own kind of presence. Residential schools enforced regimented sleep in dormitories, away from family, away from the sounds of home. Sleeping in a place where your language is spoken, where you are safe, where the night sounds are familiar — this is not small. Rest well.

lessons.title: Lesson 66 — Full Daily Routine Narration
lessons.sub: Producing a first-person account of a complete day
intro: This lesson is a production challenge. You now have the vocabulary for an entire day: waking, washing, dressing, eating, going, returning, evening, sleep. This lesson asks you to connect those pieces into a coherent first-person narration.

The connecting particle Onen' (now / so then) links events in sequence. The time expressions frame each moment. The verbs describe each action. Put together, these elements produce something a Kanien'kehá:ka speaker can recognize as a daily account.
You will not be given new words here. You will be given a structure to fill with what you know. That is what speaking a language requires.
lessons.ped: Extended discourse as productive milestone: Nation (2001) identifies connected discourse — a sequence of related utterances — as the highest level of productive vocabulary use. This lesson is the unit's synthesis point: learners produce a multi-step daily narration using all the vocabulary and structures introduced across Lessons 58–65. Freire's (1970) principle of functional language is the measure: can the learner produce something a community member would recognise as a real daily account? LaFever's all four domains converge: knowing the vocabulary (Mental), producing it in sequence (Physical), connecting it to one's actual daily life (Emotional), and situating the daily routine within the broader pattern of a life lived in this language (Spiritual).

exercises.prompt: Onen' — now / so then / at this point — is the particle that connects events in a daily narration. In the sentence 'Onen' wakátskhwa's', what does Onen' signal?
exercises.correct: A transition — 'and then / so then I eat' — moving the narrative to the next event
options: A transition — 'and then / so then I eat' — moving the narrative to the next event
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onen' is the discourse hinge — it moves a narration forward. 'Onen' ohron'ké:ne satkahnekárhons. Onen' wakátskhwa's.' = Then in the morning you brush your teeth. Then I eat. Each Onen' moves you one event further through the day.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does Onen' do in a sequence?

exercises.prompt: Put these daily events in chronological order
exercises.sub: Match each event to its position in the day

pairs.english: I am going to the longhouse
This evening — Where did you come from?

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Morning grooming → eating → going out → returning in the evening. This is the shape of a day in Kanien'kéha. Each event is a verb; the sequence is a narration.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what order does a day go in?

exercises.prompt: You want to say 'Now I am sleeping' to close your daily narration. Which time-of-day word best frames this closing sentence?
exercises.correct: iohsiénhaon — nighttime (the day is done; it is night; now I sleep)
options: iohsiénhaon — nighttime (the day is done; it is night; now I sleep)
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! iohsiénhaon (nighttime) closes the daily arc. 'Onen' iohsiénhaon. Onen' katanón:we.' — Now it is night. Now I sleep. The day is complete.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what time is it when you sleep?

exercises.prompt: Say your daily narration aloud
exercises.sub: Morning to night — use Onen' to connect each event
exercises.word: Onen' ohron'ké:ne — Onen' wakátskhwa's — Onen' wà:ke — Onen' iohsiénhaon
exercises.meaning: Then morning — Then I eat — Then I go — Then nighttime
exercises.hint: Take a breath between each Onen'. This is a narration, not a list. Each event deserves its own moment. If you add more detail — the brushing, the dressing, the destination — the narration becomes richer. Use what you know.

exercises.prompt: A day in the language
exercises.meaning: That is all / I am finished
exercises.storyPrompt: Onen' ki' wáhi — that is all. You have now described a full day in Kanien'kéha. Morning, grooming, dressing, eating, going, returning, evening, sleep. These are the most ordinary things a person does. They are also the things that residential schools made impossible in Kanien'kéha — the daily language of home and body and routine was taken. To name an ordinary day in this language is to restore something. A whole day. In the language of the people it belongs to.

lessons.title: Lesson 67 — Asking About Someone's Day
lessons.sub: tó:kens as an opener; daily-life question frames
intro: You already know tó:kens? — how are you? / are you well? — from Unit 9's conversation lesson. This lesson puts it to work as the opener for a daily-life conversation: how was your day, what did you do, where did you go?

The questions in this lesson draw on vocabulary across the whole course. You are not learning new words; you are learning to ask about the daily life of someone else — to turn your own routine vocabulary into questions directed outward.
This is the beginning of conversation, not just narration. The difference is relational: you are attending to another person's day, not describing your own.
lessons.ped: Receptive and productive balance: Krashen (1982) emphasises that acquisition requires input as well as output — listening to others describe their day (input) is as important as producing your own narration (output). This lesson explicitly practices the outward-facing question forms — turning known vocabulary into questions — as preparation for real dialogue. García & Li Wei (2014) note that translanguaging learners often develop receptive skills before productive; this lesson meets both. LaFever's Emotional domain is primary: asking about another person's day is an act of care, not just linguistic practice.

exercises.prompt: Shé:kon, tó:kens? — Hello, how are you? — is the opening pair from Unit 9. In a daily-life context, what typically follows tó:kens?
exercises.sub: Think about what comes after 'how are you?'
exercises.correct: Io'tenhró:ton (I am well) or Iah tó:kens (not so well) — the state-of-being response
options: Io'tenhró:ton (I am well) or Iah tó:kens (not so well) — the state-of-being response
Ka' wáhse'? — where are you going?
Kanié:ri iken'? — what is your name?
Onen' ki' wáhi — that is all
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The greeting-response pair is Shé:kon, tó:kens? → Io'tenhró:ton. After the exchange of wellbeing, the conversation opens — you can then ask about the day, about where someone has been, about what they have been doing.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Build a daily-life conversation opener
exercises.sub: Match question to natural follow-up
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! A daily conversation: greeting → wellbeing → direction → hunger. These four questions cover the ground of most ordinary daily-life exchanges in Kanien'kéha.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Iah tó:kens — not so well. Someone says this in response to tó:kens. What is the appropriate next move in the conversation?
exercises.sub: Think about care and response
exercises.correct: A follow-up question or expression of concern — 'Oh nikonhraién:ta'ne' or asking what has happened
options: A follow-up question or expression of concern — 'Oh nikonhraién:ta'ne' or asking what has happened
End the conversation with Onen' ki' wáhi
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! When someone says Iah tó:kens, the relational response is to notice and care — to ask a follow-up question, to express concern. Language is not just information exchange. Asking how someone is means being prepared to receive the real answer.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does care look like in conversation?

exercises.prompt: Say this opening exchange aloud
exercises.sub: Both question and answer — as if in a real conversation
exercises.meaning: Hello, how are you? — I am well.
exercises.hint: Shé:kon (warm, open). tó:kens? (genuine question, rising intonation). Io'tenhró:ton (settled, confident). Practice both sides — you will play both roles in real conversation.

exercises.prompt: Asking and caring
exercises.storyPrompt: Io'tenhró:ton — I am well. Tó:kens? — how are you? These are small words and large ones. In many Indigenous languages, the greeting is not a formality but a genuine inquiry — 'is it well with you?' is not rhetorical. McCarty & Lee (2014) note that languages of oral tradition carry relational ethics in their everyday phrases. When you ask tó:kens in Kanien'kéha, you are asking with the full weight of a culture that means it. Is it well with you — truly?

lessons.title: Lesson 68 — Routine and Ceremony
lessons.sub: How daily routine intersects with the ceremonial calendar
intro: A daily routine in Kanien'kéha is not separate from the ceremonial year — it is embedded in it. The seasons determine what work is done, what foods are eaten, what ceremonies are held. The daily routine in winter is different from the daily routine in planting season.

This lesson connects the daily-routine vocabulary you have built across this unit to the seasonal and ceremonial vocabulary from Units 4, 5, and 7. It is not introducing new grammar — it is deepening the cultural frame.
The question 'what do you do in the morning?' has a different answer in Tsiothorákson (Midwinter) than in Ohiarí:ha (summer). Language and ceremony are not separate things.
lessons.ped: Language situated in ceremonial life: McCarty & Lee (2014) argue that language revitalization must serve community-defined cultural continuity, not just communicative function. This lesson makes explicit what has been implicit throughout the unit: daily routine is not culturally neutral. The morning acknowledgement, the seasonal food, the direction you go each day — all of these are shaped by the ceremonial calendar.
