Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) identify this integration of language and cultural practice as central to decolonizing pedagogy. LaFever's Spiritual domain is primary here; this lesson is the unit's most explicitly Spiritual lesson, while all four domains remain present.

exercises.prompt: Kenién:ke — winter. In Haudenosaunee ceremonial life, which ceremony marks the beginning of the new year and shapes the daily routine of winter?
exercises.sub: You learned this in Unit 12 (Unit 12, Lesson 50)
exercises.correct: Tsiothorákson — the Midwinter Ceremony, the Haudenosaunee New Year
options: Tsiothorákson — the Midwinter Ceremony, the Haudenosaunee New Year
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsiothorákson — Midwinter — shapes the daily life of Kenién:ke (winter). The stories told in winter evenings, the ceremonies held in the longhouse, the foods prepared — these are the winter routine. The daily routine is never just personal; it is communal and ceremonial.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which ceremony marks the Haudenosaunee New Year?

exercises.prompt: Match the season to what characterises daily routine within it
exercises.sub: Connect seasons from Unit 4 to their ceremonial and practical content

pairs.english: Storytelling evenings; Midwinter Ceremony; indoor work
Maple sap gathering (Sugar Bush); planting begins; Strawberry Ceremony
Garden tending; Green Corn Ceremony; outdoor work

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Each season carries its own daily routine — its own work, its own ceremonies, its own foods. The language of daily life in Kanien'kéha is always seasonal. What you do in the morning depends on what time of the ceremonial year it is.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address — Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — is traditionally spoken at the start of each day. How does this make the daily routine of a Kanien'kehá:ka speaker different from a routine organised only by clock time?
exercises.sub: Think about what precedes the day's activities
exercises.correct: The day begins with collective acknowledgement — of the people, the earth, the waters, the sky — before individual tasks begin; the routine is relational, not only personal
options: The day begins with collective acknowledgement — of the people, the earth, the waters, the sky — before individual tasks begin; the routine is relational, not only personal
The Address replaces all daily activities
It is only spoken on Sundays
The Address is optional and rarely used
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Thanksgiving Address situates the individual within a web of relationships — with the earth, the waters, the animals, the sky — before the day begins. This is not a religious obligation in the colonial sense; it is a relational orientation. The daily routine that follows is not disconnected from that orientation.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: The Creator and the daily
exercises.storyPrompt: Shonkwaiá:tison — the Creator. In Haudenosaunee understanding, the Creator gave the people everything they need for a good life: the Three Sisters, the medicines, the ceremonies, the language. The daily routine — eating, working, resting — is not separate from that gift. To do the ordinary things of a day well, in the language of your people, is itself a form of acknowledgement. What ordinary thing have you done today that you could name in Kanien'kéha?

lessons.title: Lesson 69 — Irregular Daily Verbs
lessons.sub: Verbs of daily life that don't follow expected patterns
intro: Every language has verbs that do not follow the patterns you have learned. Kanien'kéha is no exception. This lesson introduces several daily-life verbs whose forms are irregular — or whose stem class does not behave exactly as expected.

The irregular verbs here are not problems to be solved — they are words to be learned as wholes, alongside the pattern vocabulary. Nation (2001) calls these 'high-frequency irregulars': they appear so often that rote memory is efficient, because you will encounter them dozens of times a week.

The verbs in this lesson include aié:re (to walk), which you have already seen, and atohsaré:ko (to wipe) and satkahthó:ni (you blow your nose) — body-action verbs with slightly irregular incorporation patterns.
lessons.ped: Irregulars as high-frequency vocabulary: Nation (2001) recommends that irregular high-frequency words receive explicit attention rather than being left for incidental acquisition — learners will encounter them constantly and need reliable access to them. This lesson treats daily-life irregular verbs not as exceptions to be worried about but as words to be owned through repeated exposure. LaFever's Mental domain is primary (learning that these verbs exist and what they mean); the Physical domain is activated through production exercises. Krashen's comprehensible input principle applies: even if the internal morphology is opaque, meaningful exposure in context creates acquisition.

exercises.prompt: Aié:re — to walk. This is one of the most important motion verbs in Kanien'kéha. What makes it worth treating separately from the direction verbs (wà:ke, etc.)?
exercises.sub: Think about what walking describes vs what direction verbs describe
exercises.correct: Aié:re describes the manner of movement (walking, on foot); direction verbs describe the trajectory (going toward, coming from)
options: Aié:re describes the manner of movement (walking, on foot); direction verbs describe the trajectory (going toward, coming from)
Aié:re is only used in ceremonies
Direction verbs replace aié:re in all contexts
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Manner vs trajectory — a fundamental distinction in motion vocabulary. Aié:re tells you how someone is moving (on foot, walking). Wà:ke tells you where they are going. A sentence can have both: 'I am walking to the longhouse' — aié:re + wà:ke + destination.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does aié:re describe?

exercises.prompt: Match the irregular daily verb to its meaning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four daily body-action verbs — walking, wiping, nose-blowing, coughing. They are not all irregular in the same way; each has its own specific form to learn. High-frequency means you will use them every day.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Atohsaré:ko — to wipe. This verb does not incorporate a body part, unlike satkahnekárhons (brush teeth) or sato'nnenhstáhkwa's (wash hands). What does this tell you?
exercises.sub: Think about when incorporation happens and when it doesn't
exercises.correct: Noun incorporation is a productive strategy but not obligatory — some action verbs exist without an incorporated noun, leaving the object unstated or to be specified separately
options: Noun incorporation is a productive strategy but not obligatory — some action verbs exist without an incorporated noun, leaving the object unstated or to be specified separately
It is an error in the verb
Atohsaré:ko is not a real Kanien'kéha word
Only body-part verbs can be incorporated
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Mithun (1984) notes that noun incorporation in polysynthetic languages is productive but optional. Some verbs incorporate their object; others do not. Atohsaré:ko (to wipe) does not specify what is being wiped — the object can be added separately or understood from context.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this verb aloud
exercises.hint: a-IÉ:-re — three syllables. The long vowel on IÉ: is held. This is a smooth, flowing verb — say it the way walking feels.

exercises.prompt: Walking on the land
exercises.storyPrompt: aié:re — to walk. In Kanien'kehá:ka territory, walking on the land is not just transportation. The land has names — every hill, every bend in the river, every stand of trees. To walk through the territory knowing those names is to read the land in the language of the people who have lived there for generations. Place names are not just labels; they are descriptions, histories, relationships. When you walk through the land saying its Kanien'kéha names, you are walking in the language. Where would you walk if you knew the names?

lessons.title: Lesson 70 — Working and Study
lessons.sub: The verbs of work, study, and language learning
intro: The daily routine includes work — paid work, community work, domestic work, study. This lesson covers the verbs of that productive middle of the day: rí:waks (to work), atkatón:ni (to study / to read), and the language-learning phrase Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — I am studying Mohawk.
The learning phrase is particularly significant: it names what you are doing right now. The act of studying Kanien'kéha is itself a Kanien'kéha verb.
This lesson also introduces the place of work and study — the school (tsi yontaweya'táhkwa) and the band office (tsi thatitsyénhayens) — in the context of daily departure and return.
lessons.ped: Metalinguistic awareness as Spiritual domain: Naming the act of language learning in the language being learned is a specific kind of metacognitive and cultural act. Maracle (1993) describes the experience of learning Kanien'kéha as an adult — the strange recursion of using the language to talk about learning the language. LaFever's Spiritual domain is activated here: learning an ancestral language is not just an educational activity. It is a form of relationship with the people who came before, with the community around you, and with the land the language names. Nation (2001) identifies work and study vocabulary as high-frequency and socially important — these are the words learners need for daily-life conversation about what they do.

exercises.prompt: Rí:waks — to work. What is the habitual first-person form — 'I work (regularly)'?
exercises.sub: Apply the habitual rule for this verb type
exercises.correct: Karí:waks — I work (habitual) — the k- prefix marks first person; the -s suffix marks habitual
options: Karí:waks — I work (habitual) — the k- prefix marks first person; the -s suffix marks habitual
Rí:waks — infinitive only, cannot be conjugated
Wakarí:waks — first person with w- prefix
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Karí:waks — I work. The k- marks first person on this verb type; -s marks the habitual. Compare this to other first-person habitual forms you have learned across the unit.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what marks first person here?

exercises.prompt: Atkatón:ni — to study / to read. This is an A-stem verb. What command form tells someone to study?
exercises.sub: Apply the A-stem command rule
exercises.correct: Satkatón:ni — study / read (A-stem command: s- replaces initial a-)
options: Satkatón:ni — study / read (A-stem command: s- replaces initial a-)
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Satkatón:ni — study / read. The A-stem command replaces the initial vowel (a-) with s-. This is the same rule you applied in Unit 3 and throughout this unit.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does the A-stem command do to the initial vowel?

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I am studying Mohawk / Kanien'kéha'?
exercises.sub: This phrase is in your glossary
exercises.correct: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — I am studying Mohawk. Kateweyénhstha is the first-person active form of the studying/learning verb. Kanyen'kéha is the Six Nations / Ontario variant spelling of Kanien'kéha — both refer to the same language.
exercises.wm: Please try again — check the spelling carefully, including the accent marks.

exercises.prompt: Why is it significant that the phrase 'I am studying Kanien'kéha' exists as a single verb phrase in Kanien'kéha itself?
exercises.sub: Think about what it means to name learning in the language being learned
exercises.correct: The language can talk about its own transmission — the act of learning Kanien'kéha is something Kanien'kéha can express, making language learning part of the language's living use
options: The language can talk about its own transmission — the act of learning Kanien'kéha is something Kanien'kéha can express, making language learning part of the language's living use
The phrase was invented recently and is not traditional
It only works if you are Kanien'kehá:ka
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! A living language can name its own transmission. The fact that Kanien'kéha has a verb for 'I am studying Kanien'kéha' means that language learning is part of the language's domain — it belongs inside the language, not outside it. McCarty & Lee (2014) would call this language sovereignty: the language defines its own conditions of existence.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this phrase aloud
exercises.hint: Ka-te-we-YÉN-hstha (5 syllables) — Kan-yen'-KÉ-ha (4 syllables). The stress falls on YÉN in the verb and KÉ in the language name. This is a true sentence about what you are doing right now.

exercises.prompt: Why you are here
exercises.storyPrompt: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — I am studying Kanien'kéha. You are doing this right now. Whatever brought you here — ancestry, curiosity, community, commitment, grief, love — you are here. The Indigenous Languages Act (Canada, 2019) affirms the right of Indigenous peoples to reclaim, use, and transmit their languages. This app is one small expression of that right. You are another. Why are you studying Kanien'kéha? What do you hope to be able to say, to do, to give, to receive, by the end of this?

title: Unit 14 — The Natural World: Weather, Sky and Seasons
desc: Rain, snow, wind, sun, moon, stars, thunder — the language of the sky and the land, and the beings the Thanksgiving Address gives thanks to

lessons.title: Lesson 105 — Weather Today
lessons.sub: The most common weather expressions and how to use them
intro: Weather is one of the first things people talk about. In Kanien'kéha, weather is expressed through verbs — not through adjectives. The weather is doing something, not simply being something.
You have already seen some of these: otonhnhéton — it is cold, ohsohkwá:ren — it is warm. This lesson adds rain, snow, wind, and a few more, and shows you how to use them in everyday conversation.
A useful opener: Tsi ioiánere' — it is good weather / a good day. You can build from there.
lessons.ped: Weather as daily functional language: Weather is among the highest-frequency conversational topics. Nation (2001): known words (otonhnhéton, ohsohkwá:ren from earlier units) reintroduced alongside new weather verbs. The lesson treats weather verbs as verbs — not adjectives — consistent with the framework established in Unit 23. LaFever Physical domain primary: learners produce weather descriptions. Spiritual domain: weather in Kanien'kéha is connected to living beings — the Thunderers, the winds — not neutral atmospheric conditions.

exercises.prompt: Match the weather verb to what it describes
exercises.sub: All of these are verbs — the weather is doing something
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four weather verbs — cold, warm, raining, snowing. Each one describes what the weather is doing right now.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Iokahrónhkwa means 'it is windy'. What kind of word is this in Kanien'kéha?
exercises.correct: A verb — the wind is doing something, not just being described
options: A verb — the wind is doing something, not just being described
A noun — the name of the wind
An adjective — a word describing conditions
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Iokahrónhkwa — it is windy. Weather in Kanien'kéha is expressed through verbs. The wind acts; it does not simply exist. This is consistent with how Kanien'kéha sees the natural world — as active and alive.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Three more weather expressions
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! You now have a full set of everyday weather expressions. Rain, snow, wind, cold, warm, hot — enough to talk about any day.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: It is raining today. Complete: ohron'ké:ne ___
exercises.correct: ó:iats
exercises.hint: ó
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ohron'ké:ne ó:iats — this morning it is raining. Time expression + weather verb. A complete observation about the day.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which word means it is raining?

exercises.prompt: You have two words for 'it is cold': otonhnhéton and Ionkwahró:ri. When might you use each?
exercises.correct: Both are correct — they are slightly different forms used in different contexts or dialects; either is understood
options: Both are correct — they are slightly different forms used in different contexts or dialects; either is understood
Otonhnhéton is for weather, Ionkwahró:ri is for food
Otonhnhéton is only used in winter
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Two forms for the same weather condition — both in use across communities. This is the kind of variation you will encounter as you speak with more people. Both are correct.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this weather verb aloud
exercises.hint: io-kah-RÓN-hkwa — four syllables. The stress falls on RÓN. Say it like you are leaning into the wind.

exercises.prompt: The wind is doing something
exercises.storyPrompt: Iokahrónhkwa — it is windy. The wind in Kanien'kéha is not just weather. The winds are acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address as beings that keep the air clean and moving, that carry the breath of life across the land. When you say it is windy in Kanien'kéha, you are using the same word a speaker would use to name the action of those beings. The wind is not a neutral atmospheric condition. It is alive.

lessons.title: Lesson 106 — Rain and Clouds
lessons.sub: The language of water falling from the sky
intro: Rain has more than one word in Kanien'kéha, and clouds have their own name. This lesson looks at the vocabulary of rain and cloud — from a light drizzle to a storm.
Shonkwa'nikónhrakon — cloud (lit. what covers the sky mind / what fills the sky). The cloud vocabulary in Kanien'kéha often describes what a cloud does rather than simply naming it.
Rain connects to water, which connects to the Thanksgiving Address: the waters are acknowledged as keeping us alive. Rain is part of that relationship.
lessons.ped: Rain and water as relational vocabulary: Water vocabulary in Kanien'kéha is dense and relational — rivers, lakes, rain, and sky water are all connected through roots and through the Thanksgiving Address. This lesson draws those connections without requiring learners to analyse the morphology. LaFever Spiritual domain: rain connects to gratitude for water. Physical domain: weather description sentences.

exercises.prompt: You have two forms for 'it is raining': ó:iats and tewatkahráthos. Both are correct. What does seeing two forms for the same thing tell you?
exercises.correct: Kanien'kéha is a living language — different forms are used in different communities and contexts
options: Kanien'kéha is a living language — different forms are used in different communities and contexts
One is for heavy rain and one is for light rain
One is ceremonial and one is everyday
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Multiple forms for the same thing are a sign of a living language with multiple communities using it. You will encounter both — learn to recognise them both.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the water word to what it names
exercises.sub: Water appears in many forms in Kanien'kéha

pairs.english: At the river / on the river

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Water in four forms — drinking water, river, lake, and 'at the river'. The water root runs through all of them. When you know one, you begin to see the others.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: In the Thanksgiving Address, the waters of the world are acknowledged and thanked. Why does rain fit naturally into that acknowledgement?
exercises.correct: Rain is one of the ways the waters return to the land — it is part of the same cycle of water that sustains life
options: Rain is part of the same cycle of water that sustains life — it is one of the ways the waters return to the land
Rain is not mentioned in the Thanksgiving Address
Rain is only relevant in summer ceremonies
The Thanksgiving Address only acknowledges rivers and lakes
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Thanksgiving Address thanks the waters — rivers, lakes, rain, underground water — as a whole. Rain is not separate from the river; it is part of the same living cycle. The Kanien'kéha vocabulary reflects this — water words are related to each other through their roots.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: It rained all morning. Complete: ohron'ké:ne akwé:kon ___
exercises.correct: tewatkahráthos
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ohron'ké:ne akwé:kon tewatkahráthos — it rained all morning. Akwé:kon means 'all / everything' — you have seen it in my name is (akwé:kon). Here it adds 'all morning'.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which word means 'it is raining' (variant form)?

exercises.prompt: Say this weather sentence aloud
exercises.hint: O-nen' (2 syllables) — Ó:-iats (2 syllables). The long vowel on Ó: is held. Say it like you have just looked out the window.

exercises.prompt: Water
exercises.storyPrompt: Ónhka — water. The Thanksgiving Address thanks the waters before almost anything else, because without water there is no life. Rain, rivers, lakes, springs — all of it is Ónhka in its different forms and places. For Kanien'kehá:ka communities along the St. Lawrence and the Ottawa River, the water has always been central — for travel, for food, for ceremony. And now, with climate change and pollution, the waters are under threat. When you say Ónhka and mean it, you are part of a long relationship with water that the language has always carried.

lessons.title: Lesson 107 — Snow and Ice
lessons.sub: Winter words — snow, ice, and the cold
intro: Kanien'kéha has rich winter vocabulary — the language of a people who have lived through hard winters in the St. Lawrence Valley for thousands of years.
Ohné:ka' — it is snowing. Óniehte — snow (the substance). Ó:wise' — ice / glass. Kenién:ke — winter. These are the building blocks of winter conversation.
Snow in Kanien'kéha is not just weather — it is the season, the substance, the silence. The Midwinter Ceremony happens in the heart of this vocabulary.
lessons.ped: Winter vocabulary as seasonal knowledge: Winter words in Kanien'kéha are connected to the ceremonial calendar in ways that everyday weather vocabulary in English is not. This lesson introduces winter vocabulary in that context — not just weather report words but seasonal identity words. LaFever Spiritual domain: Kenién:ke is the season of ceremony and storytelling.

exercises.prompt: Match the winter word to its meaning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Two verbs for snowing, the noun for snow, and the season. Winter has its own full vocabulary.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ó:wise' means both 'glass' and 'ice'. What do glass and ice have in common that explains why Kanien'kéha uses one word for both?
exercises.correct: Both are clear and hard — ice was the original clear hard substance before glass; the same word covers both
options: Both are clear and hard — ice was the original clear hard substance before glass; the same word covers both
Glass is made from ice in Kanien'kéha culture
Only ice is meant — glass is a borrowed meaning
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Ice was the original clear, hard, brittle substance. When Kanien'kéha speakers encountered glass, it went into the same category — Ó:wise'. This is the same productive naming strategy you saw in Unit 3: naming new things by what they resemble.
exercises.wm: Please try again — think about what ice and glass look and feel like.

exercises.prompt: Kenién:ke — winter. You learned in Unit 12 that age is counted in winters. What does this tell you about how important the season is in Kanien'kehá:ka life?
exercises.correct: Winter is the most significant seasonal threshold — it marks the turn of the year, the Midwinter Ceremony, and the passage of time
options: Winter is the most significant seasonal threshold — it marks the turn of the year, the Midwinter Ceremony, and the passage of time
Age is counted in winters only because it is the longest season
Winter is less important than summer
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Winter is the hinge of the Haudenosaunee year — the Midwinter Ceremony is the New Year, the storytelling season begins when the ground freezes, and age is measured in winters survived. The language encodes that centrality.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'There is snow on the ground' — ___ e'tho ká:yen Ohwén:tsiake
exercises.correct: Óniehte
exercises.hint: Ó
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Óniehte e'tho ká:yen Ohwén:tsiake — snow is there on the ground. The existence pattern from Unit 20, now with winter vocabulary.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which word is the noun for snow?

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'it is snowing'?
exercises.correct: ohné:ka'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ohné:ka' — it is snowing. A short, complete weather sentence. Long vowel on né:, glottal stop at the end.
exercises.wm: Please try again — long vowel on né: and a glottal stop at the end.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud — feel the season in it
exercises.hint: ke-NIÉ:N-ke — three syllables. The stress and long vowel are on NIÉ:N. Say it slowly — it is a heavy word, full of cold and firelight and story.

exercises.prompt: Winter
exercises.storyPrompt: Kenién:ke — winter. The ground freezes. The snakes and insects sleep. And so the stories can be told. The Midwinter Ceremony gathers the community in the longhouse to renew relationships with the spirit world, to give thanks, to celebrate the new year. Winter is not a dead season in Kanien'kehá:ka life — it is the season most full of ceremony, of story, of gathering indoors around warmth. What does winter mean where you live? What happens in your community when the cold comes?

lessons.title: Lesson 108 — Wind and Storms
lessons.sub: The winds and what they do
intro: Wind in Kanien'kéha is not just a weather condition — the winds are living forces. The Thanksgiving Address acknowledges the winds for keeping the air clean and carrying life across the land.
You already know iokahrónhkwa — it is windy. This lesson adds storm vocabulary and explores the relationship between wind and the natural world as Kanien'kéha speakers have always understood it.
The four directions — from which the winds come — are also part of this vocabulary. North, south, east, west: the winds have homes.
lessons.ped: Wind as living force: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): Indigenous language pedagogy should connect vocabulary to the worldview of the language. Wind in Kanien'kéha is not metaphorically alive — it is, within Haudenosaunee understanding, genuinely alive and purposeful. This lesson introduces that understanding plainly without anthropological framing. LaFever Spiritual domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Iokahrónhkwa — it is windy. The Thanksgiving Address thanks the winds for keeping the air clean and bringing life. What does this tell you about how Kanien'kéha speakers relate to wind?
exercises.correct: The winds are living forces with a purpose — not just atmospheric movement
options: The winds are living forces with a purpose — not just atmospheric movement
Wind is considered dangerous and unwelcome
The Thanksgiving Address does not mention wind
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! In Haudenosaunee understanding, the winds are active, purposeful beings. They move the air, carry seeds, bring weather, and keep the world breathing. The word for wind in Kanien'kéha names something alive.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Karonhià:ke means 'sky / heaven'. The root -ronhià:- relates to the sky world. Where have you seen sky-world vocabulary before in this course?
exercises.correct: Otsikonhríhson — sky world / upper world (from the creation story vocabulary)
options: Otsikonhríhson — sky world / upper world (from the creation story vocabulary)
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The sky world — Otsikonhríhson — is where the creation story begins, where Sky Woman falls from. Karonhià:ke names the same domain in everyday speech. The sky in Kanien'kéha vocabulary is a place with a history.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which vocabulary word relates to the sky world from the creation story?

exercises.prompt: Match sky and above words
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Sky vocabulary: the sky above, the general sky, the stars, the sunrise. Each one names a different aspect of what is above.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: The winds come from four directions. In Haudenosaunee understanding, each direction has its own character and purpose. Why might it matter to know which wind is blowing?
exercises.correct: Different winds bring different weather, seasons, and conditions — knowing the direction tells you what is coming
options: Different winds bring different weather, seasons, and conditions — knowing the direction tells you what is coming
Wind direction only matters for canoe travel
It does not matter in Kanien'kehá:ka culture
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Knowing the wind's direction tells you what weather is coming, what season is shifting, what the land is doing. This is practical knowledge that generations of Kanien'kehá:ka people used to plan travel, planting, and ceremony. The winds are informative — if you know how to listen.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this sky word aloud
exercises.hint: Ka-ron-hiÀ:-ke — four syllables. The grave accent on hiÀ: means the pitch falls there, and the colon holds the vowel. A word for the whole sky above you.

exercises.prompt: The sky
exercises.storyPrompt: Karonhià:ke — the sky. In the Haudenosaunee creation story, there was first a Sky World — Otsikonhríhson — where the people lived before the earth existed. Sky Woman fell from there, carried by geese to a turtle's back, and the earth was built from mud brought up from the bottom of the water. The sky is not empty in Kanien'kéha — it is full of history, of beings, of purpose. When you look up at Karonhià:ke, you are looking at the place the story begins.

lessons.title: Lesson 109 — Thunder and the Thunderers
lessons.sub: Ratiwéras — the beings who bring the thunder
intro: Thunder in Kanien'kéha is not just a sound — it is the voice of Ratiwéras, the Thunderers. The Thunderers are beings acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address for bringing the rains that water the plants and keep the land alive.
When it thunders in Kanien'kehá:ka understanding, those beings are at work — traveling across the sky, bringing life-giving water and clearing away harmful forces.
This lesson introduces Ratiwéras and the thunder vocabulary, and connects them to the Thanksgiving Address you have known since Unit 5.
lessons.ped: Thunder as relational being: The Thunderers are not a metaphor — within Haudenosaunee cosmology, they are real beings with specific responsibilities. This lesson introduces them plainly and respectfully, without over-explaining or reducing them to 'mythology'. LaFever Spiritual domain primary. This is one of the most explicitly spiritual lessons in the unit.

exercises.prompt: Ratiwéras means 'the Thunder People / Thunderers'. In Haudenosaunee understanding, who are the Thunderers?
exercises.correct: Living beings who travel across the sky bringing rain and protecting the land from harmful forces
options: Living beings who travel across the sky bringing rain and protecting the land from harmful forces
A metaphor for storms in general
Ancestral spirits with no specific role
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Thunderers — Ratiwéras — are real beings in Haudenosaunee understanding, not metaphors. They travel in the sky, bring the rains that water the plants, and drive away forces that would harm the people and the land. The Thanksgiving Address thanks them for this work.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' means 'it makes our minds happy / we give thanks'. This phrase appears in the Thanksgiving Address when each being is acknowledged. When the Thunderers are acknowledged, what are people giving thanks for?
exercises.correct: For the rain they bring, which waters the plants and keeps the land and people alive
options: For the rain they bring, which waters the plants and keeps the land and people alive
For bringing lightning to light up the sky
For announcing the end of winter
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The Thunderers bring the rains. Without rain, the Three Sisters cannot grow. Without the Three Sisters, the people cannot eat. The Thunderers are part of the chain of life that the Thanksgiving Address honours, link by link.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: When you hear thunder, a Kanien'kehá:ka speaker might say 'Ratiwéras' — the Thunderers. How is this different from saying 'there's a thunderstorm'?
exercises.correct: It names the beings who are responsible — it is a recognition rather than just a weather report
options: It names the beings who are responsible — it is a recognition rather than just a weather report
It is the same thing said differently
It is only said in ceremonies
It expresses fear of the storm
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Saying Ratiwéras when thunder sounds is an act of recognition — you are naming who is at work. Not 'there is weather happening' but 'those beings are moving'. The language keeps you in relationship with the world rather than observing it from the outside.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Write the word for the Thunderers
exercises.correct: Ratiwéras
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ratiwéras — the Thunderers. Ra- marks male beings; -tiwéras is the root. A word that names living beings, not just a weather event.
exercises.wm: Please try again — Ra- is the male prefix, then -tiwéras.

exercises.prompt: Say this name aloud — with respect
exercises.hint: Ra-ti-WÉ-ras — four syllables. Stress on WÉ. Say it clearly and with weight. This is a name, not just a weather word.

exercises.prompt: When the thunder comes
exercises.storyPrompt: Ratiwéras. The next time you hear thunder, say this word. Not as a magic formula — just as a practice of recognition. Something is happening. Those beings are moving across the sky, bringing water, keeping the world alive. The Thanksgiving Address thanks them every time it is spoken — before the gathering, before the ceremony, before the day's work. They have been doing their work for as long as anyone can remember. Ratiwéras.

lessons.title: Lesson 110 — The Sun and Warmth
lessons.sub: Karáhkwa — the sun and its warmth
intro: You already know Karáhkwa — the sun — from Unit 3's Ka-class nouns and from the shapes lesson (the sun as a shape). This lesson goes deeper: the sun in the Thanksgiving Address, the vocabulary of warmth, and the sun's role in the daily and seasonal cycle.
Ohsohkwá:ren — it is warm. Iotontátsion — it is hot. Ionkhi'nikónhrakon tsi watkaráhkwas — sunrise. Ionkhi'nikónhrakon tsi iotiénhonton — sunset.
The sun marks time — the length of the day, the turn of seasons, the hours of the ceremonial calendar.
lessons.ped: The sun as time-keeper and Thanksgiving Address being: The sun is acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address as the source of light and warmth that makes life possible. This lesson connects the practical vocabulary (warm, hot, sunrise, sunset) to that larger role. LaFever Spiritual domain: the sun is a being, not just a star. Physical domain: learners produce weather and time-of-day sentences.

exercises.prompt: Karáhkwa means 'the sun'. It is a Ka-class noun. Where have you seen Karáhkwa before in this course?
exercises.correct: In Unit 3 (Ka-class nouns) and in the shapes lesson — the sun as a shape
options: In Unit 3 (Ka-class nouns) and in the shapes lesson — the sun as a shape
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Karáhkwa has been in the course since Unit 3. Now it reappears as part of the natural world unit — the same word doing new work in a new context.
exercises.wm: Please try again — where did you first learn Ka-class nouns?

exercises.prompt: Match the sun and warmth expression to its meaning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Sun, warmth, heat, sunrise — the vocabulary of the sun's presence and work.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikónhrakon tsi iotiénhonton means 'sunset'. You already know Ionkhi'nikónhrakon from the Thanksgiving Address. What does that root mean?
exercises.correct: It makes our minds happy / it pleases us — so sunset is 'the time that makes our minds happy as it sets'
options: It makes our minds happy / it pleases us — so sunset is 'the time that makes our minds happy as it sets'
It has no connection to the Thanksgiving Address
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — it makes our minds happy — is the Thanksgiving Address phrase of gratitude. The same root appears in sunrise and sunset: these times of day make our minds happy. The language connects gratitude to the daily rhythm of light.
exercises.wm: Please try again — you have seen Ionkhi'nikónhrakon in the Thanksgiving Address.

exercises.prompt: It is warm today. Complete: onkwa'nikón:ra ___
exercises.correct: ohsohkwá:ren
exercises.hint: o
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! onkwa'nikón:ra ohsohkwá:ren — today it is warm. A simple, complete sentence about a good day.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which word means it is warm?

exercises.prompt: In the Thanksgiving Address, the sun is thanked for giving light and warmth and helping living things grow. What does this tell you about how the Haudenosaunee understand the sun?
exercises.correct: The sun is a living being with a purpose — not just a star but a relative that gives life
options: The sun is a living being with a purpose — not just a star but a relative that gives life
The sun is worshipped as a god
The sun is a natural object with no special significance
The sun is only important in summer
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Thanksgiving Address thanks the sun as a living being — a relative who does specific, essential work. This is not sun worship in the colonial religious sense. It is recognition of relationship and gratitude for what the sun does every day.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say the sun's name aloud
exercises.hint: Ka-RÁH-kwa — three syllables. Stress on RÁH. A Ka-class noun you have known since Unit 3 — say it with the confidence of a word you own.

exercises.prompt: Karáhkwa
exercises.storyPrompt: Karáhkwa — the sun. Every morning it rises. Every evening it sets. The Thanksgiving Address thanks it — not once, but every time the Address is spoken, which is every time people gather. Think about how many times Karáhkwa has risen over Kanien'kehá:ka territory. Think about how many times that word has been said, pointing up at the same sun you see today. The sun has not changed. The word has survived. Both of them are still here.

lessons.title: Lesson 111 — The Moon and Her Cycles
lessons.sub: Tsí:tewen — Grandmother Moon
intro: The moon is Tsí:tewen in Kanien'kéha. She is often called Grandmother Moon — a relative who guides the tides, the planting calendar, and the women's cycles.
The moon's phases structure time in the traditional calendar. Each month is a moon — the names of the months you learned in Unit 13's daily-life context carry the moon's cycle inside them.
Tsi ioteniáton — half moon. The moon has her own vocabulary, her own relationship to ceremony, and her own place in the Thanksgiving Address.
lessons.ped: The moon as Grandmother — relational vocabulary: Calling the moon Grandmother is not metaphor in Haudenosaunee understanding — it is a statement of kinship and relationship. This lesson introduces that relationship plainly. LaFever Spiritual and Emotional domains: the moon is a relative, and her cycles connect to women's lives and the ceremonial calendar. Nation (2001): moon vocabulary connects to the month names already encountered.

exercises.prompt: Tsí:tewen means 'moon'. She is often called Grandmother Moon. What does calling the moon 'Grandmother' tell you about the Haudenosaunee relationship to the natural world?
exercises.correct: The natural world is understood as family — the moon is a relative, not just a celestial object
options: The natural world is understood as family — the moon is a relative, not just a celestial object
It is a poetic expression only
Only elder women call the moon Grandmother
It means the moon is thought to be human
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kinship language for the natural world — Grandmother Moon, Mother Earth — expresses a genuine understanding of relationship, not just affection. The moon is a relative with responsibilities toward the people and the living world.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Tsi ioteniáton means 'half moon'. You have seen ioteniáton before — where?
exercises.sub: Think about the shapes lesson in Unit 6
exercises.correct: In the shapes lesson — as a shape name for something that is half (half-moon shape)
options: In the shapes lesson — as a shape name for something that is half (half-moon shape)
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Tsi ioteniáton appeared in the shapes lesson as a shape — the crescent or half-moon shape. Now you see it as a description of the actual moon in a phase. The shape word and the sky word are the same.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which unit covered shapes?

exercises.prompt: The month names carry the moon's cycle. Match the month to its meaning
exercises.sub: These are the traditional Kanien'kéha month names
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The month names are moon names — each one describes what is happening in the natural world during that moon. The green corn moon, the great cold moon, the beginning of winter. The calendar is written in the land.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address acknowledges Grandmother Moon for guiding the tides, influencing women's cycles, and helping regulate planting. Why does planting connect to the moon?
exercises.correct: Many agricultural traditions plant by lunar cycles — the moon's phases affect soil moisture and germination
options: Many agricultural traditions plant by lunar cycles — the moon's phases affect soil moisture and germination
Planting only happens at full moon
The moon has no effect on planting
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Lunar planting — timing planting to the moon's phases — is a practical knowledge tradition shared across many cultures. The Thanksgiving Address thanks Grandmother Moon for this guidance. The ceremonial and the practical are the same thing.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Write the Kanien'kéha word for moon
exercises.correct: Tsí:tewen
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsí:tewen — Grandmother Moon. Acute accent on tsí:, long vowel after it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — acute accent on the first syllable, then colon for the long vowel.

exercises.prompt: Say Grandmother Moon's name aloud
exercises.hint: TSÍ:-te-wen — three syllables. The long vowel is on TSÍ:. Say her name with the same respect you would use for a grandmother.

exercises.prompt: Grandmother Moon
exercises.storyPrompt: Tsí:tewen — Grandmother Moon. She has been rising and setting over Kanien'kehá:ka territory since before the language had a writing system. She will be rising long after all of us are gone. Her cycles have guided planting, ceremony, and the rhythms of women's bodies for generations. The Thanksgiving Address thanks her every time it is spoken — and it has been spoken every day, at every gathering, for as long as people have been gathering here. When you look up at Tsí:tewen tonight, you are looking at the same being that has been called Grandmother by people in this territory for thousands of years.

lessons.title: Lesson 112 — Stars and the Sky at Night
lessons.sub: Iotiia'tá:kon — the stars and what they mean
intro: The stars are Iotiia'tá:kon — the stars (collective). They too are acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address — for providing light at night, for helping travellers navigate, and for the beauty they add to the world.
Star navigation was practical knowledge for the Kanien'kehá:ka people — knowing the stars meant knowing where you were on the water and the land at night.
The night sky in Kanien'kéha vocabulary is a populated place — not empty space, but a world of beings doing work.
lessons.ped: Stars as practical and spiritual knowledge: Star navigation is practical land-based knowledge; the Thanksgiving Address acknowledgement of the stars is spiritual. This lesson holds both — not separating them, because Haudenosaunee understanding does not separate them. LaFever Spiritual domain: the stars are acknowledged beings. Mental domain: star navigation as knowledge.

exercises.prompt: Iotiia'tá:kon means 'the stars (collective)'. The word groups all the stars together. Why might the stars be named collectively rather than individually?
exercises.correct: The stars work together — they are acknowledged as a group doing the same work, not as individual objects
options: The stars work together — they are acknowledged as a group doing the same work, not as individual objects
There are too many stars to name individually
The language has no words for individual stars
Individual stars are not visible from Kanien'kehá:ka territory
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Thanksgiving Address thanks the stars as a collective — they work together to provide light and guidance. This reflects how Haudenosaunee thought often works: beings are named by what they do together, not sorted into individual categories.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the night sky word to its meaning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Stars, moon, night, sky — four words for the night world above. Together they give you vocabulary for describing any night.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address thanks the stars for providing light at night and helping travellers find their way. What kind of knowledge is star navigation?
exercises.correct: Land-based and practical — knowing the stars meant you could travel safely on water and land at night
options: Land-based and practical — knowing the stars meant you could travel safely on water and land at night
Only ceremonial — star knowledge has no practical use
Astronomical science in the modern sense
Knowledge held only by elders in ceremony
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Star navigation is practical knowledge — the Kanien'kehá:ka people travelled by canoe across vast territories. Knowing which star was which, which direction it pointed, what season it marked — this was survival knowledge. The Thanksgiving Address thanks the stars for exactly this practical help.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'The stars are there' — ___ e'tho ká:yen
exercises.correct: Iotiia'tá:kon
exercises.hint: I
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iotiia'tá:kon e'tho ká:yen — the stars are there. The existence pattern from Unit 20, naming the stars. A sentence you could say on any clear night.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the word for the stars?

exercises.prompt: Say the stars' name aloud
exercises.hint: io-tiia'-TÁ:-kon — four syllables. The glottal stop is in the middle — iia' — and the stress and long vowel are on TÁ:. Look up when you say it.

exercises.prompt: The stars
exercises.storyPrompt: Iotiia'tá:kon — the stars. They are acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address for lighting the night and guiding those who travel. Before electric light, before GPS, the stars were navigation. A Kanien'kehá:ka traveller on the St. Lawrence at night would have known the stars the way you know a road — by what was there, what direction it pointed, what it meant. The language of the stars is the language of finding your way. What would you need to know, in this language, to find your way?

lessons.title: Lesson 113 — Seasonal Weather Patterns
lessons.sub: What the weather does in each season — and what the season calls for
intro: Weather does not exist apart from season. In Kanien'kehá:ka life, the weather of each season is connected to what the season requires — planting, harvesting, ceremony, rest.

This lesson connects the weather vocabulary from earlier lessons to the four seasons. What is the weather like in Kenién:ke (winter)? In Sé:ske (spring)? The language of weather and the language of season belong together.
The seasonal round is also the ceremonial round. The weather that comes with each season signals what ceremony is next.
lessons.ped: Weather as seasonal and ceremonial signal: Separating weather vocabulary from seasonal and ceremonial context misses half the meaning. This lesson reconnects them. Nation (2001): vocabulary deepens when encountered in multiple meaningful contexts. LaFever Spiritual domain: seasons are ceremony. Emotional domain: seasonal change is felt, not just observed.

exercises.prompt: Match the season to its typical weather
exercises.sub: What does the language say about each season's character?

pairs.english: ohsohkwá:ren tánon ó:iats — warming and rain

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four seasons, four weather characters. In Kanien'kéha you can say what the weather is doing and what season it belongs to — they go together.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Sé:ske — spring. Spring rain is especially important in Kanien'kehá:ka agricultural life. Why?
exercises.correct: Spring rain softens the ground and brings water for the first planting of the Three Sisters
options: Spring rain softens the ground and brings water for the first planting of the Three Sisters
Rain does not affect Haudenosaunee agriculture
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Spring rain prepares the earth for planting. The Sugar Bush (maple sap gathering) happens in early spring, and the first plantings of the Three Sisters follow. The Strawberry Ceremony celebrates the first berries. Spring rain makes all of this possible.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ohiarí:ha — summer. The Green Corn Ceremony happens in late summer. What connects the summer heat and the Green Corn Ceremony?
exercises.correct: The summer sun grows the corn — when the corn is ready, the ceremony gives thanks for it
options: The summer sun grows the corn — when the corn is ready, the ceremony gives thanks for it
Summer heat is avoided, not celebrated
The ceremony is not connected to the weather
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The summer sun ripens the corn. The Green Corn Ceremony — Seskéha in the month name — gives thanks when the corn is ready. Weather, agriculture, and ceremony are one continuous thing in the Haudenosaunee calendar.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'In autumn it gets cold' — ___ otonhnhéton
exercises.correct: Ken'niká:ien
exercises.hint: K
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ken'niká:ien otonhnhéton — in autumn it is cold. Season + weather verb. The glottal stop in Ken'niká:ien is part of the word.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which season word means autumn?

exercises.prompt: Say this seasonal weather sentence aloud
exercises.hint: o-hia-RÍ:-ha (4 syllables) — io-ton-TÁT-sion (4 syllables). Season + weather verb. A complete sentence about summer.

exercises.prompt: The seasonal round
exercises.storyPrompt: Sé:ske — spring. The ice on the river breaks up. The sap rises in the maple trees. The geese return. The Strawberry Ceremony comes, marking the beginning of the growing season. Every spring in Kanien'kehá:ka territory has been like this — the same signs, the same responses, the same ceremonies. The weather is not just background. It is the signal that tells the community what comes next. What does the change of season signal where you live?

lessons.title: Lesson 114 — Weather and the Thanksgiving Address
lessons.sub: The sky beings in the Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen
intro: This lesson brings the whole unit together through the Thanksgiving Address — the ceremony you first encountered in Unit 5.

The Thanksgiving Address thanks the natural world in order, from the earth up to the Creator. This unit has covered many of the beings in that upper section: the winds, the Thunderers, the sun, the moon, the stars.
Reading those sections of the Address is now within reach. You know the beings. You know their names. You know what they do.
lessons.ped: The Thanksgiving Address as integrating framework: McCarty & Lee (2014): culturally sustaining pedagogy centres the oral traditions of the community. The Thanksgiving Address has structured this curriculum since Unit 5. This lesson returns to it with the weather and sky vocabulary now in hand — so learners can read and hear sections they could not fully understand before. LaFever Spiritual domain primary. All four domains converge.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address moves through the natural world in order. Which beings from this unit appear in the Address?
exercises.sub: Choose the most complete answer
exercises.correct: The winds, the Thunderers, the sun, the moon, and the stars — all acknowledged in turn
options: The winds, the Thunderers, the sun, the moon, and the stars — all acknowledged in turn
None of the weather beings — only the earth is thanked
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The upper sections of the Thanksgiving Address thank the winds, the Thunderers, the sun (Karáhkwa), the moon (Tsí:tewen), and the stars (Iotiia'tá:kon). Every being from this unit appears there.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match each Thanksgiving Address being to what they are thanked for
exercises.sub: These are the sky and weather beings from this unit

pairs.english: Bringing rain and clearing the air
Giving light and warmth to help living things grow
Guiding tides and cycles, helping regulate time
Lighting the night and guiding travellers

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Four sky beings, four responsibilities. The Thanksgiving Address is specific — it does not thank 'nature' in general. It thanks each being by name and by what they do.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — it makes our minds happy / we give thanks — appears after each being is acknowledged. Why does gratitude come after naming?
exercises.correct: You acknowledge who they are first — then you express gratitude for what they do. Recognition comes before thanks.
options: You acknowledge who they are first — then you express gratitude for what they do. Recognition comes before thanks.
Gratitude comes first in Kanien'kéha custom
Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' is only said once at the end
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Thanksgiving Address names each being — who they are, what they do — and then expresses gratitude. Recognition before thanks. You cannot properly thank something you have not properly seen.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: The Address begins with Onkwehshón:'a — the people / human beings. Why do you think the people are thanked first?
exercises.correct: Acknowledging the people first affirms that everyone is gathered and present before turning to the wider world
options: Acknowledging the people first affirms that everyone is gathered and present before turning to the wider world
Humans are more important than other beings
The people are thanked last in some versions
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Address begins by acknowledging the people — those present, those gathered. You thank your own community first, then turn together to thank the world around you. The movement is from the immediate to the vast.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say the name of the Thanksgiving Address — slowly and completely
exercises.meaning: The words that come before all else
exercises.hint: OH-én:-ton (3 syllables) — Ka-ri-hwa-TÉH-kwen (5 syllables). You have said this name many times since Unit 5. Say it now with everything you have learned about the sky beings it thanks.

exercises.prompt: The words that come before all else
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. You have now met many of the beings this Address thanks: the earth, the waters, the plants, the animals, the fish, the birds, the winds, the Thunderers, the sun, the moon, the stars. You have learned their names in Kanien'kéha. When the Thanksgiving Address is spoken, every name you know is a moment of fuller understanding — not just words in a ceremony but beings you have been introduced to, one by one, in this language. The Address was always this. Now you know a little more of what it contains.

lessons.title: Lesson 115 — Environmental Change
lessons.sub: How the language names what is happening to the land and water
intro: The natural world this unit has described — clear rivers, productive seasons, reliable snow, the Thunderers bringing rain — is changing. Climate change, pollution, and resource extraction are affecting Kanien'kehá:ka territory.
Kanien'kéha speakers are naming these changes. The language is alive and adapting — new words and phrases are being created to describe what was not there before.
This lesson is not a vocabulary drill. It is a reflection on what it means to have a language that names your home — and what it means when that home changes.
lessons.ped: Environmental change as language context: Indigenous communities are among the most directly affected by climate change and environmental degradation. A language curriculum that teaches 'the river is there' without acknowledging that rivers are threatened would be incomplete. This lesson connects environmental vocabulary to the present situation without being prescriptive. LaFever Emotional and Spiritual domains primary.

exercises.prompt: Ohwén:tsia ne Ionkwarihó:ten — the earth that sustains us. This phrase from the Thanksgiving Address describes the earth's role. What does it mean when that earth is being harmed?
exercises.correct: The relationship the Address describes is in danger — what sustains us is at risk
options: The relationship the Address describes is in danger — what sustains us is at risk
It is a problem for scientists only
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Thanksgiving Address describes a web of relationships — the earth sustains us. When any part of that web is harmed, the whole relationship changes. The Address is not just ceremony — it describes how the world actually works. Protecting what it thanks is part of living by it.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Kaniatarà:ke — at the river. Kanien'kehá:ka communities along the St. Lawrence have dealt with industrial pollution of the river for decades. What does it mean to have a language that names a river as a living being — when that river is polluted?
exercises.correct: The language holds the relationship as it should be — and the pollution names what has been broken
options: The language holds the relationship as it should be — and the pollution names what has been broken
Naming the river causes the problem
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kanien'kehá:ka communities — particularly Akwesasne — have faced decades of industrial contamination of the St. Lawrence River from nearby factories. The language that names the river as a living relative also names what has been done to it. The word Kaniatarà:ke holds both the river as it should be and the river as it is.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Wahtá: — the maple tree. Maple trees are sensitive to climate change and are shifting northward as temperatures rise. What does this mean for the Sugar Bush tradition?
exercises.correct: The traditional practice of maple sap gathering is changing as the timing and location of maple trees shifts
options: The traditional practice of maple sap gathering is changing as the timing and location of maple trees shifts
Climate change does not affect maple trees
The Sugar Bush tradition is unrelated to maple trees
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Sugar Bush — maple sap gathering — is one of the first spring ceremonies. As maple trees shift their range and sap runs earlier or later due to warming temperatures, the traditional timing of the Sugar Bush is affected. The land is changing, and the ceremonies that follow the land must respond.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ónhka — water. Access to clean water is a serious issue in many Indigenous communities across Canada. Why might a language that has many words for water be especially relevant in this context?
exercises.correct: The language names water as a relative worth protecting — and having that vocabulary supports advocacy for clean water
options: The language names water as a relative worth protecting — and having that vocabulary supports advocacy for clean water
Language has no role in environmental protection
Having many words for water means the problem is less serious
Only English can be used for political advocacy
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! A language that names water as a living relative — acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address, embedded in place names, central to daily vocabulary — is a language that carries a framework for protecting water. Language and land rights are not separate issues.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: The earth that sustains us
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohwén:tsia ne Ionkwarihó:ten — the earth that sustains us. This phrase from the Thanksgiving Address is not past tense. It is present. The earth is sustaining us right now — with the food it grows, the water it holds, the air it produces. And right now, in this territory and in many others, the earth is under pressure. Climate change, pollution, resource extraction. The Haudenosaunee have been watching this land for thousands of years. They have seen change before — ice ages, floods, drought. They are watching now. And they are still speaking, in this language, about the earth that sustains us. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 30 — Reading and Writing Kanien'kéha
desc: The marks that make Kanien'kéha readable — long vowels, glottal stops, stress, and nasals — and why every one of them matters

lessons.title: Lesson 94 — The Writing System: Where It Came From
lessons.sub: How Kanien'kéha came to be written — and why it matters
intro: Kanien'kéha is a spoken language first. It lived in the mouths and ears of the Kanien'kehá:ka people for thousands of years before anyone wrote it down.

The writing system used in this app — and in most Kanien'kehá:ka communities today — was developed by linguists and community members working together. It uses the same alphabet as English but adds marks: accent marks, long vowel markers, and special letters to capture sounds English cannot.

There is more than one way to write Kanien'kéha. Different communities use slightly different spelling conventions. None is wrong. This unit focuses on the Kanehsatà:ke / Kahnawà:ke system used throughout this app.
lessons.ped: Orthography as political and cultural act: The history of writing systems for Indigenous languages is entangled with colonial documentation practices. This lesson names that history plainly — missionaries and linguists wrote down Indigenous languages for their own purposes — while foregrounding the community-led work of developing a usable, respected orthography. LaFever Spiritual domain: writing the language is a form of asserting its existence and permanence. Mental domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Kanien'kéha was spoken for thousands of years before it was written. What does this tell you about how to think about the writing system?
exercises.correct: It is a tool to help people read and learn — the real language lives in speech
options: It is a tool to help people read and learn — the real language lives in speech
Written Kanien'kéha is the correct form
Speaking without writing is informal and incorrect
The writing system replaced the spoken language
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The writing is a tool — useful and important, but it serves the spoken language. When you learn to read and write Kanien'kéha, you are learning a way to study and remember the spoken language. The real home of the language is in the voice.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Oh ni:ioht tsi enshi:ron ___? means 'How do you say ___?' This is a learner's tool. When would you use it?
exercises.correct: When you want to know the Kanien'kéha word for something you don't know yet
options: When you want to know the Kanien'kéha word for something you don't know yet
When you already know the word
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Oh ni:ioht tsi enshi:ron ___? — how do you say ___? Keep this question close. Every speaker you meet is a teacher.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Oh naho:ten ken:ton ne ___? means 'What does ___ mean?' Why is having this question in your toolkit so important?
exercises.correct: It lets you ask for help when you don't understand — and keeps you in the conversation instead of stopping
options: It lets you ask for help when you don't understand — and keeps you in the conversation instead of stopping
It is only useful in writing class
Fluent speakers never need to ask this
It is considered rude to ask
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Oh naho:ten ken:ton ne ___? — what does ___ mean? A learner who can ask this question can keep learning from every conversation. You do not need to understand everything. You need to be able to ask.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the learner's tool to what it does
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four tools every learner needs. How do you say it? What does it mean? Say it again. I don't understand. These four phrases can carry you through many conversations even when your vocabulary is still growing.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Writing the language
exercises.storyPrompt: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — I am studying Kanien'kéha. At one point in history, people who wanted to suppress this language did not bother writing it down — they just forbade it. Every time Kanien'kéha is written — in a textbook, on a sign, in an app like this one — it is one more place the language exists in the world. Writing is not the whole language. But it is a form of saying: this language is here. It is real. It is not going away. What does it mean to you to see Kanien'kéha written?

lessons.title: Lesson 95 — Short and Long Vowels
lessons.sub: The colon (:) — the most important mark in Kanien'kéha writing
intro: Every vowel in Kanien'kéha comes in two lengths: short and long. A long vowel is held about twice as long as a short one. The colon (:) after a vowel is what tells you it is long.
This is not a decoration. Changing a vowel's length can completely change the meaning of a word. The colon is as important as any letter.
You have been seeing it since the first lesson: í:se' (you), Shé:kon (hello), Tho niioht, Niawenhkó:wa. This lesson makes the pattern visible.
lessons.ped: Vowel length as phonemic contrast: Mithun (1982) establishes vowel length as fully contrastive in Kanien'kéha. This is introduced through minimal pairs drawn from known vocabulary — words learners have been using throughout the course — so the point lands with words they already care about. Nation (2001): orthographic awareness through familiar examples. Physical domain: learners practise distinguishing and producing length contrast.

exercises.prompt: In í:se' (you), the colon after í tells you the vowel is long. How does a long vowel sound different from a short one?
exercises.correct: It is held longer — about twice the length of a short vowel
options: It is held longer — about twice the length of a short vowel
It has a different sound entirely
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! A long vowel is held longer. Not louder, not higher — just longer. í: in í:se' is held about twice as long as a plain i would be. Stress (loudness and pitch) is marked separately with an accent mark.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Find the long vowel in each word
exercises.sub: The colon (:) marks it

pairs.english: Long vowel on í — held double
Long vowel on é — held double
Long vowel on ó — held double
Long vowel on é — held double

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Long vowels are everywhere in Kanien'kéha. Once you see the colon, you know to hold that vowel. Missing the length can change the meaning of a word.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look for the colon.

exercises.prompt: Shé:kon has a long vowel on é:. What happens if you shorten it — if you say Shékon without the length?
exercises.correct: You might be misunderstood — length is part of the word, not optional
options: You might be misunderstood — length is part of the word, not optional
Nothing — length does not affect meaning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Vowel length in Kanien'kéha is not optional — it is part of the word. Shé:kon is a specific word because of that long é:. Speakers who know the language will notice if you shorten it, just as an English speaker notices if you say a word wrong.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Mark the long vowel: Sh___:kon
exercises.correct: é
exercises.hint: é
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shé:kon — the long vowel is é:. The colon comes right after the vowel it lengthens.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which vowel does the colon follow in Shé:kon?

exercises.prompt: Count the long vowels in: Onen' ki' wáhi
exercises.sub: Look for colons — remember wáhi has an accent but no colon
exercises.correct: Zero — none of the vowels in Onen' ki' wáhi are long
options: Zero — none of the vowels in Onen' ki' wáhi are long
Two — the o and the i
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! No colons in Onen' ki' wáhi — all short vowels. The accent mark on wáhi (á) marks stress, not length. Accent and length are two different marks.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look only for colons (:), not accent marks.

exercises.prompt: Say these two words — feel the length difference
exercises.hint: Shé:kon: hold the é: for a full beat. Shékon: cut it short. Can you feel the difference? The first is the word. The second is a mistake. Train your ear to hear it.

exercises.prompt: The colon
exercises.storyPrompt: A single small mark — the colon. It changes everything it touches. Without it, words blur into each other, meanings shift. With it, Kanien'kéha becomes readable, teachable, transmittable. The people who worked to develop this writing system chose to put the colon in because they respected what it represents — the precision of a language that has always known the difference between short and long. When you honour the colon, you honour that precision.

lessons.title: Lesson 96 — The Glottal Stop
lessons.sub: The apostrophe (') — a real consonant, not punctuation
intro: The apostrophe (') in Kanien'kéha is not punctuation. It is a real consonant — the glottal stop. Your throat closes briefly, then opens again. You do this in English too, in the middle of 'uh-oh' — that little catch between the two sounds.
In Kanien'kéha the glottal stop appears at the beginning, middle, and end of words. It is as real as k, t, or n. Leaving it out can change the meaning of a word.
You have been seeing it since Unit 1: í:'i (I), iken', Onen', Raksá:'a. This lesson makes it explicit.
lessons.ped: Glottal stop as phoneme: The glottal stop is consistently the hardest orthographic feature for English-background learners to take seriously — they read the apostrophe as punctuation. This lesson addresses that head-on with familiar examples. Mithun (1982): the glottal stop is a full phoneme in Kanien'kéha. Physical domain primary: learners practise producing and identifying it.

exercises.prompt: In í:'i (I / me), the apostrophe between í: and i is a glottal stop. What does a glottal stop feel like to produce?
exercises.correct: A brief closure of the throat — like the catch in the middle of 'uh-oh' in English
options: A brief closure of the throat — like the catch in the middle of 'uh-oh' in English
A silent pause with no throat movement
A puff of air like the h in hello
A sound like the letter k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The glottal stop is produced by briefly closing the vocal cords — the same catch you feel in 'uh-oh'. In í:'i, you hold the í:, close briefly, then open onto the final i. It takes practice but it is a learnable sound.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Find the glottal stop in each word
exercises.sub: Look for the apostrophe (')

pairs.english: Glottal stop between í: and i
Glottal stop before the final a

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Glottal stops appear at the start, middle, and end of words. In every position, the apostrophe marks a real throat closure. It is not decoration.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look for the apostrophe (').

exercises.prompt: í:se' ends with a glottal stop (the ' at the end). What would happen to the meaning if you dropped it — saying í:se instead of í:se'?
exercises.correct: The word would be incorrect — the glottal stop is part of the word
options: The word would be incorrect — the glottal stop is part of the word
The word would become a question
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The glottal stop in í:se' is part of the word — leaving it out produces an incorrect form. Kanien'kéha speakers will notice. Honour the apostrophe.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Write the pronoun 'I / me' correctly with its glottal stop
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! í:'i — the glottal stop sits between the long vowel and the final i. One small mark, but the word is not complete without it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what mark goes in the middle of í:'i?

exercises.prompt: Ieksá:'a (girl) has a glottal stop before the final 'a. In writing, how do you know a glottal stop is there rather than just an apostrophe used for English-style punctuation?
exercises.correct: Context — in Kanien'kéha writing, every apostrophe represents a glottal stop. There is no other use for it.
options: Context — in Kanien'kéha writing, every apostrophe represents a glottal stop. There is no other use for it.
It is only a glottal stop if it appears at the end of a word
Glottal stops are marked with a different symbol
You cannot know — it is ambiguous
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! In Kanien'kéha orthography, every apostrophe is a glottal stop. There is no ambiguity — it is not used for contractions or possessives the way English uses it. Every ' you see is a sound.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say these two words — feel the glottal stop
exercises.hint: í:'i: hold the long í:, close the throat briefly, then say the final i. iken': say it, then close the throat at the end — don't let the word trail off. The glottal stop closes the word like a period closes a sentence.

exercises.prompt: The throat's consonant
exercises.storyPrompt: í:'i — I. The very first pronoun you learned has a glottal stop in its heart. The word for 'I' in Kanien'kéha closes briefly on itself — a small pause, a catch, before it opens again. Think about that. The word for yourself has a built-in moment of pause. Not silence — a closure that then reopens. What does it mean to say 'I' with that catch in it?

lessons.title: Lesson 97 — Stress and Accent Marks
lessons.sub: The acute (á) and grave (à) — where the voice rises and falls
intro: Stress in Kanien'kéha is marked with accent marks over vowels. The acute accent (á) marks the syllable where your voice rises and gets a little stronger. The grave accent (à) marks a falling pitch.
You have been seeing these throughout the course: Shé:kon, wáhi, Kanehsatà:ke. The accent tells your ear — and your mouth — where to land.
Stress in Kanien'kéha can change meaning. It is not decoration and it is not optional. But it is learnable — and the more you listen, the more naturally it comes.
lessons.ped: Stress as meaningful — not decorative: English speakers often treat accent marks as optional in foreign languages. This lesson makes clear that in Kanien'kéha they change meaning, using familiar words as examples. Nation (2001): orthographic features best taught through known vocabulary. Physical domain: listening and production.

exercises.prompt: In Shé:kon, the acute accent (é) is on the first syllable. What does the acute accent tell you to do with your voice?
exercises.correct: Rise slightly on that syllable — it is a little louder and higher
options: Rise slightly on that syllable — it is a little louder and higher
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The acute accent (á, é, í, ó) marks where your voice rises — slightly louder, slightly higher. It is the stressed syllable. SHÉ-kon: the stress is on SHÉ.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does the acute accent do to pitch?

exercises.prompt: Kaniatarà:ke has a grave accent (à) in the middle. The grave marks a falling pitch on that syllable. How is this different from the acute accent?
exercises.correct: The grave (à) is a falling pitch — the voice drops; the acute (á) is a rising pitch — the voice rises
options: The grave (à) is a falling pitch — the voice drops; the acute (á) is a rising pitch — the voice rises
The grave marks a long vowel
The grave marks a glottal stop
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Acute (á) = rising, stressed. Grave (à) = falling. Kaniatarà:ke: Ka-nia-ta-RÀ:-ke — the voice falls on the rà:. Both marks give you direction for your voice.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which way does each accent direct the pitch?

exercises.prompt: Find the stressed syllable in each word
exercises.sub: The acute accent (') marks it
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Stress moves around in Kanien'kéha — it is not always on the first syllable the way English defaults. The accent marks show you exactly where it lands.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look for the acute accent (').

exercises.prompt: Kanehsatà:ke has a grave accent on the à. Write the accented vowel: Kanehsat___:ke
exercises.correct: à
exercises.hint: à
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanehsatà:ke — the grave accent sits on à, and the colon immediately follows it, marking vowel length. So this syllable has both a falling pitch and a long vowel. Two marks working together on one syllable.
exercises.wm: Please try again — the grave accent goes over the a before the colon.

exercises.prompt: Niawenhkó:wa — count the syllables and find the stressed one.
exercises.correct: Four syllables: Nia-wen-KÓ:-wa — stress on the third syllable KÓ:
options: Four syllables: Nia-wen-KÓ:-wa — stress on the third syllable KÓ:
Three syllables: stress on the first
Five syllables: stress on the last
Two syllables: stress on the second
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Nia-wen-KÓ:-wa — four syllables, stress on KÓ:. That syllable has both the stress (acute accent) and a long vowel (colon). It carries a lot of weight — and Niawenhkó:wa is a word of great weight.
exercises.wm: Please try again — count the syllables first, then find the acute accent.

exercises.prompt: Say this word and feel where the stress lands
exercises.hint: Nia-wen-KÓ:-wa. The KÓ: carries the stress and the length together. Let your voice rise and hold on that syllable. The word is not in a hurry — it is an expression of real gratitude.

exercises.prompt: The name on the land
exercises.meaning: Kanehsatà:ke — where the crust is on the sand
exercises.storyPrompt: Kanehsatà:ke. Say it slowly. Ka-neh-sa-tà:-ke. The falling pitch on tà:, the long vowel, the final ke that tells you this is a place. Every accent mark and every colon in this word is part of how the name has been said for generations. The place name has a sound, and the writing tries to capture that sound faithfully. When you read Kanehsatà:ke and say it correctly — with the falling pitch and the long vowel in the right place — you are saying the name the way it has always been said.
