lessons.title: Lesson 98 — Nasal Vowels
lessons.sub: ę and ǫ — vowels that come through the nose
intro: Kanien'kéha has two nasal vowels: ę and ǫ. When you say these, air passes through both your mouth and your nose at the same time. It is a different sound from any vowel in English.
ę sounds something like 'an' — but the n is not a separate sound. It just tells you air is going through the nose. ǫ is similar but rounder, like 'on' without the separate n.
These two letters are easy to miss when you are reading — they look like o and e with a little hook underneath. But they sound different from o and e, and using them correctly matters.
lessons.ped: Nasal vowels as a distinct phonological category: Nasal vowels are a core feature of Kanien'kéha that have no parallel in English. The lesson introduces them through feel and contrast — air through nose and mouth simultaneously — rather than through IPA description. Nation (2001): new phonemes best taught through familiar word environments. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: The letter ę (with a hook underneath) is a nasal vowel. What makes it different from a regular e?
exercises.correct: Air flows through both the mouth and the nose at the same time — it is a nasal vowel
options: Air flows through both the mouth and the nose at the same time — it is a nasal vowel
It is longer than a regular e
It is the same as e but only in formal speech
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ę is a nasal vowel — the mouth stays open but air also comes through the nose, giving the vowel a nasal quality. It is a sound that does not exist in English as a single vowel, which is why it needs its own letter.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: ǫ is the nasal version of o. If ę sounds roughly like 'an' (with air through the nose), what does ǫ sound roughly like?
exercises.correct: 'on' — but with air through the nose, not a separate n sound
options: 'on' — but with air through the nose, not a separate n sound
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! ǫ is the rounded nasal vowel — somewhere between 'on' and 'oh' but with nasal air flowing through. ę and ǫ are both nasal; ę is the front (mouth more open), ǫ is the back (lips more rounded).
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: You see the letter ę in a Kanien'kéha word. What should you do?
exercises.correct: Say the vowel with air coming through the nose as well as the mouth
options: Say the vowel with air coming through the nose as well as the mouth
Say it like a regular e
Skip it — it is a spelling variant only
Say it like the letter n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ę = nasalise. Every time you see the hooked e or hooked o, air goes through the nose. It takes practice, but the more you do it, the more natural it becomes.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Kanien'kéha has two nasal vowels: ę and ǫ. English has no nasal vowels as single sounds. What does this mean for English speakers learning Kanien'kéha?
exercises.correct: It is a new sound that requires practice — your mouth needs to learn to do something it has not done in English
options: It is a new sound that requires practice — your mouth needs to learn to do something it has not done in English
English speakers cannot learn nasal vowels
Nasal vowels are optional in Kanien'kéha
They sound exactly like English vowels with an n after them
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Learning a new sound takes physical practice — your mouth, throat, and nose learning to coordinate in a new way. This is normal. Every new language asks your body to do new things. The nasal vowels are one of Kanien'kéha's most distinctive sounds.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say the two nasal vowels — feel where the air goes
exercises.hint: For both: keep your mouth open and let air come through your nose at the same time. ę: like 'an' said with a relaxed, open jaw — but no separate n. ǫ: lips slightly rounded, like 'on' — but the n is just nasal airflow, not a separate consonant. Practise in front of a mirror if you can.

exercises.prompt: A sound your body learns
exercises.storyPrompt: ę and ǫ. Two sounds that exist in this language and not in English. Your mouth may resist them at first — it has spent years doing something different. That is normal. Learning a language means teaching your body new sounds. Babies do this without thinking. Adults do it with effort and repetition. Every time you practise ę and ǫ, you are retraining something physical — not just memorising a fact. The language is entering your body.

lessons.title: Lesson 99 — Reading Practice: Short Words
lessons.sub: Seeing all the marks together — and reading what they mean
intro: This lesson puts all four orthographic features together: long vowels (:), glottal stops ('), accent marks (á / à), and nasal vowels (ę / ǫ). Short, familiar words — read carefully, with attention to every mark.
The goal here is not speed. It is accuracy. Reading Kanien'kéha slowly and correctly is better than reading it quickly and wrong. Every mark is there for a reason.
You will see words from Units 1 through 23 — words you know by now. The task is to read them as they are written, not as you expect them to look.
lessons.ped: Reading accuracy before reading speed: Nation (2001) identifies orthographic accuracy — reading the written form correctly — as a prerequisite for using written text as a learning resource. This lesson provides controlled practice with known vocabulary. Physical domain primary: eyes and mouth working together.

exercises.prompt: Read this carefully: í:se' — How many distinct phonological features are present in this one word?
exercises.sub: Count: long vowel, glottal stop, accent, nasal vowel
exercises.correct: Three — a long vowel (í:), an acute accent (í), and a glottal stop (') at the end
options: Three — a long vowel (í:), an acute accent (í), and a glottal stop (') at the end
Two — accent and glottal stop only
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! í:se' — three features in one short word: the acute accent on í, the long vowel marker : after it, and the glottal stop ' at the end. This two-syllable word is doing a lot of orthographic work.
exercises.wm: Please try again — count each mark separately.

exercises.prompt: Match each word to the orthographic features it contains
exercises.sub: Look carefully at each mark

pairs.english: Acute accent + long vowel + glottal stop

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Every word in Kanien'kéha carries its own combination of marks. Reading carefully means attending to all of them.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look at each mark in the word.

exercises.prompt: Write the correct vowel with its accent and length marker for Niawenhk___:wa
exercises.correct: ó
exercises.hint: ó
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Niawenhkó:wa — the ó carries the acute accent (stress) and is followed by the colon (length). One syllable doing two jobs at once.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which accented vowel goes before the colon?

exercises.prompt: Type the word for bear exactly as it is written in Kanien'kéha
exercises.sub: Every mark matters
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri — acute accent on á, colon after it. Two marks on one syllable. The bear's name in Kanien'kéha, written exactly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — include both the accent mark and the colon.

exercises.prompt: Read: Onón:ta. How many syllables does this word have, and where is the stress?
exercises.correct: Three syllables: O-NÓN:-ta — stress on the second syllable NÓN:
options: Three syllables: O-NÓN:-ta — stress on the second syllable NÓN:
Three syllables: stress on the first
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! O-NÓN:-ta — three syllables, stress on NÓN:. The long vowel and the stress both sit on the second syllable. Corn — Onón:ta — named and said correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — count the vowels, then find the accent.

exercises.prompt: Read these five words aloud — one by one, carefully
exercises.hint: Take each word on its own. Find the accent, feel the length of the long vowel, produce any glottal stop. Speed comes later. Accuracy comes first.

exercises.prompt: Reading your own language
exercises.storyPrompt: í:se' — you. Every mark in this word has been placed there carefully by people who wanted the language to be readable, learnable, transmittable. The people who developed this writing system were thinking of you — the learner, the one coming back to the language. When you read í:se' and say it correctly, you are using a gift that was made for you. You are the person this writing was written for.

lessons.title: Lesson 100 — Reading Practice: Full Sentences
lessons.sub: Putting it together — reading sentences aloud from the page
intro: Short words are one thing. Full sentences are where reading really gets tested. This lesson gives you full Kanien'kéha sentences to read aloud — sentences you have already seen in this course.

Your job is to read them as written. Notice every colon, every apostrophe, every accent mark. Say the long vowels long. Close your throat on the glottal stops. Let the stress land where the accent tells you.
This is what it looks like to read Kanien'kéha.
lessons.ped: Sentence-level reading as consolidation: Nation (2001) identifies reading connected text as the highest-value orthographic practice — it integrates phonological awareness with meaning. All sentences in this lesson are drawn from earlier units, so learners can focus on the reading task without new vocabulary demands. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Read this sentence: 'Shé:kon, tó:kens?' — How many long vowels are in this sentence?
exercises.sub: Count every colon (:)
exercises.correct: Two — é: in Shé:kon and ó: in tó:kens
options: Two — é: in Shé:kon and ó: in tó:kens
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shé:kon, tó:kens? — two long vowels: é: and ó:. A simple greeting, and already two length marks to honour.
exercises.wm: Please try again — count every colon that follows a vowel.

exercises.prompt: Read: 'Onen' ki' wáhi' — How many glottal stops are in this phrase?
exercises.sub: Count every apostrophe (')
exercises.correct: Two — one at the end of Onen' and one at the end of ki'
options: Two — one at the end of Onen' and one at the end of ki'
One — only at the end of Onen'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Onen' ki' wáhi — two glottal stops. Both close the words they end. Say Onen' with a slight throat closure, then ki' with another, then wáhi open and clear.
exercises.wm: Please try again — count every apostrophe.

exercises.prompt: Read this description: 'Ohkwá:ri owá:nen tánon Kayé:ri.' — How many long vowels?
exercises.correct: Four — á: in Ohkwá:ri, á: in owá:nen, á: in tánon, é: in Kayé:ri
options: Four — á: in Ohkwá:ri, á: in owá:nen, á: in tánon, é: in Kayé:ri
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Four long vowels in one sentence — the bear is big and dark. Kanien'kéha is rich with vowel length. When you hold every long vowel, the language sounds like itself.
exercises.wm: Please try again — go word by word and count each colon.

exercises.prompt: Complete the name of the Thanksgiving Address — the word before Karihwatéhkwen
exercises.correct: Karihwatéhkwen
exercises.hint: K
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. You have known this name since Unit 5. Now you can read every mark in it correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — the second word of the Thanksgiving Address name.

exercises.prompt: Type this sentence exactly as written: The corn is beautiful and yellow
exercises.sub: Onón:ta ohsì:io tánon Otsì:nekwahr
exercises.correct: Onón:ta ohsì:io tánon Otsì:nekwahr
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onón:ta ohsì:io tánon Otsì:nekwahr — every accent mark and colon in place. The corn is beautiful and yellow, written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — copy the sentence exactly, including every accent mark and colon.

exercises.prompt: Read this full sentence aloud — every mark honoured
exercises.meaning: The Thanksgiving Address is good and great.
exercises.hint: OH-én:-ton (3 syllables) — Ka-ri-hwa-TÉH-kwen (5 syllables) — oh-SÌ:-io (3 syllables) — TÁ-non (2 syllables) — o-WÁ:-nen (3 syllables). Fourteen syllables in one sentence. Say it slowly. Every colon is a held vowel. Every accent is a lift.

exercises.prompt: Reading the words that come before all else
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. This name has lived in the mouths of Kanien'kehá:ka people for generations — spoken at the opening of every gathering, ceremony, and meeting. Now it is written. Now you can read it. The marks on the page — é:, á:, é — try to capture what the voice has always known. They are an attempt to write down something that was made to be spoken. When you read Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen correctly, you are reaching through the writing to the speech it came from.

lessons.title: Lesson 101 — Dictation: Listening and Writing
lessons.sub: Hearing Kanien'kéha and writing what you hear
intro: Dictation is one of the oldest and most effective tools for learning to write a language. You listen, you write what you hear — including every mark.
This lesson gives you the guidance for dictation practice, and presents short word and sentence challenges that ask you to write from memory and from sound.
You do not need to be perfect. You need to try. Every attempt teaches you something about where your ear and your hand agree — and where they do not yet.
lessons.ped: Dictation as integrative practice: Nation (2001) identifies dictation as a high-value integrative activity — it requires phonological processing, orthographic knowledge, and memory simultaneously. The lesson acknowledges that learners may not have audio playback in the app and redirects toward writing from memory of known words. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: When you do dictation in Kanien'kéha, which four things do you need to listen and write for?
exercises.correct: Long vowels (:), glottal stops ('), accent marks (á/à), and nasal vowels (ę/ǫ)
options: Long vowels (:), glottal stops ('), accent marks (á/à), and nasal vowels (ę/ǫ)
Vowels and consonants but not marks
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! All four orthographic features must be captured in dictation. Missing the colon, the apostrophe, the accent, or the nasal hook changes the word.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Write from memory: Hello
exercises.sub: Include every mark
exercises.correct: Shé:kon
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shé:kon — the first word of every lesson. Acute accent on é, colon after it. You have seen this hundreds of times. Now you can write it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — include the accent mark and the colon.

exercises.prompt: Write from memory: Thank you very much
exercises.sub: Include every mark
exercises.correct: Niawenhkó:wa
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Niawenhkó:wa — four syllables, stress and length both on kó:. Well done.
exercises.wm: Please try again — four syllables: Nia-wen-kó:-wa.

exercises.prompt: Write from memory: That is all / I am finished
exercises.sub: Three words — every mark
exercises.correct: Onen' ki' wáhi
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onen' ki' wáhi — two glottal stops (Onen' and ki') and one acute accent (wáhi). You have said this at the end of every unit. Now you can write it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — three words, two apostrophes, one accent mark.

exercises.prompt: Write from memory: bear
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri — acute accent on á, colon after it. The Bear Clan written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — don't forget both the accent and the colon on á:.

exercises.prompt: Write from memory: corn
exercises.correct: Onón:ta
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onón:ta — acute accent on ó, colon after it. One of the Three Sisters, written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — the stress and length are on the second syllable: nón:.

exercises.prompt: Writing yourself
exercises.storyPrompt: í:'i — I. Can you write it without looking? Acute accent on í, colon after it, apostrophe in the middle, then i. Four marks and two letters — the word for yourself, in the language of your people. If you can write í:'i correctly from memory, you can write anything. Start there. Start with yourself.

lessons.title: Lesson 102 — Writing Your Own Sentences
lessons.sub: From reading to writing — making sentences in Kanien'kéha
intro: Reading someone else's Kanien'kéha and writing your own are different skills. Reading follows marks that are already there. Writing means making those marks yourself — placing the colon, the apostrophe, the accent, in exactly the right places.
This lesson asks you to write short Kanien'kéha sentences from what you know. Use the sentence patterns from Units 20 and 23: naming, describing, locating, possessing.
Write slowly. Check every word. Kanien'kéha written correctly is a form of respect for the language.
lessons.ped: Writing as productive orthographic control: Nation (2001) identifies writing production as the highest-demand orthographic task. Learners who can write correctly have fully internalised the orthographic system. This lesson provides structured writing prompts with known vocabulary — the challenge is orthographic, not lexical. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: That is the turtle
exercises.sub: Use the naming pattern from Unit 20
exercises.correct: A'nó:wara ne ní:'i
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! A'nó:wara ne ní:'i — glottal stop at the start, acute accent and long vowel on nó:, glottal stop in ní:'i. A short sentence with four orthographic features.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Pattern: [noun] ne ní:'i. Don't forget the glottal stop at the start of A'nó:wara.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: The bear is big
exercises.sub: Use the describing pattern from Unit 23
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri owá:nen
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen — both words have an á: that needs both accent and length. Noun + describing verb, both written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Both words have á: — acute accent followed by colon.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: The corn is good
exercises.correct: Onón:ta ohsì:io
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onón:ta ohsì:io — both words have long vowels: nón: and sì:. The grave accent on sì: means the pitch falls there.
exercises.wm: Please try again — check the vowel length markers in both words.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: The bear is at the river
exercises.sub: Use the location pattern from Unit 20
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke — three words, multiple marks. The grave accent on Kaniatarà: and the colon after it. A complete location sentence, written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun + tsi + location. Check the grave accent and colon in Kaniatarà:ke.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: The leaf is green
exercises.sub: Ohná:ken is leaf
exercises.correct: Ohná:ken Óhonte'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohná:ken Óhonte' — long vowel in Ohná:ken, acute accent and glottal stop at the end of Óhonte'. Green — plant-coloured — written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — Ohná:ken has ná: (accent + length). Óhonte' ends with a glottal stop.

exercises.prompt: Write then say: The wolf is calm
exercises.sub: Write it first, then read what you wrote aloud
exercises.hint: ok-WÁ-ho (3 syllables) — SKÉN:-nen (2 syllables). Write both words from memory, then check your marks: á in Okwáho, é: in skén:nen. Then say what you wrote.

exercises.prompt: Writing the language into the world
exercises.storyPrompt: Okwáho skén:nen — the wolf is calm. You wrote that in Kanien'kéha. Think about what it means to write in a language that was, for generations, not allowed to be written in schools — not allowed to be spoken in schools. Every sentence you write in Kanien'kéha is a small act of presence. The language is here. You are putting it on the page. The wolf is calm.

lessons.title: Lesson 103 — Dialect Spelling: Kahnawà:ke, Akwesasne, and Six Nations
lessons.sub: Different communities write some things differently — and all are right
intro: You have been learning the spelling conventions of Kanehsatà:ke and Kahnawà:ke throughout this app. But Akwesasne and Six Nations / Tyendinaga communities sometimes spell things differently.
The biggest differences you have already seen: in Akwesasne, words with r are often spelled with l (raónha / laónha). In Ontario dialects, some words with i are spelled with y (í:se' / ý:se').
These are not mistakes. They are different communities writing the sounds they actually say. When you encounter a different spelling, your job is to recognise it — not correct it.
lessons.ped: Dialect variation as orthographic asset: Flores & Rosa (2015): the ideology that one spelling is correct and others are errors is a form of language standardisation that has historically disadvantaged minority communities. This lesson names dialect spelling variation plainly and teaches recognition without judgment. Nation (2001): encountering variation in known words deepens orthographic awareness.

exercises.prompt: In Akwesasne Kanien'kéha, raónha (he) is often spelled laónha. Why?
exercises.correct: Because in Akwesasne speech, the r sound is pronounced as l — the spelling reflects how the community actually says it
options: Because in Akwesasne speech, the r sound is pronounced as l — the spelling reflects how the community actually says it
Because Akwesasne has a different alphabet
The two words mean different things
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Akwesasne speakers say l where Kanehsatà:ke speakers say r. The spelling follows the speech. laónha is fully correct — it is the right spelling for the Akwesasne pronunciation.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the Kanehsatà:ke spelling to the Akwesasne spelling
exercises.sub: r becomes l in Akwesasne
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The r → l alternation is consistent across all words with an r-initial prefix in Akwesasne. Once you know the pattern, you can read Akwesasne spelling even if you learned the Kanehsatà:ke form.
exercises.wm: Please try again — r in Kanehsatà:ke becomes l in Akwesasne.

exercises.prompt: In Six Nations and Tyendinaga (Ontario) dialects, í:se' (you) is sometimes written ý:se'. What sound does y represent in this context?
exercises.correct: A palatal glide — the y-sound at the start, where Kanehsatà:ke uses an i-sound
options: A palatal glide — the y-sound at the start, where Kanehsatà:ke uses an i-sound
The same sound as i — just a different letter
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! In Ontario dialects, some positions where Kanehsatà:ke uses i (as a vowel-like sound) use y instead (as a glide). ý:se' and í:se' are the same word, said slightly differently in two communities, spelled to reflect those differences.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: You are reading a text in Six Nations Kanien'kéha and see ý:'i instead of í:'i. What do you do?
exercises.correct: Recognise it as the Ontario spelling of the same word — 'I / me'
options: Recognise it as the Ontario spelling of the same word — 'I / me'
Conclude the text has an error
Look it up as if it is a different word
Skip it — it cannot be the same word
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Dialect reading requires this kind of recognition: same word, different community spelling. ý:'i and í:'i are both correct. Both mean I / me. A fluent reader of Kanien'kéha can move between community spellings.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Why does this app accept multiple spellings as correct answers in typing exercises?
exercises.correct: Because all community forms of the language are valid — no learner should be marked wrong for using their own community's spelling
options: Because all community forms of the language are valid — no learner should be marked wrong for using their own community's spelling
Because spelling does not matter in Kanien'kéha
Because there is no agreed spelling for anything
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The app accepts dialect variants precisely because no community's spelling is more correct than another's. If you learned Akwesasne Kanien'kéha, you should not be penalised for writing laónha. If you learned Six Nations, ý:se' should be accepted. The language belongs to all its communities.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: One language, many spellings
exercises.storyPrompt: í:se' — ý:se'. The same word, two communities, two ways of writing it. Both correct. The Kanien'kehá:ka people span what is now the Canada-US border — Akwesasne, Kanehsatà:ke, Kahnawà:ke, Six Nations, Tyendinaga, Wahta. Each community has its own history, its own dialect, its own way of saying some things. The language holds all of them. When you see a spelling you do not recognise, lean toward it with curiosity rather than correction. It is probably someone else's home.

lessons.title: Lesson 104 — Writing Has Power
lessons.sub: What it means to write Kanien'kéha today
intro: This is the final lesson of Unit 30. It does not introduce new orthographic rules. It asks you to sit with what writing this language means.

Kanien'kéha has been spoken for thousands of years. It was suppressed, forbidden in schools, almost silenced. It is now being written on signs, in apps, in textbooks, on social media, in government documents.
Every correctly written Kanien'kéha sentence is part of that return.
lessons.ped: Writing as revitalization: The TRC Calls to Action, the Indigenous Languages Act (2019), and UNDRIP Article 13 all affirm the right to transmit Indigenous languages. This lesson connects writing as a skill to writing as a political and cultural act — without jargon, in plain language. LaFever Spiritual domain primary. All four domains present.

exercises.prompt: Onkwehón:we means 'Indigenous person / original person'. The word is built from roots meaning the original people of this land. What does this tell you about the relationship between the language and the land?
exercises.correct: The language names people by their relationship to the land — the original people are named as such in the language itself
options: The language names people by their relationship to the land — the original people are named as such in the language itself
It is a historical term no longer in use
It refers only to people from Kanehsatà:ke
It is a borrowed word from French
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onkwehón:we — the original people. The name is built into the language. The language remembers who was here first and names them accordingly. When you write Onkwehón:we correctly, you are writing that memory.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Rotinonhsyón:ni means 'People of the Longhouse' — the name the Haudenosaunee use for themselves. Why does it matter to write this name in Kanien'kéha rather than only in English translation?
exercises.correct: Because the Kanien'kéha name carries meaning and identity that the English translation cannot fully hold
options: Because the Kanien'kéha name carries meaning and identity that the English translation cannot fully hold
It does not matter — both names mean the same thing
The Kanien'kéha name is only used in ceremonies
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Rotinonhsyón:ni — People of the Longhouse. This is how the Haudenosaunee name themselves, in their own language. Writing it in Kanien'kéha rather than only in English is a form of using the name that belongs to them. English can translate it. But it cannot be it.
exercises.wm: Please try again.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: I am learning Kanien'kéha
exercises.sub: This sentence is in your glossary
exercises.correct: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — I am learning Kanien'kéha. Written by you, about you, in the language you are learning. That is something.
exercises.wm: Please try again — check the accent mark on yén and the glottal stop in Kanyen'.

exercises.prompt: Say this aloud — then write it from memory
exercises.hint: Ro-ti-non-hsyÓN:-ni — five syllables. Stress and length on yÓN:. This is a name. Names deserve to be said correctly.

exercises.prompt: Onen' ki' wáhi
exercises.meaning: That is all / I am finished
exercises.storyPrompt: Onen' ki' wáhi. You have now worked through Unit 30. You can read Kanien'kéha. You can write it. You know the marks — the colon, the apostrophe, the accent, the nasal vowel — and you know what each one means. You can read a sentence in this language and understand it. You can write a sentence and have it be correct. This is not small. The Canadian government passed the Indigenous Languages Act in 2019, affirming the right to reclaim, revitalize, and transmit Indigenous languages. You are doing that right now. In this lesson. In this language. Onen' ki' wáhi.
